prompts.reasoning: Unilateral (right) limb ataxia with ipsilateral nystagmus localises to the right cerebellar hemisphere — the right cerebellar hemisphere controls right limb coordination. Preserved truncal stability with lateralised limb signs suggests a hemispheric rather than vermis lesion. Medulloblastoma more typically presents with vermian ataxia (truncal, midline). Pilocytic astrocytoma favours the cerebellar hemisphere. However, all three tumours can show overlapping features — imaging is required.

prompts.label: Routine Bloods
prompts.text: FBC, CRP, metabolic panel, and LFTs all within normal range. No anaemia, no electrolyte disturbance. Urinalysis normal. Blood cultures sent: no growth at 48 hours.
prompts.reasoning: Normal inflammatory markers and cultures make infectious causes (abscess, cerebellitis) effectively excluded. Ependymoma is less consistent with pure hemispheric ataxia without lower cranial nerve signs or floor-of-4th-ventricle involvement. MRI is now essential for definitive characterisation. The differential now rests between pilocytic astrocytoma and medulloblastoma.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: well-circumscribed cystic lesion (4cm) in the right cerebellar hemisphere with a discrete avidly enhancing mural nodule at the lateral wall. No diffusion restriction in the solid component. No leptomeningeal enhancement or CSF seeding. Histopathology (post-resection): biphasic pattern — compact fibrillary astrocytes with bipolar hair-like processes (Rosenthal fibres) and loose microcystic areas. BRAF V600E mutation detected.
prompts.reasoning: The cyst-with-mural-nodule pattern is the classic MRI appearance of cerebellar pilocytic astrocytoma. Medulloblastoma typically presents as a solid, homogeneous, midline vermian mass with restricted diffusion (high cellularity). Lack of diffusion restriction and the cystic-nodular pattern favour pilocytic astrocytoma. Histopathology confirms WHO Grade I pilocytic astrocytoma: Rosenthal fibres and biphasic pattern are pathognomonic. BRAF fusion or V600E mutation is characteristic.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old man is brought to the ED following a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 2 minutes from which he recovered fully within 30 minutes.
prompts.reasoning: A first unprovoked seizure in a young adult requires evaluation for both primary epilepsy and structural causes. Metabolic causes (hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, drugs) must be excluded. Structural aetiologies include low-grade glioma, cavernous malformation, and focal cortical dysplasia. Idiopathic generalised epilepsy is plausible in this age group.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: This is his first such event and he has no significant past medical history. On closer questioning, he admits to two prior episodes over the past year of involuntary right arm jerking lasting 1–2 minutes, which he attributed to stress and overwork; the seizures begin in the right arm before spreading. He denies headaches, cognitive changes, or personality change.
prompts.reasoning: A focal onset (right arm) with secondary generalisation changes the diagnosis significantly. Focal motor seizures with right arm onset and secondary spread localise to the left motor cortex or its vicinity. This is not consistent with idiopathic generalised epilepsy, which is now excluded. Structural causes are strongly favoured — low-grade glioma, focal cortical dysplasia, and cavernous malformation are all plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological history. No family history of brain tumours, NF1, or hereditary epilepsy syndromes. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol. No illicit drug use. No prior cranial irradiation. Works long hours in a stressful desk job. No recent head trauma.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of NF1 does not exclude glioma. No head trauma makes traumatic cortical dysplasia unlikely. Cavernous malformation typically has a family history or prior bleed; absence reduces but does not eliminate it. All three structural epilepsy causes remain plausible. History cannot distinguish between low-grade astrocytoma, oligodendroglioma, and focal cortical dysplasia.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Full neurological examination is normal — no focal motor or sensory deficits. No papilloedema. MMSE 30/30. BP and cardiovascular examination unremarkable. No skin stigmata of NF1 (no café-au-lait spots or neurofibromas).
prompts.reasoning: A completely normal interictal neurological examination is consistent with all three remaining diagnoses — low-grade gliomas and focal cortical dysplasia are typically non-progressive until advanced. Normal examination does not narrow the differential further; MRI is essential. Absence of NF1 stigmata makes NF1-associated glioma very unlikely.

prompts.label: Bloods and EEG
prompts.text: FBC, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, LFTs, and TFTs all normal — metabolic causes excluded. Urine drug screen negative. Interictal EEG: focal left frontal slow wave activity with occasional sharp wave discharges. No generalised epileptiform abnormality.
prompts.reasoning: Normal metabolic panel conclusively excludes metabolic seizure provocation. Left frontal focal EEG abnormality localises the epileptic focus and confirms a structural left frontal lesion. Focal cortical dysplasia would typically show more prominent repetitive spike-wave discharges directly over the dysplastic cortex; low-grade glioma and oligodendroglioma show focal slow-wave activity. MRI is required for differentiation.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Biopsy
prompts.text: MRI brain: ill-defined T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesion (3cm) in the left frontal lobe with grey-white matter infiltration, no gadolinium enhancement, no mass effect, no necrosis, and no calcification on gradient echo. Stereotactic biopsy: mildly hypercellular astrocytic proliferation with nuclear atypia, no mitoses, no necrosis, no microvascular proliferation. IDH1 R132H mutation positive. ATRX loss. 1p/19q non-codeleted.
prompts.reasoning: Non-enhancing infiltrative T2/FLAIR signal without calcification favours a diffuse astrocytoma over oligodendroglioma (which typically shows calcification and gyral expansion on CT/MRI). Histopathology confirms WHO Grade 2 Diffuse Astrocytoma: IDH mutant, ATRX loss, and 1p/19q non-codeletion (the latter two distinguish astrocytoma from oligodendroglioma, which requires IDH mutation PLUS 1p/19q codeletion).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 40-year-old woman is referred to the neurology outpatient clinic with a 3-year history of recurrent focal seizures affecting her right hand and arm, previously attributed to stress by her GP.
prompts.reasoning: Drug-resistant focal epilepsy with increasing frequency in a middle-aged adult requires structural brain evaluation. A 3-year history before referral is not uncommon in slowly growing tumours. The differential includes benign structural causes (cavernous malformation, focal cortical dysplasia) and slowly growing primary tumours (oligodendroglioma, low-grade astrocytoma, ganglioglioma).

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Seizure frequency has increased from monthly to weekly over the past 6 months. The seizures are stereotyped — beginning with right hand tingling that spreads to clonic jerking of the right arm over 1–2 minutes; over the past year her husband has noticed subtle personality change and mild word-finding difficulties between seizures.
prompts.reasoning: The somatotopic spread (tingling then clonic jerking) localises to the left sensorimotor cortex. Cognitive change between seizures suggests cortical infiltration — more consistent with a slowly growing infiltrating tumour than focal cortical dysplasia or cavernous malformation, which do not typically infiltrate progressively. Low-grade glioma subtypes are now the leading diagnosis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological history. No family history of brain tumours or NF1. Works as a nurse but has reduced hours due to seizures. Non-smoker. No prior cranial irradiation. Currently takes levetiracetam and lamotrigine with partial seizure control — seizure frequency reduced but not eliminated.
prompts.reasoning: Drug-resistant epilepsy unresponsive to two appropriate antiepileptics is a strong indicator for a structural lesion rather than idiopathic epilepsy. No known risk factors distinguish between oligodendroglioma and low-grade astrocytoma — both are IDH-mutant tumours occurring in young-to-middle-aged adults. Focal cortical dysplasia remains possible as it is also drug-resistant. MRI is essential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Neurological examination between seizures: mild right hand fine motor slowing on rapid alternating movements. MMSE 28/30 — attention mildly reduced, language intact. No papilloedema. No gross focal weakness. Interictal EEG: focal spike-and-slow-wave discharges over the left centroparietal region.
prompts.reasoning: Subtle fine motor deficit and EEG localisation to the left centroparietal cortex confirm a left-sided structural lesion. Progressive cognitive slowing with cortical infiltration over years is more consistent with a diffuse infiltrating glioma than a discrete focal cortical dysplasia. Between oligodendroglioma and low-grade astrocytoma, clinical features alone cannot distinguish — imaging and molecular markers are required.

prompts.label: MRI and CT Head
prompts.text: CT head: gyral expansion in the left frontal-parietal region with peripheral calcification visible as focal hyperdensity, without associated oedema. MRI brain: T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesion with cortical involvement and subcortical extension, without gadolinium enhancement, without mass effect. Gradient echo (GRE) sequence confirms calcification.
prompts.reasoning: Calcification within a cortical non-enhancing glioma strongly favours oligodendroglioma — calcification occurs in 70-90% of oligodendrogliomas but in only ~10% of diffuse astrocytomas. Gyral expansion (tumour expanding the cortex rather than destroying it) is also characteristic of oligodendroglioma's cortical growth pattern. However, molecular confirmation is required for a definitive WHO classification.

prompts.label: Biopsy and Molecular Pathology
prompts.text: Awake craniotomy with cortical mapping performed. Histopathology: uniform round cells with perinuclear clearing ('fried-egg' artefact), chicken-wire vascular pattern, and calcification. IDH1 R132H mutation positive. 1p/19q codeletion confirmed on FISH. MGMT promoter methylated.
prompts.reasoning: The histological fried-egg appearance and chicken-wire vasculature, combined with the molecular signature of IDH mutation PLUS 1p/19q codeletion, meet the WHO 2021 criteria for Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant, 1p/19q codeleted — the defining molecular markers that distinguish oligodendroglioma from all other gliomas. 1p/19q codeletion is the defining molecular event, not just a prognostic marker. MGMT methylation predicts chemosensitivity to temozolomide.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old woman is referred to neurology by her GP with a 4-month history of progressive word-finding difficulties.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive focal deficits (word-finding difficulty, left hand focal seizures) localising to the right hemisphere require urgent evaluation for a space-occupying lesion. The differential spans primary brain tumours, secondary tumours (metastases, dural infiltration), abscess, and structural lesions. The subacute 4-month course suggests a slower-growing process.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has also had intermittent right-sided headaches that are mild, positional (worse on bending forward), and non-pulsatile. Over the past month she has had two episodes of left hand twitching lasting 30 seconds in which she remained fully conscious.
prompts.reasoning: Preserved consciousness during the hand twitching episodes confirms focal (not generalised) seizures. Progressive language difficulty localising to the left (dominant) hemisphere, with right-sided focal motor seizures, indicates a left hemisphere lesion. No fever makes abscess unlikely. The slow progression is more consistent with a benign or slowly growing tumour than GBM.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No known malignancy. No family history of cancer or brain tumours. No prior cranial irradiation. Has taken the combined oral contraceptive pill for 18 years. Non-smoker. No NF2 family history. No recent head trauma. Perimenopausal — still having periods.
prompts.reasoning: No known primary malignancy reduces but does not exclude metastases (occult primary). Prolonged hormonal exposure (OCP) is an established risk factor for meningioma — oestrogen and progesterone receptors are expressed on most meningiomas. No known NF2 mutation, but sporadic meningioma does not require NF2. The slow course and hormonal risk factor raise meningioma probability, but low-grade glioma remains plausible.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Mild anomic aphasia — difficulty naming low-frequency objects. No papilloedema. Left hand grip mildly reduced (4/5) with subtle pronator drift. MMSE 27/30. Cardiovascular and systemic examination unremarkable. Temperature 36.8°C.
prompts.reasoning: Anomic aphasia with mild left UMN weakness localises to the right hemisphere in this left-handed? — no, left hemisphere lesion causing right hand deficit and language impairment localises to the dominant (left) hemisphere. No papilloedema suggests moderate mass effect without severe raised ICP. The examination narrows the anatomical localisation but does not yet discriminate between the three remaining diagnoses.

prompts.label: Bloods and Staging
prompts.text: FBC, CRP, CA-125, CEA, and full metabolic panel all normal. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no primary malignancy, no lymphadenopathy. CT head: well-defined, hyperdense, extra-axial convexity mass overlying the left frontoparietal region with adjacent bony hyperostosis.
prompts.reasoning: No primary malignancy on CT staging effectively eliminates brain metastases. CT showing an extra-axial (outside the brain parenchyma) hyperdense mass with adjacent bony hyperostosis is characteristic of meningioma. Low-grade glioma is an intra-axial lesion — extra-axial location strongly favours meningioma. MRI with contrast is required to confirm.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: 3.5cm uniformly enhancing extra-axial dural-based mass at the left frontoparietal convexity with a dural tail sign, CSF cleft between tumour and cortex, and adjacent cortical compression. No intra-axial invasion. Elective resection: histopathology shows whorls of meningothelial cells with psammoma bodies and intranuclear inclusions. WHO Grade I (NF2-mutant, no high-risk features).
prompts.reasoning: The dural tail sign (dural enhancement extending from the mass margin), extra-axial location with CSF cleft, and uniform enhancement confirm meningioma on MRI. Histopathology with meningothelial whorls and psammoma bodies is classic for WHO Grade I (benign) meningioma. Extra-axial position with dural attachment definitively excludes all intra-axial tumours (glioma, metastases).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the ED with 5 weeks of progressive early-morning vomiting and headaches that are worsening in frequency and severity.
prompts.reasoning: Morning vomiting with new-onset gait instability and headaches in a child is a classic presentation of raised ICP from a posterior fossa tumour. The four main paediatric posterior fossa tumours must be considered: ependymoma, medulloblastoma, pilocytic astrocytoma, and brainstem glioma. Gastrointestinal causes of vomiting must be excluded initially.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Her parents also report she has become increasingly irritable and unsteady, having fallen twice in the past 2 weeks. The vomiting is worst in the morning before rising and also occurs with position changes; the headaches are holocephalic.
prompts.reasoning: Lower cranial nerve symptoms (hoarse voice, difficulty swallowing — CN IX/X/XII involvement) are a critically important localising feature pointing to the floor of the 4th ventricle or brainstem. Ependymoma classically arises from the floor of the 4th ventricle and extends through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie, compressing lower cranial nerve nuclei. Gastrointestinal causes are now excluded.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Normal birth and developmental history. Fully vaccinated. No family history of NF2, Li-Fraumeni, or other tumour predisposition syndromes. No prior cranial irradiation. No recent infection. Previously healthy and active in school sports.
prompts.reasoning: No NF2 family history (NF2 increases risk of spinal ependymoma in adults). No prior radiotherapy. All three tumour types can occur without known predisposition. Brainstem glioma is less characteristic without cranial nerve deficits from intrinsic brainstem origin at onset — the lower cranial nerve involvement in this case suggests extrinsic compression from the 4th ventricle floor rather than intrinsic brainstem tumour.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade II-III). Dysarthria and reduced gag reflex bilaterally. Bilateral CN VI palsy (false localising sign from raised ICP). Truncal ataxia — unsteady sitting without support. Limb coordination intact. No lateralising signs. Neck stiffness absent.
prompts.reasoning: Lower cranial nerve deficits (reduced gag, dysarthria) with truncal ataxia (vermis/4th ventricle midline lesion) and bilateral papilloedema point to a midline 4th ventricular mass with floor involvement and raised ICP. Pilocytic astrocytoma more typically causes hemispheric ataxia without lower cranial nerve signs. Medulloblastoma is also midline/vermian. Ependymoma's classic feature is floor-of-4th-ventricle involvement causing lower cranial nerve dysfunction. Confirmation requires imaging.

prompts.label: Bloods and CSF Staging
prompts.text: FBC, CRP, metabolic panel normal. MRI spine: no drop metastases in the spinal canal. CSF obtained post-imaging: no malignant cells on cytology, lymphocyte count mildly elevated (12 cells/µL), protein mildly elevated. No organisms on culture.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of CSF seeding does not distinguish ependymoma from medulloblastoma at this stage — both can or cannot have spinal dissemination. Normal inflammatory markers and CSF make infectious meningitis unlikely. Spinal MRI staging is necessary pre-operatively in both tumours. The differential now hinges on MRI characterisation of the 4th ventricular mass.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI brain: heterogeneous 4th ventricular mass arising from the floor, extending through the foramen of Magendie into the cisterna magna ('plastic tumour' squeezing through foramina). Moderate enhancement, calcification on GRE, no diffusion restriction. Histopathology post-resection: perivascular pseudorosettes (tumour cells arranged around vessels), true ependymal rosettes, and positive GFAP staining. RELA fusion not detected; posterior fossa group B (PFB) methylation profile.
prompts.reasoning: The 'plastic' or 'toothpaste' tumour extending through the foramina of Magendie/Luschka into the cisterna magna is the classic MRI hallmark of 4th ventricular ependymoma. Medulloblastoma typically fills the 4th ventricle from above (vermis) rather than growing from the floor. Histopathological perivascular pseudorosettes with GFAP positivity confirm ependymoma. PFB methylation profile is characteristic of childhood posterior fossa ependymoma.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric ED with a 4-week history of daily morning vomiting and worsening headaches.
prompts.reasoning: Morning vomiting with progressive cerebellar ataxia in a young child is the archetypal presentation of a posterior fossa tumour causing obstructive hydrocephalus. All four major paediatric posterior fossa tumours (medulloblastoma, pilocytic astrocytoma, ependymoma, brainstem glioma) must be considered alongside other causes of hydrocephalus. Gastrointestinal causes of vomiting should be initially excluded.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has also developed an increasingly unsteady gait and has had three falls in the past 10 days. The vomiting occurs on waking and improves once he is upright; the headache is worst in the morning and on coughing.
prompts.reasoning: Midline (truncal/gait) instability rather than limb-specific incoordination localises to the cerebellar vermis. Medulloblastoma and ependymoma classically arise in the midline vermis or 4th ventricle. Absence of lower cranial nerve symptoms at this stage is less typical of 4th-floor ependymoma (which often presents with dysphagia or dysarthria), though not exclusive. Pilocytic astrocytoma typically causes hemispheric ataxia. Brainstem glioma excluded by absence of cranial nerve signs early.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Normal birth history and developmental milestones. No family history of NF1, NF2, tuberous sclerosis, or Gorlin syndrome (basal cell naevus syndrome — associated with desmoplastic medulloblastoma). Father has colonic polyposis but no brain tumour history. Fully vaccinated. No prior cranial irradiation.
prompts.reasoning: Father's colonic polyposis suggests possible Turcot syndrome (APC mutation — associated with medulloblastoma variant) but is not confirmatory. Gorlin syndrome is specifically associated with desmoplastic/nodular medulloblastoma in infants. All three tumours can occur without defined genetic predisposition. Family history raises medulloblastoma probability but does not eliminate others.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade III). Gait: broad-based, truncal instability, falls backward (posterior) without limb ataxia. Negative finger-nose test bilaterally. Negative heel-shin test. Bilateral CN VI palsy (false localising signs). No lower cranial nerve palsies. No meningism. Head circumference normal.
prompts.reasoning: Marked truncal ataxia with preserved limb coordination (negative finger-nose, negative heel-shin) localises to the midline vermis — the vermis controls axial/truncal coordination, whereas the hemispheres control ipsilateral limb coordination. This pattern is more consistent with a vermian midline tumour. Bilateral CN VI palsies are false localising signs of severe raised ICP. Pilocytic astrocytoma now less likely (predominantly hemispheric). Ependymoma remains — but absence of lower CN palsies is less typical.

prompts.label: Bloods and MRI Spine
prompts.text: FBC, metabolic panel, CRP: normal. MRI full spine: no leptomeningeal enhancement or drop metastases. CSF cytology (obtained post-imaging): no malignant cells. Opening pressure elevated at 26 cmH₂O.
prompts.reasoning: Elevated opening pressure confirms hydrocephalus/raised ICP. Absence of spinal seeding at presentation does not exclude medulloblastoma — M0 stage (no metastases) is the most common presentation. CSF staging is essential pre-operatively in both medulloblastoma and ependymoma. MRI brain characterisation is now the critical next step.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI brain: hyperdense on CT, avidly contrast-enhancing solid mass in the cerebellar vermis filling the 4th ventricle from above, with restricted diffusion on DWI (low ADC values indicating high cellularity). No extension through the foramina of Magendie. Obstructive hydrocephalus at the level of the 4th ventricle. Histopathology: sheets of highly cellular small round blue cells (SRBC) with scant cytoplasm, Homer-Wright rosettes, and high Ki-67 proliferation index (>30%). Molecular: WNT-activated subgroup.
prompts.reasoning: Restricted diffusion (high cellularity) in a midline vermian mass filling the 4th ventricle from above — without floor involvement or foraminal extrusion — is the classic imaging profile of medulloblastoma. Ependymoma grows from the floor and characteristically extrudes through the foramina; it does not typically restrict diffusion. Homer-Wright rosettes are characteristic of medulloblastoma (not ependymoma, which has perivascular pseudorosettes). WNT-activated is the most favourable molecular subgroup.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old woman presents to the ophthalmology clinic with a 5-month history of gradually progressive difficulty seeing objects to the sides.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive visual field loss with frontal headache in a young adult suggests compression of the optic chiasm in the suprasellar region. The differential includes all suprasellar and parasellar masses: pituitary adenoma, craniopharyngioma, suprasellar meningioma, optic chiasm glioma, ICA aneurysm, and inflammatory causes such as sarcoidosis. Bitemporal visual field loss (if confirmed) would reflect chiasmal compression.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has been bumping into door frames and misjudging the edges of objects, and reports intermittent frontal headaches. She mentions she has not had a menstrual period for 9 months, initially attributed to stress, and has noticed unexpected milk discharge from both breasts despite not being pregnant, with markedly reduced libido over the past 6 months.
prompts.reasoning: Amenorrhoea, galactorrhoea, and reduced libido indicate hyperprolactinaemia — elevated prolactin suppresses GnRH → LH/FSH → hypogonadism. Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma) is the commonest cause in a young woman. However, any large suprasellar mass can cause 'stalk effect' (compressing the dopaminergic stalk, releasing prolactin from tonic inhibition). Drug causes (antipsychotics, antiemetics) must also be considered.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No psychiatric conditions or depression. Not taking antipsychotics, antidepressants, antiemetics, domperidone, or other dopamine-antagonist drugs. No hypothyroidism. No history of head trauma or cranial irradiation. No family history of MEN1 syndrome or pituitary disease. Not pregnant (urine hCG negative).
prompts.reasoning: Drug-induced hyperprolactinaemia is effectively excluded — no relevant medications. Hypothyroidism excluded (TRH stimulates both TSH and prolactin — hypothyroidism can rarely cause hyperprolactinaemia). No MEN1 history. Pregnancy excluded. The combination of chiasmal compression with prolactin-mediated hypogonadism now strongly points to a sellar/suprasellar mass. All three remaining diagnoses can cause stalk compression.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Visual field testing (confrontation and Humphrey perimetry): bitemporal hemianopia — denser superiorly. Galactorrhoea confirmed bilaterally on expression. No acromegalic features. No cushingoid features. No ophthalmoplegia. Skin and systemic examination unremarkable. Blood pressure normal.
prompts.reasoning: Bitemporal hemianopia confirms optic chiasm compression — the decussating nasal fibres (representing temporal visual fields) are compressed from below by a suprasellar mass. All three remaining diagnoses can produce this finding. No cushingoid or acromegalic features suggests the adenoma is not ACTH- or GH-secreting. The superior density of the hemianopia (denser superiorly) is consistent with compression from below (as from a pituitary adenoma extending superiorly).

prompts.label: Endocrine Bloods
prompts.text: Serum prolactin: 4,200 mU/L (markedly elevated; normal <600 mU/L in females). FSH and LH: undetectable. Oestradiol: low. TFTs: normal. Cortisol: 320 nmol/L (normal). IGF-1: normal. Urine and serum calcium: normal (excluding sarcoidosis/hypercalcaemia). Full pituitary axis screen: isolated hyperprolactinaemia.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated prolactin (>3,000 mU/L) is disproportionate to what stalk compression alone typically produces (usually <2,000 mU/L). This level of elevation is more consistent with active prolactin secretion by a prolactinoma. However, a large non-functioning adenoma with stalk compression cannot be excluded without imaging, and craniopharyngioma can also cause significant prolactin elevation via stalk involvement. Meningioma less likely to cause such marked prolactin elevation.

prompts.label: MRI Pituitary and Diagnosis
prompts.text: MRI pituitary (dedicated sella protocol with gadolinium, thin coronal cuts): 2.8cm macroadenoma arising from the sella with suprasellar extension, compressing the optic chiasm. Tumour is isointense to normal pituitary on T1, enhances less avidly than normal pituitary, no calcification, no cyst. Normal pituitary tissue compressed inferiorly. No cavernous sinus invasion. Diagnosis confirmed: macroprolactinoma. Cabergoline initiated — prolactin normalised at 3 months with 60% tumour shrinkage.
prompts.reasoning: MRI sella confirms a homogeneous sellar macroadenoma with suprasellar extension and chiasmal compression — no calcification (excludes craniopharyngioma, which typically calcifies) and no extra-axial dural-based appearance (excludes meningioma). Response to dopamine agonist (cabergoline) confirms functional prolactin secretion and is diagnostic of prolactinoma — craniopharyngioma and meningioma do not respond to dopamine agonists.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 10-year-old boy is referred by his paediatrician for a 6-month history of progressively worsening headaches, declining school performance, and his parents' concern that he has not grown as expected over the past 2 years.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive headaches with growth failure and cognitive decline in a child requires evaluation for a suprasellar mass affecting the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. Craniopharyngioma, optic pathway glioma, and hypothalamic glioma are the leading structural causes. Isolated growth hormone deficiency and constitutional delay are endocrine diagnoses of exclusion. Pituitary adenoma is rare in children but possible.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has difficulty seeing the sides of the blackboard at school and has moved to the front row. He is significantly thirstier than his peers, drinks 4–5 litres of water daily, and passes large volumes of dilute urine frequently, including nocturia. He fatigues easily and his teachers note his memory and concentration have declined.
prompts.reasoning: Polydipsia and polyuria with nocturia indicate diabetes insipidus (DI) — deficiency of ADH (vasopressin) from hypothalamic or posterior pituitary involvement. DI combined with visual field restriction (peripheral vision loss) and growth failure in a child is a classic triad of craniopharyngioma. Optic pathway glioma can cause visual loss but rarely DI or growth failure early. All four remain plausible — DI raises craniopharyngioma probability substantially.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Normal birth and early development until age 8 — crawled, walked, and spoke at appropriate milestones. Growth velocity declined from age 8. Previously seen by GP for short stature — no investigation undertaken. No family history of growth disorders, pituitary disease, or MEN1. Not on any medications. No prior cranial irradiation.
prompts.reasoning: Normal development until age 8 with subsequent growth deceleration suggests an acquired (not congenital) process developing from mid-childhood — consistent with craniopharyngioma's typical childhood presentation (peak age 5-14 years). No MEN1 family history makes pituitary adenoma less likely. Optic pathway and hypothalamic gliomas can occur without identified genetic predisposition. Isolated constitutional delay does not cause DI or visual loss.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Height -2.5 SD (severely short for age and parental midparental height). Weight at 50th percentile (height-weight dissociation). Bitemporal hemianopia with pale optic discs bilaterally (optic atrophy from chronic compression). Delayed puberty (no pubertal signs at age 10). No acromegalic features. Tanner stage I. Fundoscopy: bilateral optic pallor.
prompts.reasoning: Optic atrophy (pale discs) indicates chronic optic nerve/chiasm compression — distinguishing from papilloedema (which would indicate acute raised ICP). Bitemporal hemianopia with optic atrophy confirms chiasmal compression of long duration. Height-weight dissociation (short stature with normal or increased weight) is characteristic of hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction with GH deficiency plus hypothalamic obesity. Hypothalamic glioma now less likely (typically intrinsic to hypothalamus without prominent chiasmal compression pattern).

prompts.label: Endocrine and Fluid Investigations
prompts.text: Morning serum cortisol: low (185 nmol/L). GH stimulation test: GH <3 ng/mL (severe GH deficiency). LH, FSH: prepubertal (appropriate for age but unresponsive to GnRH stimulation). TSH: low-normal, free T4: low (secondary hypothyroidism). Urine osmolality: 85 mOsm/kg (dilute); serum osmolality: 302 mOsm/kg (mildly elevated) — consistent with cranial DI. Serum prolactin: mildly elevated (stalk effect).
prompts.reasoning: Panhypopituitarism (GH deficiency, secondary hypothyroidism, secondary hypocortisolism) combined with cranial diabetes insipidus (low urine osmolality despite high serum osmolality) confirms hypothalamic-pituitary axis disruption at multiple levels. DI specifically implicates the posterior pituitary/hypothalamic stalk. This combination of anterior AND posterior pituitary failure is most characteristic of craniopharyngioma — optic pathway glioma rarely causes such complete pituitary axis failure.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and CT Head
prompts.text: CT skull: suprasellar calcification in an eggshell pattern. MRI with gadolinium: lobulated suprasellar-sellar mass with mixed solid and cystic components, heterogeneous enhancement of the solid component, and T1 hyperintense cyst (containing cholesterol-rich fluid). Mass compresses the optic chiasm and hypothalamus. Intraoperative: dark 'machine-oil' viscous cyst fluid. Histopathology: adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma — wet keratin nodules, cholesterol clefts, and calcification.
prompts.reasoning: Suprasellar calcification on CT combined with the MRI appearance of a mixed cystic-solid suprasellar mass with T1-hyperintense cyst content (cholesterol-rich fluid — which appears bright on T1 MRI) are pathognomonic of adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma. Optic pathway glioma is solid, non-calcified, and rarely cystic. The 'machine-oil' cyst fluid at surgery and wet keratin nodules on histopathology confirm adamantinomatous type — the childhood variant of craniopharyngioma (vs papillary type in adults).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old male, known to be HIV-positive (CD4 count 180 cells/µL, not currently on antiretroviral therapy), presents with a 3-week history of progressive confusion, right arm weakness, and personality change noted by his partner.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute focal neurological deficits with cognitive change in a severely immunosuppressed HIV patient (CD4 <200) demands urgent CNS evaluation. The differential includes all major AIDS-defining neurological diseases: toxoplasma encephalitis, primary CNS lymphoma (PCNSL), progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), cryptococcal meningitis, and HIV encephalopathy. Systemic malignancy with CNS metastases is also possible.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The confusion has been progressive over 3 weeks with personality change (disinhibition, aggression) and right arm weakness developing over 10 days. No fever, no headache, no photophobia. His partner reports he has lost 6kg in weight over the past 2 months. No history of cat exposure or undercooked meat.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of fever and no cat/meat exposure reduces (but does not eliminate) toxoplasma probability — toxoplasma reactivation can occur without identifiable exposure history. No headache or photophobia makes cryptococcal meningitis less likely. Absence of fever is less consistent with cryptococcal infection. PML and PCNSL both cause subacute progressive deficits without fever. Weight loss raises concern for systemic lymphoma or occult malignancy.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV diagnosed 6 years ago. Poor antiretroviral adherence — CD4 nadir 80 cells/µL. No prior AIDS-defining illness. No prior malignancy or chemotherapy. No prior CNS infection or cryptococcal disease. Heterosexual. Non-smoker. No travel to TB-endemic countries recently. No immunosuppressive medications other than HIV itself.
prompts.reasoning: Severe immunosuppression (historical CD4 nadir 80) makes all AIDS-defining neurological complications plausible. No prior CNS infection or malignancy. No TB exposure history reduces cerebral tuberculoma. PML (JC virus reactivation) is more common at CD4 <100 but can occur up to CD4 200. PCNSL and toxoplasma are the two diagnoses most likely to present with a focal ring-enhancing mass on imaging in this setting.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 13. Right hemiparesis (arm 3/5, leg 4/5). Mild expressive aphasia. No meningism. No fever (temperature 37.0°C). No fundoscopic abnormality. No lymphadenopathy. No hepatosplenomegaly. No skin lesions. Toxoplasma IgG serology: positive.
prompts.reasoning: Right UMN pattern weakness with aphasia localises to the left hemisphere. Positive Toxoplasma IgG confirms prior exposure — reactivation is possible. However, IgG positivity alone does not confirm active toxoplasma encephalitis (>90% of HIV patients with CD4 <200 are Toxoplasma IgG positive). Afebrile state with aphasia and hemiparesis is consistent with focal mass lesions from both PCNSL and toxoplasma. PML can cause aphasia and hemiparesis but is typically afebrile and progressive without mass effect.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis and Serology
prompts.text: CT head (pre-LP): single left periventricular lesion with surrounding oedema — safe for LP. CSF: protein mildly elevated (0.9 g/L), lymphocytes 18/µL, glucose normal. CSF Toxoplasma PCR: negative. CSF Cryptococcus antigen: negative. CSF JC virus PCR: negative. CSF cytology: atypical lymphoid cells suspicious for malignancy. Serum LDH: elevated (420 U/L).
prompts.reasoning: Negative CSF Toxoplasma PCR significantly reduces toxoplasma probability (sensitivity ~50-60%, so cannot fully exclude). Negative Cryptococcus antigen and JC virus PCR effectively exclude cryptococcal meningitis and PML. Atypical lymphoid cells in CSF are highly suspicious for CNS lymphoma. Elevated LDH reflects high lymphoma tumour burden. Standard practice: empirical anti-toxoplasma treatment for 2 weeks, with failure to respond prompting biopsy for PCNSL.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Biopsy
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: single left periventricular ring-enhancing lesion (3.5cm) with central hypointensity, homogeneous enhancement, restricted diffusion (high cellularity), and contact with the ependymal surface. After failed empirical toxoplasma treatment (no clinical or radiological improvement at 2 weeks), stereotactic biopsy performed: diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) — large B-cells with prominent nucleoli, CD20 positive, MYC and BCL2 co-expression. EBV EBER positive (EBV-driven lymphomagenesis).
prompts.reasoning: Periventricular location with ependymal contact, homogeneous ring enhancement, and restricted diffusion are characteristic of PCNSL — especially in immunosuppressed patients. Toxoplasma lesions are typically multiple (ring-enhancing abscesses), peripheral (not periventricular), and do not restrict diffusion. Failure to respond to anti-toxoplasma treatment is a diagnostic criterion supporting PCNSL. DLBCL is the histological type in >90% of PCNSL. EBV association is specific to AIDS-related PCNSL (vs EBV-negative in immunocompetent patients).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 62-year-old female presents to her oncologist with a 3-week history of progressive headaches, unsteadiness when walking, and two episodes of involuntary left hand twitching lasting 30 seconds each, from which she recovered fully.
prompts.reasoning: New neurological symptoms in an oncology patient require urgent CNS evaluation. Brain metastases, GBM, and meningioma are all plausible. Radiation necrosis is important to consider in any patient with prior radiotherapy. Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis can cause multifocal symptoms. In a patient without stated cancer history, CNS lymphoma and abscess also enter the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Headaches are progressive, worst in the morning, and worsen with Valsalva. Over 3 weeks she has also developed left arm weakness and difficulty with balance. She was treated for breast cancer 3 years ago and has been on hormonal therapy, considered in remission.
prompts.reasoning: Prior breast cancer history substantially raises the probability of brain metastases — breast cancer is one of the most common sources of brain metastases. Morning headache worsening with Valsalva suggests raised ICP. Radiation necrosis is possible if prior brain irradiation was given. GBM and meningioma remain possible as independent primary events. No fever and subacute course make abscess less likely.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Breast cancer: hormone receptor-positive (ER+/PR+), HER2-negative, pT2N1M0 Stage IIA, treated with mastectomy, adjuvant chemotherapy (FEC-T), and radiotherapy to the chest wall 3 years ago. No prior cranial irradiation. Currently on letrozole. Treated hypertension. Non-smoker. No family history of brain tumours.
prompts.reasoning: No prior cranial irradiation effectively excludes radiation necrosis. ER+/PR+ HER2-negative breast cancer has a particular propensity for late brain metastases (years after primary treatment — the 'brain sanctuary' phenomenon, as chemotherapy poorly penetrates the BBB). N1 disease (nodal involvement) at presentation confers higher systemic relapse risk. Meningioma and GBM remain possible de novo events. Brain metastases now the most likely diagnosis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade II). Left facial droop (UMN pattern). Left arm power 3/5 with pronator drift. Left leg power 4/5. Right lateral gaze palsy (CN VI — false localising sign). MMSE 22/30. No meningism. Temperature 36.9°C.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral papilloedema confirms raised ICP. Left-sided UMN deficits (face, arm > leg) localise to the right hemisphere. CN VI palsy is a false localising sign of raised ICP. Significant cognitive decline (MMSE 22) suggests either multiple lesions or extensive mass effect. In a breast cancer patient with raised ICP and focal deficits, GBM is less likely but cannot be excluded without imaging. Meningioma is less likely to cause this degree of raised ICP.

prompts.label: Bloods and CT Staging
prompts.text: FBC: normal. CEA mildly elevated (8 µg/L). LDH elevated (380 U/L). CA 15-3: elevated (68 U/mL — normal <30). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: new pulmonary nodules in the right lung (3 lesions), mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and a hepatic lesion (2cm right lobe). CT head: multiple bilateral cerebral hyperdense lesions with surrounding oedema.
prompts.reasoning: Elevated CA 15-3 with pulmonary nodules, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, and hepatic lesions confirm systemic relapse of breast cancer. Multiple bilateral lesions on CT head in the context of confirmed systemic metastases is virtually diagnostic of brain metastases. GBM is excluded (primary brain tumour — no systemic metastases at onset). MRI brain confirms and characterises the metastatic burden.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Tissue Confirmation
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: 6 ring-enhancing lesions at grey-white junctions in both hemispheres, largest 3.5cm in right frontal lobe. Associated vasogenic oedema. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Stereotactic biopsy of the right frontal lesion: metastatic adenocarcinoma, morphology and immunoprofile (ER+, GATA3 positive) consistent with breast primary. HER2-negative confirmed on biopsy.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions at the grey-white junction (a watershed zone where embolic tumour cells preferentially lodge) is the classic MRI pattern of haematogenous brain metastases. GBM is characteristically solitary or oligofocal. Histopathological confirmation with breast cancer immunoprofile (ER+, GATA3) confirms metastatic breast carcinoma. Grey-white junction localisation, multiplicity, and ring-enhancing pattern all support metastatic over primary brain tumour.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old man presents to his GP with a 2-year history of progressive hearing loss in his right ear.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss with ipsilateral tinnitus has a broad differential. Presbycusis (age-related) is typically bilateral and symmetric. Ménière disease presents with episodic vertigo, fluctuating hearing loss, and tinnitus. Noise-induced hearing loss affects the 4kHz notch. Labyrinthitis is typically self-limiting. Vestibular schwannoma classically presents with progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss and tinnitus without prominent vertigo.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He also reports a constant high-pitched ringing in the same ear and intermittent mild dizziness, but no episodes of sustained rotational vertigo. He has particular difficulty understanding speech in noisy environments despite adequate volume.
prompts.reasoning: Disproportionate speech discrimination loss despite maintained sound detection is a classic feature of retrocochlear pathology (lesion affecting the cochlear nerve rather than the cochlea itself). Absent episodic vertigo and no fluctuation argue against Ménière disease. Unilateral progressive course without spontaneous improvement argues against labyrinthitis. Vestibular schwannoma and CPA epidermoid cyst remain leading diagnoses.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior significant noise exposure (not a musician, not in construction industry). No family history of bilateral acoustic neuromas or NF2. No family history of hearing problems. No ear infections in childhood. No prior cranial irradiation. Non-smoker. Normal blood pressure. Works as a software engineer.
prompts.reasoning: No NF2 family history makes bilateral vestibular schwannoma unlikely, but sporadic unilateral vestibular schwannoma does not require NF2. No noise exposure makes noise-induced hearing loss effectively excluded. No ear infection history makes chronic suppurative otitis media unlikely. The progressive unilateral course with speech discrimination loss and absent vertigo is most consistent with a CPA lesion, of which vestibular schwannoma is most common.

prompts.label: Physical Examination and Audiometry
prompts.text: Rinne test right: Rinne positive (AC > BC but reduced compared to left) — sensorineural pattern. Weber lateralises to the left (away from the worse ear — sensorineural loss on right). No middle ear effusion, normal tympanic membrane. Otoscopy normal bilaterally. Facial sensation intact. Facial movements symmetric. No cerebellar signs. Audiogram: right-sided high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss with markedly poor speech discrimination (40% correct at 60dB) — retrocochlear pattern.
prompts.reasoning: Rinne positive with reduced threshold bilaterally, Weber lateralising to the left, and audiometric sensorineural pattern confirm right-sided sensorineural hearing loss. Markedly disproportionate speech discrimination loss relative to pure tone thresholds is highly characteristic of retrocochlear (cochlear nerve) pathology — this pattern occurs because the tumour disrupts neural synchrony in the cochlear nerve. Normal facial movement rules out significant facial nerve compression (CN VII) for now. Both vestibular schwannoma and CPA epidermoid cyst remain plausible.

prompts.label: Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potentials
prompts.text: Brainstem auditory evoked potentials (BAEPs): prolonged right-sided wave I–III interpeak latency, absent wave V on right, with normal left-sided responses. ABR pattern is consistent with retrocochlear (pre-synaptic cochlear nerve) pathology on the right.
prompts.reasoning: Prolonged wave I–III interpeak latency on BAEPs confirms retrocochlear pathology affecting cochlear nerve conduction velocity — most commonly caused by a mass lesion in the internal auditory canal (IAC) or cerebellopontine angle (CPA). Both vestibular schwannoma and epidermoid cyst can produce this pattern. MRI with IAC protocol is now required for definitive diagnosis and to distinguish between these entities.

prompts.label: MRI IAM and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI internal auditory meatus (IAM) with gadolinium (thin-cut 1mm sequences): 2.5cm avidly enhancing mass at the right cerebellopontine angle with extension into the internal auditory canal ('ice cream cone' configuration — widening the IAC). Mass is centred on the porus acusticus. No bilateral lesions. Histopathology post-retrosigmoid craniotomy: Antoni A and Antoni B areas — compact elongated bipolar cells with nuclear palisading (Verocay bodies) alternating with loose myxoid stroma. S-100 strongly positive.
prompts.reasoning: The 'ice cream cone' configuration (round CPA component with cylindrical IAC extension through the porus acusticus) is the classic and essentially pathognomonic MRI appearance of vestibular schwannoma. Epidermoid cysts are non-enhancing on gadolinium (they restrict diffusion on DWI and are hypointense on T1/T2 with no enhancement). Avid homogeneous enhancement excludes epidermoid cyst. Antoni A/B histological pattern with Verocay bodies and S-100 positivity confirms schwannoma origin.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old man with no prior headache history presents to the ED with a 6-week history of progressive early-morning headaches.
prompts.reasoning: Raised ICP symptoms (morning headache, vomiting, visual disturbance) in a young adult require evaluation of intraventricular and periventricular pathology. The differential includes intraventricular tumours common in young adults: central neurocytoma (most common intraventricular tumour in young adults), ependymoma, choroid plexus papilloma, and colloid cyst. Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma occurs in tuberous sclerosis. All can cause obstructive hydrocephalus.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has had two episodes of vomiting on waking and intermittent blurred vision. The headaches are worst in the morning and have become constant over the past 2 weeks, with episodes of sudden severe headache and visual blurring when changing position.
prompts.reasoning: Positional headaches — worse with position change and accompanied by transient visual obscurations — indicate obstructive (non-communicating) hydrocephalus with ball-valve effect. Colloid cyst classically causes positional 'drop attacks' and sudden-onset positional headache from its 3rd ventricle location. Central neurocytoma and ependymoma can obstruct the foramina of Monro. The positional nature raises concern for a mobile or ball-valve lesion at the foramen of Monro.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior medical history. No family history of tuberous sclerosis, NF1, NF2, or intracranial malignancy. No skin lesions noted. Non-smoker. University student. No prior cranial irradiation. No developmental delay or learning difficulties.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of tuberous sclerosis stigmata (no skin lesions, no developmental issues) makes subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA) very unlikely — excluded. No known risk factors for any of the remaining tumours. Central neurocytoma is the most common intraventricular tumour of young adults (peak age 20-40). Colloid cyst is a developmental lesion arising at the foramen of Monro. Ependymoma can occur in the ventricle in adults.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14 (mildly confused). Bilateral papilloedema (Grade II-III). No focal limb weakness or sensory deficit. Cranial nerves intact. Cerebellar examination normal. MMSE 24/30 — attention and working memory reduced. No meningism. Skin: no ash leaf patches, no shagreen patches, no facial angiofibromata.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral papilloedema confirms raised ICP. Normal cerebellar examination (no ataxia, no nystagmus) makes a posterior fossa location less likely — narrows the location to supratentorial intraventricular disease. No tuberous sclerosis stigmata on skin definitively excludes SEGA. The preserved focal neurological examination with global ICP effects is consistent with an intraventricular mass obstructing CSF flow.

prompts.label: Bloods and CT Head
prompts.text: FBC, metabolic panel, CRP: all normal. CT head: large intraventricular mass attached to the septum pellucidum in the lateral ventricle/foramen of Monro region, with foci of calcification and cystic change, causing obstructive hydrocephalus of the lateral ventricles.
prompts.reasoning: Lateral ventricle mass at the septum pellucidum with calcification and cystic change is the characteristic CT location for central neurocytoma. Colloid cyst occurs strictly at the foramen of Monro in the anterior 3rd ventricle — not attached to the septum pellucidum — and does not contain calcification. Ependymoma can occur in the lateral ventricle but septum pellucidum attachment with calcification is more specific to neurocytoma. MRI with gadolinium required for definitive characterisation.

prompts.label: MRI Brain and Histopathology
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: large (4cm) heterogeneous intraventricular mass attached to the septum pellucidum in the right lateral ventricle, with soap-bubble architecture (multiple small cysts), moderate inhomogeneous enhancement, and no restricted diffusion. Post-resection histopathology: uniform round cells with perinuclear halos resembling oligodendrocytes, with neuropil islands. Synaptophysin strongly positive; GFAP negative. NeuN positive. IDH wild-type.
prompts.reasoning: The soap-bubble cystic architecture on MRI with septum pellucidum attachment in a young adult is highly characteristic of central neurocytoma. Ependymoma shows perivascular pseudorosettes on histology and does not show synaptophysin positivity or neuropil islands. Synaptophysin and NeuN positivity (neuronal markers) confirm the neuronal origin of central neurocytoma — distinguishing it from glial tumours. IDH wild-type and absence of 1p/19q codeletion exclude oligodendroglioma.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 19-year-old male university student is brought to the ED by his flatmates with a 12-hour history of severe headache and high fever.
prompts.reasoning: Acute onset of severe headache, fever, neck stiffness, and impaired consciousness constitutes a neurological emergency with broad differential. Bacterial meningitis, viral meningitis, encephalitis, and SAH must all be considered urgently. Tuberculous and cryptococcal meningitis typically present more subacutely. In a young student, meningococcal disease is a leading concern. The 12-hour rapid onset favours acute bacterial aetiology or SAH over subacute meningitis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He is increasingly drowsy and confused; on arrival he has marked neck stiffness. The headache developed over several hours — not instantaneously. He has vomited three times, is photophobic, and his flatmates noticed a spreading rash on his legs over the past 2 hours.
prompts.reasoning: The rash is a critical finding but its nature has not yet been confirmed — it could be the non-blanching petechial rash of meningococcal septicaemia or a viral exanthem. Gradual onset over hours (rather than instantaneous 'thunderclap') makes SAH less likely but does not exclude it. Photophobia and phonophobia can occur in both meningitis and migraine. All four diagnoses remain plausible pending examination.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Lives in a university dormitory with shared kitchen and bathroom — recent freshers' events attended. Meningococcal MenACWY vaccine received at school age 14, but not vaccinated against MenB. No immunosuppression. Non-smoker. Moderate alcohol use. No recent travel abroad. No recent illness in contacts.
prompts.reasoning: Dormitory living with freshers' events is a recognised high-risk setting for meningococcal meningitis (Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B). MenACWY vaccination but no MenB vaccination leaves MenB susceptibility — MenB is the most prevalent serogroup in the UK. Close communal living increases transmission risk. SAH becomes less likely in the absence of exertional triggers or prior 'sentinel headache'. Viral meningitis and encephalitis remain plausible.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.8°C. HR 128 bpm. BP 88/56 mmHg (hypotensive). RR 24/min. GCS 12 (E3V4M5). Severe neck stiffness — Kernig's sign positive, Brudzinski's sign positive. Purpuric, non-blanching petechial rash on both lower limbs and trunk. No focal neurological deficits. Fundoscopy deferred due to urgency.
prompts.reasoning: Non-blanching purpuric rash (does not blanch on glass test) is the hallmark of meningococcal septicaemia causing disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) and endothelial damage. This finding combined with haemodynamic instability (BP 88/56) and signs of meningism (Kernig's, Brudzinski's) constitutes a medical emergency. SAH effectively excluded — SAH does not cause purpuric rash or haemodynamic instability. Viral meningitis can rarely cause petechial rash (enterovirus) but haemodynamic compromise is atypical. Bacterial meningitis is now the dominant diagnosis.

prompts.label: Urgent Bloods
prompts.text: WBC 22.4 × 10⁹/L (neutrophilia). CRP 298 mg/L. Platelet count 68 × 10⁹/L (thrombocytopaenia). PT/APTT prolonged (consistent with DIC). Lactate 4.2 mmol/L. Blood cultures taken × 2. CT head performed urgently (pre-LP): cerebral oedema, no mass lesion, no shift — safe to proceed to LP.
prompts.reasoning: Severe neutrophilia with very high CRP, thrombocytopaenia, DIC coagulopathy, and raised lactate confirm overwhelming bacterial sepsis. This constellation is not consistent with viral meningitis (which does not cause DIC or haemodynamic instability). IV ceftriaxone and dexamethasone must be given immediately without awaiting CSF results. CT head clears for LP. All diagnostic criteria point to acute bacterial meningitis — most likely meningococcal.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis and Cultures
prompts.text: CSF: turbid/purulent. Opening pressure 290 mmH₂O (elevated). WBC 6,800 cells/µL (>95% polymorphonuclear neutrophils). Protein 4.2 g/L (markedly elevated). Glucose 0.9 mmol/L (simultaneous serum glucose 7.4 mmol/L — CSF:serum ratio 0.12, severely reduced). Gram stain: Gram-negative diplococci. CSF and blood cultures: Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B.
prompts.reasoning: CSF findings are diagnostic of pyogenic bacterial meningitis: turbid fluid, very high neutrophil pleocytosis, markedly elevated protein, and very low glucose (CSF:serum ratio <0.4 indicates bacterial origin). Gram-negative diplococci are characteristic of Neisseria meningitidis. Culture confirms MenB — the strain not covered by the patient's prior meningococcal vaccination. Public health notification required for prophylaxis of close contacts.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of progressively worsening headache, low-grade fever, and gradually increasing confusion.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute meningitis (3 weeks of symptoms) with fever, headache, and confusion has a broad differential that favours infectious meningitis over acute bacterial causes. The subacute time course particularly raises concern for tuberculous meningitis, cryptococcal meningitis, and fungal meningitis. The geographic background from a high TB-endemic country increases TB probability. Viral encephalitis and CNS lymphoma are also plausible.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has felt generally unwell with productive cough, drenching night sweats, and 8 kg of unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months. He also reports left eye drooping and intermittent double vision developing over the past week.
prompts.reasoning: Productive cough, night sweats, and significant weight loss — the constitutional triad of tuberculosis — preceding the neurological presentation strongly suggest TB as the underlying disease. New cranial nerve palsy (left eye drooping and diplopia — CN III or external ophthalmoplegia) indicates cranial nerve involvement, which in the context of subacute meningitis suggests basilar meningitis. Basal meningeal inflammation is the hallmark of TB and cryptococcal meningitis. Acute bacterial meningitis and viral meningitis now less likely — too subacute.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Born and raised in Indonesia (high TB-endemic country). Works in a crowded urban environment in Jakarta. Received BCG vaccination in childhood — scar visible on left arm. No known prior TB diagnosis or treatment. Tested HIV negative in Indonesia 6 months ago. No corticosteroid or immunosuppressive use. Non-smoker. No prior chest X-ray abnormality known.
prompts.reasoning: Prior BCG vaccination reduces but does not eliminate risk of TB — BCG is only ~70-80% protective against pulmonary TB and offers incomplete CNS protection. Negative HIV test 6 months ago reduces (but does not eliminate) risk of cryptococcal meningitis, which overwhelmingly occurs in AIDS patients. Crowded living and TB-endemic origin remain strong risk factors for TB reactivation or primary CNS TB. Fungal meningitis is less common in immunocompetent individuals from Southeast Asia.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.2°C. GCS 13. Neck stiffness moderate. Left CN III palsy: complete ptosis, eye deviated 'down and out', pupil 3mm (partial mydriasis). Right CN VI palsy: right lateral gaze limitation. Fundoscopy: early bilateral papilloedema. Mild right-sided hemiparesis. No skin lesions. Chest percussion: dull right upper zone.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple cranial nerve palsies (CN III left, CN VI right) with papilloedema and hemiparesis indicate severe basal meningeal inflammation with vasculitis causing infarction (hemiparesis) — the hallmark complications of tuberculous meningitis. Cryptococcal meningitis can also cause raised ICP and cranial nerve palsies but hemiparesis from vasculitis is less characteristic. Right upper zone dullness suggests active pulmonary TB (the likely primary source). Fungal meningitis (excluding Cryptococcus) is now very unlikely.

prompts.label: Bloods and Chest Imaging
prompts.text: WBC 11.2 × 10⁹/L (mild lymphocytosis). CRP 68 mg/L (modestly elevated). HIV test (repeat): negative. Serum cryptococcal antigen: negative. Chest X-ray: right upper lobe consolidation with cavitation and ipsilateral hilar lymphadenopathy — pattern highly consistent with active pulmonary TB. ESR elevated (88 mm/hr). Sputum AFB smear: positive (acid-fast bacilli seen on Ziehl-Neelsen stain).
prompts.reasoning: Positive sputum AFB smear with characteristic pulmonary TB chest X-ray (upper lobe cavitation with hilar lymphadenopathy) confirms active pulmonary tuberculosis as the primary source. Negative HIV and negative serum cryptococcal antigen definitively exclude cryptococcal meningitis. The combination of confirmed pulmonary TB with subacute basilar meningitis, multiple cranial nerve palsies, and vasculitic hemiparesis is now effectively diagnostic of TB meningitis. LP is required to confirm CNS involvement.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis and Confirmatory Tests
prompts.text: CSF (post-imaging, with platelets checked): slightly turbid, opening pressure 260 mmH₂O. WBC 320 cells/µL (predominantly lymphocytes — 85%). Protein: 1.8 g/L (markedly elevated). CSF glucose: 1.4 mmol/L (simultaneous serum glucose 5.8 mmol/L — CSF:serum ratio 0.24, severely reduced). CSF AFB smear: rare AFB seen on ZN stain. CSF MTB PCR (GeneXpert Ultra): positive for M. tuberculosis, rifampicin resistance gene not detected. MRI brain: thick basal meningeal enhancement, bilateral periventricular infarcts (vasculitis), and communicating hydrocephalus.
prompts.reasoning: CSF showing lymphocytic pleocytosis, very high protein, very low glucose (CSF:serum <0.5), with AFB on smear and MTB PCR positive confirms tuberculous meningitis. MRI basal enhancement is pathognomonic of TBM — the thick exudate enveloping basal cisterns is the hallmark of TBM pathology. Periventricular infarcts from vasculitis explain the hemiparesis. Communicating hydrocephalus results from blockage of arachnoid villi by inflammatory exudate. Rifampicin sensitivity allows standard RHEZ (rifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol, pyrazinamide) therapy to begin, with adjunctive dexamethasone.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 22-year-old woman presents to the ED with a 2-day history of headache, fever, and neck stiffness.
prompts.reasoning: Headache, fever, and neck stiffness is the classic meningitic triad requiring urgent evaluation. Acute bacterial meningitis must be excluded first as the most dangerous diagnosis. SAH, viral meningitis, viral encephalitis, and TB meningitis are all plausible. The 2-day course suggests infectious rather than haemorrhagic aetiology, but both remain on the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She feels generally unwell and has vomited once. The headache developed gradually over 24 hours and has worsened progressively; she is photophobic and phonophobic, and reports a mild sore throat and coryzal symptoms 5 days ago that have now resolved. No confusion, no focal neurological symptoms, no rash.
prompts.reasoning: A preceding viral upper respiratory illness followed by meningism is a classic prodrome of viral (aseptic) meningitis — enteroviruses commonly cause this pattern. Absence of confusion or focal deficits makes viral encephalitis less likely. Absent rash makes meningococcal disease less likely. Gradual onset over 24 hours makes SAH less likely. All four diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Final-year university student. Lives in a shared house with 4 others — all currently well. Non-smoker. No immunosuppression. No foreign travel. Fully vaccinated including MenACWY. No prior neurological disease. No medications. No sexual contacts with STIs. No recent tick exposure.
prompts.reasoning: Shared living raises meningococcal concern, but MenACWY vaccination and well contacts reduce this. No immunosuppression or HIV risk factors make cryptococcal meningitis unlikely. No tick exposure reduces Lyme disease. TB meningitis unlikely without endemic exposure or risk factors. Acute bacterial meningitis, viral meningitis, and viral encephalitis remain the working differential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.4°C. HR 98 bpm. BP 118/74 mmHg. GCS 15 — fully alert and oriented. Mild neck stiffness. Kernig's sign weakly positive. No rash. Cranial nerves intact. Full neurological examination normal between assessments. Throat mildly erythematous. No lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Full GCS 15 with preserved cognition and no focal deficits makes encephalitis (which causes altered cognition) less likely. No rash makes meningococcal disease less likely. Haemodynamically stable without sepsis criteria makes severe bacterial meningitis less likely — but bacterial meningitis can present before haemodynamic compromise. LP is required to differentiate.

prompts.label: Bloods and CT Head
prompts.text: WBC 9.8 × 10⁹/L (mild lymphocytosis). CRP 28 mg/L (mildly elevated). Procalcitonin 0.08 ng/mL (normal — <0.5 suggests non-bacterial). Blood cultures: no growth at 48 hours. CT head: normal, no contraindication to LP. Blood film: no malaria parasites.
prompts.reasoning: Mild lymphocytosis and only mildly elevated CRP with normal procalcitonin suggests a viral rather than bacterial infection — procalcitonin rises sharply in bacterial sepsis and is a useful discriminator. However, blood results alone cannot exclude bacterial meningitis early in its course. Normal CT clears for LP. CSF analysis is now the definitive next step.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis
prompts.text: CSF: clear, colourless. Opening pressure 170 mmH₂O (upper normal). WBC 148 cells/µL (predominantly lymphocytes — 92%). Protein 0.68 g/L (mildly elevated). Glucose 3.2 mmol/L (simultaneous serum glucose 5.6 mmol/L — CSF:serum ratio 0.57, normal). Gram stain: no organisms. Culture: no growth. CSF enterovirus PCR: positive (Echovirus 11). Bacterial 16S PCR: negative.
prompts.reasoning: CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with mildly elevated protein and normal glucose is the hallmark of viral (aseptic) meningitis — distinguishing it from bacterial meningitis (neutrophilic pleocytosis, very high protein, very low glucose). Positive CSF enterovirus PCR (Echovirus 11) confirms the diagnosis. Enteroviruses (echovirus, coxsackievirus) account for >85% of viral meningitis cases. No treatment beyond supportive care required — condition is self-limiting.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of progressive headache, low-grade fever, and increasing confusion.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute meningitis with progressive confusion and personality change over weeks has a broad differential including both infectious and non-infectious causes. The subacute course is most consistent with cryptococcal meningitis, TB meningitis, viral encephalitis, or CNS lymphoma. Acute bacterial meningitis is less likely with this time course. Geography, immune status, and exposure history will be critical to narrow the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His partner reports a noticeable personality change over the past month. The headache is severe, constant, and has worsened over 2 weeks; he has vomited daily for the past 5 days and reports intermittent blurred vision. He has lost 7 kg over the past 3 months with no respiratory symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: Severe raised ICP symptoms (vomiting, visual blurring, progressive headache) with significant weight loss point to either a serious CNS infection or malignancy. Raised ICP in fungal meningitis results from impaired CSF absorption (blockage of arachnoid villi by capsular material). Weight loss over 3 months raises concern for underlying immunosuppression or systemic disease. Absence of respiratory symptoms makes pulmonary TB less obvious.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV-positive — diagnosed 3 years ago, CD4 count 45 cells/µL at last check 4 months ago, currently not on antiretroviral therapy due to non-adherence. Works as a groundskeeper — regular contact with soil and bird droppings (pigeons). Non-smoker. No prior CNS infection. No corticosteroid use.
prompts.reasoning: Severe immunosuppression (CD4 45) is the major risk factor for cryptococcal meningitis — Cryptococcus neoformans is an opportunistic fungus that overwhelmingly affects AIDS patients with CD4 <100. Environmental exposure to pigeon droppings is the classic source of C. neoformans inhalation — the fungus grows in bird excreta. TB meningitis and AIDS-related CNS lymphoma are also major differentials at this CD4 count. Cryptococcus is now strongly suspected.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.8°C. GCS 13. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade III — severe). No neck stiffness despite meningitis. Left CN VI palsy (false localising sign from raised ICP). No focal hemiparesis. No skin lesions. Oral candidiasis (thrush) present. Lymph nodes: bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Severe papilloedema disproportionate to signs of meningism (neck stiffness may be absent in cryptococcal meningitis — the organism's polysaccharide capsule blunts the inflammatory response). Absent neck stiffness despite severe raised ICP is a characteristic feature of cryptococcal meningitis. Oral candidiasis confirms severe immunosuppression. CN VI palsy is a false localising sign from raised ICP. TB meningitis can also present with severe raised ICP and CN palsies.

prompts.label: Bloods and Antigen Testing
prompts.text: CD4 count: 38 cells/µL (confirmed severe immunosuppression). HIV viral load: 280,000 copies/mL. Serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg): positive (titre 1:1024). CRP 42 mg/L. FBC: lymphopaenia. Serum LDH normal. CT head: mild generalised cerebral oedema, no focal lesions, no hydrocephalus — safe to proceed to LP.
prompts.reasoning: Positive serum CrAg at high titre (1:1024) is highly sensitive and specific for cryptococcal meningitis in HIV patients — sensitivity >99%, specificity >97%. This finding is effectively diagnostic. TB meningitis does not produce a positive cryptococcal antigen. LP is still required to confirm CSF involvement, quantify fungal burden, measure opening pressure, and guide treatment (intracranial hypertension management is critical in cryptococcal meningitis).

prompts.label: CSF Analysis
prompts.text: CSF opening pressure: 420 mmH₂O (severely elevated). CSF appearance: slightly turbid. WBC: 18 cells/µL (predominantly lymphocytes). Protein: 0.8 g/L. Glucose: 2.4 mmol/L (CSF:serum ratio 0.42 — borderline low). India ink stain: encapsulated yeast cells clearly visible (budding yeast with large polysaccharide capsule creating halo). CSF CrAg titre: 1:2048. CSF culture: Cryptococcus neoformans. Fungal burden: high.
prompts.reasoning: India ink stain showing encapsulated budding yeast is pathognomonic of Cryptococcus. The markedly elevated opening pressure (420 mmH₂O — normal <200) reflects impaired CSF absorption from polysaccharide capsule deposition in arachnoid granulations. Paradoxically low cell count despite severe infection reflects the immunosuppressed state's inability to mount inflammation. Treatment: liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine induction, then fluconazole consolidation. Serial therapeutic LP or lumbar drain for ICP control.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 41-year-old male presents to the ED with a 10-day history of progressively worsening headache, low-grade fever, and a single generalised tonic-clonic seizure this morning.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute progressive headache with fever and new-onset seizure requires urgent evaluation for both infectious and neoplastic intracranial pathology. Cerebral abscess, subdural empyema, encephalitis, GBM, and metastases all enter the differential. The fever with seizure raises concern for an infectious space-occupying lesion. Time course of 10 days makes acute bacterial meningitis less likely as the primary process.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has had progressive right arm weakness over the past 5 days and expressive word-finding difficulty over 3 days. He had dental extractions performed 3 weeks ago by his dentist for a dental abscess. He has also had right-sided ear discharge and otalgia for 2 months, for which he has not yet sought treatment.
prompts.reasoning: Recent dental procedure and untreated otitis media (ear discharge, otalgia) are recognised sources of cerebral abscess — dental and otogenic infections are among the most common sources. Direct spread from a temporal/mastoid infection or haematogenous seeding from dental bacteraemia are both plausible routes. Progressive focal deficits (right arm weakness, expressive aphasia) localise to the left hemisphere. This constellation raises cerebral abscess as the dominant diagnosis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No known immunosuppression. Not HIV-positive. Not diabetic. No prior malignancy. Non-smoker. Moderate alcohol use. No recent foreign travel. No intravenous drug use. No prior neurological history. No antibiotic use recently. No cardiac valve disease known.
prompts.reasoning: No immunosuppression or HIV makes toxoplasmosis and cryptococcal abscess less likely. No IV drug use reduces infective endocarditis-related septic emboli. No prior malignancy reduces metastases, though occult primary cannot be excluded. The dental and ear infection sources make pyogenic abscess the most plausible diagnosis. GBM remains on the differential as it can present similarly with fever from necrosis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.6°C. HR 104 bpm. GCS 14. Right arm power 3/5. Expressive aphasia. No meningism. Right tympanic membrane perforated on otoscopy with purulent discharge. No papilloedema. No skin stigmata of infective endocarditis. Dental examination: healing left lower molar socket.
prompts.reasoning: Active right-sided otitis media with perforated tympanic membrane and purulent discharge — directly adjacent to the temporal lobe — confirms an active infectious source. Progressive left hemisphere UMN deficits with focal aphasia indicate a left-sided mass lesion. Absent meningism makes meningitis less likely as the primary process. Subdural empyema (pus between dura and brain) can also arise from otogenic source and cause focal deficits. GBM now less likely given active infectious source.

prompts.label: Bloods
prompts.text: WBC 17.6 × 10⁹/L (neutrophilia). CRP 186 mg/L (markedly elevated). ESR 94 mm/hr. Blood cultures × 2: pending. Procalcitonin 1.4 ng/mL (elevated, consistent with bacterial infection). HIV test: negative. CT head: 3cm ring-enhancing lesion in the left frontal-temporal region with significant surrounding oedema and 4mm midline shift. Adjacent to the temporal lobe.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated WBC and CRP confirm active bacterial infection. CT showing a ring-enhancing lesion adjacent to the temporal lobe in the context of active otitis media is highly consistent with otogenic cerebral abscess. Subdural empyema would typically show a crescent-shaped extra-axial collection. The lesion's intra-axial position favours parenchymal abscess. MRI with DWI is required for definitive characterisation.

prompts.label: MRI Brain with DWI
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: smooth, thin-walled ring-enhancing lesion (3.2cm) in the left temporal lobe with a hypointense core. DWI: marked restricted diffusion in the central core (very high DWI signal, low ADC — pus restricts diffusion due to high viscosity). No satellite lesions. Craniotomy and drainage performed: thick green pus aspirated. Culture: Streptococcus milleri group (viridans streptococcus). Susceptible to penicillin.
prompts.reasoning: Restricted diffusion in the core of a ring-enhancing lesion is the radiological hallmark of cerebral abscess — pus has restricted water diffusion due to its viscosity, distinguishing it from necrotic tumour (which does NOT restrict diffusion).
