prompts.text: CT abdomen/pelvis: bilateral renal cysts and a 2.6 cm heterogeneously enhancing right renal mass (RCC likely); pancreatic cysts noted; 24-hour urine metanephrines: normal; germline genetic testing confirms a pathogenic missense mutation in the VHL gene (chromosome 3p25.3).
prompts.reasoning: VHL gene on chromosome 3p25.3 encodes the VHL protein (pVHL), which is an E3 ubiquitin ligase component targeting hypoxia-inducible factor-alpha (HIF-α) for proteasomal degradation under normoxia. Loss of pVHL → constitutive HIF-α activation → upregulation of VEGF, PDGF, EPO → hypervascular tumours (haemangioblastomas, RCC). The complete VHL phenotype in this patient: cerebellar haemangioblastoma + spinal haemangioblastomas + RCC + pancreatic cysts. Normal metanephrines excludes phaeochromocytoma at this time (requires annual surveillance). Management: neurosurgical resection of cerebellar haemangioblastoma + VP shunt or ETV for hydrocephalus; nephron-sparing surgery for RCC when >3 cm; belzutifan (HIF-2α inhibitor) approved for VHL-associated tumours.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 2-year history of recurrent severe headaches occurring 3 to 4 times per month.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent episodic headache is the most common neurological complaint presenting to primary care. The primary headache disorders — migraine, tension-type, and cluster — account for the vast majority. Medication overuse headache must always be sought in patients with frequent headaches. Secondary causes (raised ICP from IIH or CVT, hypertensive headache) must be excluded by identifying red flag features. The frequency (3–4/month), duration, severity, and associated features will be required before narrowing the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Each episode lasts 12 to 24 hours and is sufficiently severe to prevent her from working or studying; she reports no visual disturbance or focal neurological symptoms before or during the attacks, and no visual obscurations.
prompts.reasoning: Duration of 12–24 hours (within the 4–72 hour ICHD-3 range for migraine) and disabling severity argue against tension-type headache (typically mild-to-moderate) and cluster headache (typically 15–180 minutes). The absence of visual aura or focal neurological symptoms (preceding the headache by 20–60 minutes) does not exclude migraine — approximately 70% of migraineurs have migraine without aura. No visual obscurations (which suggest papilloedema and raised ICP) reduces the probability of IIH. At least four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Her mother and older sister both suffer from 'bad headaches'; she takes ibuprofen 2 to 3 times per week for headache relief, which she has done for the past 8 months; no prior medical diagnoses; takes no regular medications.
prompts.reasoning: A positive family history of severe headaches supports a primary headache disorder with genetic predisposition — migraine has 50% heritability. NSAID use ≥10–15 days per month for ≥3 months meets the threshold for medication overuse headache (MOH), which must now be considered — she is taking ibuprofen 2–3 days/week (approximately 8–12 days/month) and has been doing so for 8 months. However, her headache frequency (3–4/month) is episodic rather than daily/near-daily, which makes primary MOH less likely than episodic migraine with risk of transformation. Cluster headache occurs almost exclusively in males (5:1 male predominance).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Neurological examination is entirely normal; fundoscopy reveals no papilloedema and normal optic disc margins; blood pressure 112/70 mmHg; no meningism; cranial nerves intact; no scalp tenderness or temporal artery abnormality.
prompts.reasoning: A normal neurological examination with no papilloedema effectively excludes secondary headache from raised intracranial pressure (IIH, space-occupying lesion, CVT). Normal blood pressure excludes hypertensive headache. No temporal artery tenderness or scalp sensitivity reduces the probability of giant cell arteritis (inappropriate age group also). The examination does not discriminate between the primary headache disorders — normal neurology is expected in all three remaining diagnoses. Detailed headache characterisation is now required.

prompts.label: Headache Diary & Characterisation
prompts.text: A 4-week headache diary documents 4 attacks: each is unilateral (alternating sides between attacks), pulsating/throbbing in quality, severe intensity (7–8/10), aggravated by climbing stairs, and associated with nausea and vomiting; she retreats to a darkened, quiet room during each attack.
prompts.reasoning: ICHD-3 criteria for migraine without aura require ≥5 attacks of 4–72 hours' duration with ≥2 of: (1) unilateral location, (2) pulsating quality, (3) moderate-to-severe intensity, (4) aggravation by routine physical activity; AND ≥1 of: (1) nausea/vomiting, (2) photophobia + phonophobia. This patient fulfils all criteria. Tension-type headache is bilateral, pressing/non-pulsating, not aggravated by activity, and not associated with significant nausea/vomiting — definitively excluded. MOH remains a concern given her analgesic frequency, but the episodic pattern and current frequency (4/month, not ≥15 days) means current MOH criteria are not yet met.

prompts.label: Investigations & Treatment Response
prompts.text: MRI brain (performed to exclude secondary causes given age and clinical context): normal; sumatriptan 50 mg taken at onset of the next three attacks provides complete headache relief within 2 hours in each episode; analgesic overuse counselled and ibuprofen frequency reduced.
prompts.reasoning: Normal MRI brain confirms the absence of structural pathology, CVT, IIH, or other secondary causes. Triptan (5-HT1B/1D agonist) response — complete resolution within 2 hours — is highly characteristic of migraine. Triptans are not effective for tension-type headache (which does not involve the trigeminovascular pathway). MOH is excluded by the episodic pattern and confirmed by analgesic reduction without worsening headache frequency. Final diagnosis: migraine without aura (ICHD-3 code 1.1). Preventive therapy (topiramate, propranolol, amitriptyline, CGRP monoclonal antibodies) should be considered if ≥4 disabling attacks/month.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 19-year-old woman attends the emergency department with sudden-onset visual disturbance that resolved after 30 minutes, followed by a severe headache.
prompts.reasoning: Transient visual disturbance followed by headache in a young woman requires urgent evaluation to distinguish migraine with aura from cerebrovascular events (TIA, posterior fossa ischaemia) and occipital lobe seizure. A TIA causing visual field loss (homonymous hemianopia from PCA territory ischaemia) can precede headache and may be misidentified as migraine aura. Retinal artery occlusion causes monocular visual loss (distinct from binocular). Occipital seizures produce visual hallucinations, typically brief and stereotyped, and may be followed by postictal headache.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The visual disturbance was binocular — a shimmering, crescent-shaped arc of light that expanded slowly from the centre to the left periphery of her visual field over 20–25 minutes before disappearing completely; the severe headache began as the visual symptoms resolved.
prompts.reasoning: The spreading, slow progression of a scintillating scotoma over 20–25 minutes (the 'march' across the visual field) is characteristic of cortical spreading depression — the neurophysiological correlate of migrainous aura. TIA-related visual loss is typically negative (visual field defect — darkness/absence) rather than positive (shimmering lights/zigzags), and develops abruptly rather than slowly spreading. Occipital seizures produce brief (<3 minutes), stereotyped visual hallucinations (coloured circles) without the gradual spread of migrainous aura. Binocular distribution indicates retrochiasmal cortical origin (homologous to both occipital cortices). At least three diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She reports two identical episodes in the past 10 months, both preceded by the same visual disturbance; she is currently taking the combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP); no personal or family history of stroke or clotting disorders.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent stereotyped episodes with identical visual precursors are most consistent with migraine with aura — TIAs are typically non-stereotyped (different territories) and singular until a major stroke occurs. COCP use is an important risk factor in this context: combined oestrogen-containing contraceptives are contraindicated in migraine with aura due to a 2–4-fold increased ischaemic stroke risk. COCP use also raises the probability of cerebral venous thrombosis, which can mimic migraine. The absence of family history of clotting disorders reduces (but does not exclude) thrombophilic CVT. EEG and MRI are required to exclude structural and seizure-related causes.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Neurological examination is entirely normal between episodes; blood pressure 116/72 mmHg; no papilloedema; visual fields full to confrontation; no carotid bruit; no focal weakness, sensory loss, or dysphasia; cardiac examination normal.
prompts.reasoning: A normal inter-ictal neurological examination is consistent with all three remaining diagnoses — TIA, seizure, and migraine are all characterised by normal interictal neurology. Normal visual fields between episodes argue against a fixed cortical lesion but do not exclude it. Normal blood pressure and no carotid bruit reduce (but do not eliminate) the probability of structural vascular disease. Normal cardiac examination reduces the probability of cardioembolic TIA (atrial fibrillation, valvular disease), which would be unusual at age 19 without an underlying structural abnormality. MRI and EEG are required.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & EEG
prompts.text: MRI brain including DWI/ADC sequences: no acute or chronic infarction, no cortical signal change, no structural lesion; MRI MRA (intracranial and cervical): normal; EEG performed interictally: normal study with no epileptiform discharges.
prompts.reasoning: Normal DWI/ADC excludes acute cortical infarction; normal MRA excludes significant intracranial or cervical vascular disease, posterior fossa stenosis, or AVM. Normal interictal EEG reduces but does not exclude occipital seizures (a single interictal EEG has only ~50% sensitivity for focal epilepsy). However, combined with the characteristic slowly-spreading aura phenomenology over 20–25 minutes (which is too slow for seizure discharge propagation, typically seconds), normal EEG is sufficient to exclude seizure as the likely diagnosis. ICHD-3 criteria for migraine with typical aura are met: ≥2 attacks, fully reversible visual aura spreading gradually over >5 minutes, lasting 20–60 minutes, followed by headache.

prompts.label: Vascular & Thrombophilia Workup
prompts.text: Echocardiogram: normal left ventricular function, no patent foramen ovale detected; thrombophilia screen: negative; lipid profile: normal; COCP discontinued due to contraindication in migraine with aura; sumatriptan prescribed for acute attacks and topiramate initiated as prophylaxis.
prompts.reasoning: Normal echocardiogram (no PFO — PFO is associated with paradoxical embolism and migrainous stroke) and negative thrombophilia screen eliminate the main structural and haematological causes of recurrent thromboembolic events in young women. COCP is absolutely contraindicated in migraine with aura (WHO Category 4 — unacceptable health risk): oestrogen-induced hypercoagulability combined with vasospasm from aura significantly increases ischaemic stroke risk. Switching to progestogen-only contraception is recommended. Acute treatment: triptans (5-HT1B/1D agonists) — contraindicated during the aura phase (theoretical vasospasm risk), take at headache onset. Prophylaxis: topiramate (caution — teratogenic), propranolol, or CGRP monoclonal antibodies.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old male accountant presents with a 4-month history of daily headaches that he rates as moderate in severity.
prompts.reasoning: Daily headache persisting for 4 months in a middle-aged man requires both characterisation of the headache type and exclusion of secondary causes. Chronic daily headache (>15 days/month for >3 months) is an umbrella category encompassing chronic tension-type, chronic migraine, medication overuse headache, and secondary causes. Red flags (progressive, new headache pattern, systemic symptoms, focal neurology) must be excluded before attributing daily headache to a primary disorder. Hypertensive headache and cervicogenic causes are important to assess.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headaches occur most days, typically begin in the mid-afternoon, last 3 to 6 hours, and are not associated with nausea, vomiting, or sensitivity to light or sound; they do not prevent him from continuing with activities.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of nausea, vomiting, photophobia, or phonophobia argues against migraine (which requires ≥1 of nausea/vomiting or photophobia+phonophobia). Non-disabling headache that does not prevent activity is inconsistent with migraine (which causes avoidance of physical activity). Tension-type headache is characteristically non-disabling and lacks the autonomic accompaniments of migraine. Afternoon onset is consistent with tension-type (related to postural fatigue, stress accumulation). Medication overuse and cervicogenic headache remain important differentials for daily headache. At least four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has been under significant work-related stress for 6 months with prolonged computer use; he takes paracetamol on approximately 3 days per week for headache relief and has done so for 4 months; blood pressure was documented as normal at an occupational health screen 3 months ago; no prior headache history.
prompts.reasoning: Occupational stress and prolonged screen use with sustained postures are established precipitants for tension-type headache via sustained pericranial muscle contraction. Paracetamol use 3 days per week for 4 months approaches but does not quite meet the formal medication overuse threshold (≥10 days/month of non-opioid analgesics for ≥3 months) — however, MOH remains a risk. Recent normal BP from occupational health reduces the probability of hypertensive headache, though BP should be documented today. New onset of daily headache in a 35-year-old with occupational stressors and recent normal neurology leans toward a primary headache disorder.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Blood pressure 126/80 mmHg; neurological examination normal throughout; fundoscopy: no papilloedema, normal disc margins; bilateral pericranial muscle tenderness on manual palpation (temporalis and suboccipital muscles); no cervical spine tenderness, normal cervical range of motion.
prompts.reasoning: Normal fundoscopy with no papilloedema effectively excludes raised intracranial pressure — the most dangerous secondary cause of daily headache. Normal blood pressure today (and previously) excludes hypertensive headache. Bilateral pericranial muscle tenderness on manual palpation is the key physical sign of tension-type headache — increased tenderness of pericranial myofascial structures (detected by manual pressure) is the most significant abnormal finding and is present in approximately 90% of patients with chronic TTH. Normal cervical range of motion and absence of cervical spine tenderness reduce the probability of cervicogenic headache, which typically reproduces pain with cervical movement and has unilateral occipital/neck origin.

prompts.label: Headache Characterisation
prompts.text: A 4-week headache diary confirms headaches on 22 of 28 days; each episode is bilateral, pressing or tightening in quality, mild-to-moderate in intensity, and is not worsened by routine physical activity such as climbing stairs or walking; no nausea, no photophobia, no phonophobia during any episode.
prompts.reasoning: ICHD-3 diagnostic criteria for tension-type headache: headache lasting 30 minutes–7 days; ≥2 of (1) bilateral location, (2) pressing/tightening quality, (3) mild-to-moderate intensity, (4) not aggravated by routine physical activity; AND both (1) no nausea/vomiting AND (2) no more than one of photophobia or phonophobia. This patient fulfils all criteria. Chronic TTH = ≥15 days/month for >3 months (22/28 days confirms this). Migraine is excluded by bilateral, pressing, non-pulsating quality without nausea/photophobia/phonophobia and no aggravation by activity. Cervicogenic headache is excluded by bilateral location (cervicogenic is strictly unilateral) and absent cervical signs.

prompts.label: Imaging & Management
prompts.text: MRI brain: normal study, no structural abnormality; diagnosis confirmed as chronic tension-type headache; amitriptyline 10 mg nocte commenced as preventive therapy; referral for cognitive behavioural therapy and biofeedback for stress management; physiotherapy for pericranial muscle techniques; analgesic frequency reduction counselled.
prompts.reasoning: Normal MRI brain excludes structural secondary causes — imaging is warranted in new daily headache persisting >4 months or with any atypical features. Chronic TTH pathophysiology involves sensitisation of peripheral pericranial myofascial nociceptors (peripheral sensitisation) and, in chronic forms, central sensitisation of trigeminal nucleus caudalis. Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline) are the first-line preventive therapy for chronic TTH — efficacy via descending pain modulation and central sensitisation reduction. CBT and biofeedback are evidence-based non-pharmacological treatments. Analgesic overuse prevention is essential — regular paracetamol use risks MOH transformation. NSAIDs and paracetamol are appropriate acute therapy when used infrequently.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old man presents to his GP with a 3-week history of episodes of sudden, severe unilateral head pain occurring at least once daily.
prompts.reasoning: Episodic severe unilateral head pain occurring daily in a middle-aged man requires systematic evaluation of the trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs — cluster headache, paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT), primary headache disorders, structural causes (cavernous sinus pathology causing CN III/V irritation), and sinogenic pain. The frequency (at least once daily) and unilateral character provide the first discriminatory axis, but the attack duration, associated features, and temporal pattern are required to narrow the differential further.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Each episode lasts between 30 and 90 minutes, reaches maximal severity within minutes of onset, and he becomes extremely agitated and restless during attacks — unable to lie still — unlike his occasional tension headaches in the past.
prompts.reasoning: Attack duration of 30–90 minutes is within the range for cluster headache (15–180 min) and paroxysmal hemicrania (2–30 min, but PH is typically shorter). SUNCT attacks last only seconds to minutes — the 30–90 minute duration makes SUNCT unlikely. Agitation and restlessness during attacks distinguish TACs and migraine: migraineurs are prostrated and seek rest (photophobia/phonophobia), whereas TAC patients are characteristically unable to remain still. At least five diagnoses remain plausible after P2.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has had identical bouts of daily attacks every spring for the past 4 years, each bout lasting 6 to 8 weeks before resolving completely; he smokes 20 cigarettes/day; he drinks alcohol occasionally but has noticed it reliably triggers an attack when these bouts are occurring.
prompts.reasoning: A strict seasonal periodicity with complete remission between bouts is the defining temporal pattern of episodic cluster headache — attacks occur in 'clusters' lasting weeks to months, then remit. Paroxysmal hemicrania and SUNCT rarely show this clear seasonal episodic pattern with complete interbout remission. Alcohol as a reliable attack trigger during the active cluster period (but not during remission) is characteristic of cluster headache — alcohol does not typically trigger paroxysmal hemicrania or SUNCT. Cavernous sinus pathology would not remit seasonally. A structural cause still requires exclusion by imaging.

prompts.label: Physical Examination During Witnessed Attack
prompts.text: Examination during an attack witnessed in clinic: the patient is agitated and pacing; right-sided conjunctival injection and lacrimation; mild right-sided nasal congestion; pain localised periorbital/temporal on the right; neurological examination between attacks is entirely normal.
prompts.reasoning: Ipsilateral cranial autonomic features (conjunctival injection, lacrimation, nasal congestion) are present in both cluster headache and paroxysmal hemicrania — both TACs share these features via the trigeminal-autonomic reflex arc (trigeminal activation → parasympathetic outflow via sphenopalatine ganglion). Agitation and pacing are more prominent in cluster headache than paroxysmal hemicrania. SUNCT is excluded by the attack duration of 30–90 minutes. The remaining critical discriminator is attack duration and the response to indomethacin — paroxysmal hemicrania is absolutely and completely indomethacin-responsive.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Indomethacin Trial
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium including pituitary fossa views: normal — no cavernous sinus lesion, no sellar mass, no structural abnormality; a 2-week therapeutic trial of indomethacin 150 mg/day produces no reduction in attack frequency or severity.
prompts.reasoning: Paroxysmal hemicrania is defined by an absolute, complete response to indomethacin — failure to respond effectively excludes it. This is an ICHD-3 diagnostic criterion for paroxysmal hemicrania: if indomethacin does not abort attacks completely, paroxysmal hemicrania cannot be the diagnosis. Normal MRI excludes structural cavernous sinus, sellar, or posterior fossa pathology that can mimic TACs. Cluster headache remains the sole diagnosis. It is confirmed by clinical pattern, autonomic features, attack duration, and indomethacin non-response.

prompts.label: Acute & Prophylactic Treatment Response
prompts.text: High-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask (12 L/min) aborts the next attack within 15 minutes; subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg is effective in under 10 minutes in subsequent attacks; verapamil 240 mg/day is commenced as cluster period prophylaxis with reduction in attack frequency.
prompts.reasoning: High-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan are the two first-line acute treatments for cluster headache — both are highly effective and specific for this diagnosis. Neither is recommended for paroxysmal hemicrania or SUNCT. The mechanism of oxygen involves vasoconstriction and modulation of trigeminal nucleus caudalis activity. Sumatriptan acts at 5-HT1B/1D receptors. Verapamil (calcium channel blocker) is the gold standard prophylactic for the cluster period — it suppresses attack frequency by 70–80%. The response pattern confirms cluster headache. Other prophylactic options: lithium, topiramate, short-course oral prednisolone during cluster onset.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 69-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of a new, persistent headache she describes as unlike any headache she has experienced before.
prompts.reasoning: A new, uncharacterised persistent headache in a 69-year-old requires systematic evaluation. New-onset headache in patients over 50 years must prompt exclusion of giant cell arteritis (GCA), space-occupying lesion, subdural haematoma, and CVT before attributing it to a primary headache disorder. New-onset migraine is unusual after age 50 but possible. The descriptor 'unlike any prior headache' is a red flag in this age group requiring active exclusion of secondary causes.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache is constant, aching, and located predominantly over both temples; she also reports that over the past 2 to 3 weeks she has noticed fatigue when chewing food, with aching in her jaw that develops towards the end of meals.
prompts.reasoning: Temporal location is non-specific (also seen in tension-type, cervicogenic, and migraine). The jaw aching with chewing that develops and worsens during a meal is a claudicant pattern — ischaemic fatigue of the masseter due to reduced blood flow in the external carotid territory during exercise (chewing). This pattern is clinically distinct from dental or TMJ pathology (which is worse at rest or with specific jaw movement) and must raise the probability of a large vessel inflammatory process affecting the extracranial circulation. At least four diagnoses remain plausible at this stage.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: For the past 8 weeks she has experienced aching and morning stiffness in both shoulders and upper arms lasting over an hour, which she attributed to 'old age'; she has no prior headache history, no visual symptoms to date; she takes ramipril for hypertension.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral proximal limb girdle aching with prolonged morning stiffness is the clinical hallmark of polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) — which occurs in up to 50% of GCA patients and shares the same granulomatous vasculitic pathophysiology. PMR in isolation does not cause headache or jaw claudication, but its co-occurrence with new temporal headache and exertional jaw pain raises the probability of GCA substantially. A structural intracranial cause (SOL) would not explain the shoulder girdle symptoms. Space-occupying lesion and inflammatory arthritis remain in the differential pending examination and investigation.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.7°C; blood pressure 148/84 mmHg; palpation of both temporal arteries reveals a firm, thickened, nodular vessel on the right with markedly reduced pulsation; scalp tenderness on right temporal region; bilateral shoulder girdle tenderness with mildly restricted active abduction; fundoscopy: disc margins sharp, no papilloedema, no pallor.
prompts.reasoning: A thickened, nodular temporal artery with reduced pulsation and overlying scalp tenderness is the classic physical finding of GCA — transmural granulomatous inflammation causes arterial wall thickening, luminal narrowing, and tenderness. Normal fundoscopy (no pallor or disc swelling) indicates the optic nerve supply is currently intact — anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION) from posterior ciliary artery occlusion is the most feared complication. SOL and simple inflammatory arthritis are excluded by the vascular examination finding. Primary CNS angiitis and Takayasu arteritis (predominantly affects the aorta and its branches in younger patients) remain possible but less likely in this age group and presentation.

prompts.label: Blood Tests
prompts.text: ESR 96 mm/hour (markedly elevated); CRP 74 mg/L (markedly elevated); full blood count: normochromic normocytic anaemia (Hb 99 g/L), thrombocytosis (platelets 512 × 10⁹/L); alkaline phosphatase mildly elevated; renal function normal; ANA and ANCA: negative.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated ESR and CRP reflect active systemic inflammation — both are present in virtually all active GCA cases and are essential diagnostic markers. Normochromic normocytic anaemia of chronic inflammation is typical. Thrombocytosis is a reactive response to systemic inflammation. Negative ANCA effectively excludes ANCA-associated vasculitides (granulomatosis with polyangiitis, eosinophilic GPA, microscopic polyangiitis). Negative ANA reduces the probability of SLE-related vasculitis. The combination of markedly elevated acute phase reactants with temporal artery abnormality in an elderly woman with PMR features and jaw claudication makes GCA the strongly leading diagnosis. Biopsy is required for confirmation.

prompts.label: Temporal Artery Biopsy & Treatment
prompts.text: Temporal artery biopsy: transmural granulomatous inflammation with multinucleate giant cells at the intima-media junction; fragmentation and disruption of the internal elastic lamina; CD4+ lymphocyte and macrophage infiltrate; intimal hyperplasia causing luminal narrowing; skip lesions noted — multiple sections examined; high-dose prednisolone 60 mg/day commenced immediately prior to biopsy result.
prompts.reasoning: The histopathological findings are diagnostic of GCA: granulomatous transmural arteritis with giant cells, disruption of the internal elastic lamina (which triggers the granulomatous response — elastin acts as the antigen for CD4+ T cells), and intimal hyperplasia causing vascular occlusion. Skip lesions (normal segments between inflamed segments) necessitate examination of multiple sections across a long biopsy specimen (>1 cm). GCA is a medical emergency when visual symptoms are present — high-dose corticosteroids must be started immediately on clinical suspicion, before biopsy, as they reduce risk of blindness but do not affect biopsy yield within 2 weeks. Long-term steroid tapering over 1–2 years is standard. Tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor antagonist) is now licensed as a steroid-sparing agent in GCA.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 26-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of daily headaches and episodes of brief visual disturbance affecting both eyes.
prompts.reasoning: Daily headache with binocular visual disturbances in a young woman requires urgent evaluation for raised intracranial pressure. Space-occupying lesion, cerebral venous thrombosis (a dangerous cause in women of reproductive age), and idiopathic intracranial hypertension all produce this combination. Migraine with aura can cause visual symptoms preceding headache. Hypertensive encephalopathy must be excluded. The binocular nature of the visual symptoms (rather than monocular) suggests a retrochiasmal or optic disc cause rather than ocular pathology.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headaches are worse on waking and when coughing or bending forward; the visual episodes last only seconds, occur up to 10 times per day, and are described as a grey dimming affecting both eyes simultaneously before quickly clearing.
prompts.reasoning: Morning-dominant headache worsened by Valsalva-type manoeuvres (coughing, bending) indicates raised ICP — supine posture reduces venous drainage and elevates CSF pressure, explaining the morning predominance. Transient visual obscurations (TVOs) — brief, bilateral grey-outs lasting seconds, occurring multiple times per day — are caused by transient impairment of the optic nerve blood supply from papilloedema compressing the peripapillary capillaries during transient spikes in ICP. This triad (morning headache, worsened by Valsalva, bilateral TVOs) strongly suggests raised ICP from any cause. Migraine aura typically lasts 20–60 minutes and spreads gradually. At least three diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has no prior neurological history; she is not taking the oral contraceptive pill, tetracyclines, or vitamin A/retinoid supplements; she has no history of ear infections or mastoiditis; she has gained 14 kg over the past 18 months; no family history of intracranial pathology.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of OCP, tetracyclines (doxycycline), and vitamin A/retinoids reduces the probability of secondary (drug-induced) intracranial hypertension — these agents are the most common pharmacological causes. No otitis media or mastoiditis reduces the probability of lateral sinus thrombosis causing secondary raised ICP. Recent significant weight gain (14 kg) is an important risk factor for idiopathic intracranial hypertension — the mechanism involves increased intra-abdominal pressure reducing CSF drainage, and possible adipokine-mediated effects on CSF secretion. CVT and SOL cannot be excluded without imaging.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BMI 38 kg/m²; blood pressure 118/72 mmHg; fundoscopy: bilateral papilloedema with blurred disc margins and peripapillary flame haemorrhages; visual acuity intact bilaterally (6/6); visual field testing shows enlarged blind spots bilaterally; no focal neurological deficit; no sixth nerve palsy.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral papilloedema confirms chronically raised ICP — it is a clinical sign, not a diagnosis. Papilloedema with peripapillary haemorrhages indicates significant and sustained elevation of ICP with axoplasmic flow stasis in the optic nerve. Enlarged blind spots (on visual field testing) correspond to the swollen optic disc margins displacing the peripapillary retina. Visual acuity is initially preserved in papilloedema (unlike optic neuritis, which causes central vision loss early). Normal blood pressure excludes hypertensive encephalopathy. The cause of raised ICP — IIH, CVT, or SOL — requires imaging to differentiate.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & MR Venography
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: no mass lesion, no hydrocephalus, no parenchymal abnormality; an empty sella turcica (CSF partially filling the pituitary fossa); bilateral optic nerve sheaths distended; MR venography: no cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; both transverse sinuses show mild symmetrical narrowing.
prompts.reasoning: Normal MRI brain excludes space-occupying lesion, hydrocephalus, and parenchymal disease as the cause of raised ICP. Normal MRV with no venous sinus thrombosis excludes CVT. The MRI features of IIH are present: empty sella (CSF herniating into pituitary fossa under elevated pressure), distended optic nerve sheaths (CSF pressure transmitted along the subarachnoid sleeve surrounding the optic nerves), and bilateral transverse sinus narrowing (which may both cause and result from IIH, creating a positive feedback loop). Lumbar puncture is now required to measure opening pressure and confirm the diagnosis — the CSF composition should be normal in IIH (to distinguish from meningitis or other inflammatory causes of raised ICP).

prompts.label: Lumbar Puncture & Management
prompts.text: Lumbar puncture in lateral decubitus position: opening pressure 34 cmH₂O (markedly elevated; normal <20 cmH₂O); CSF clear, colourless; protein 0.38 g/L (normal); glucose normal; white cell count 2 cells/µL (normal); 25 mL CSF removed — immediate partial headache relief; acetazolamide 500 mg twice daily commenced; ophthalmology referral for ongoing visual field monitoring.
prompts.reasoning: The diagnostic criteria for IIH are fulfilled: (1) papilloedema, (2) normal neurological examination except CN VI palsy, (3) normal MRI brain and MRV, (4) elevated CSF opening pressure (>25 cmH₂O in adults), and (5) normal CSF composition. Opening pressure of 34 cmH₂O is markedly elevated. Normal CSF composition distinguishes IIH from infective or inflammatory meningitis causing raised ICP. CSF removal provides immediate temporary relief by reducing ICP acutely. Acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) reduces CSF production by the choroid plexus. Weight loss is critical — 5–10% body weight reduction can induce remission. Refractory cases: optic nerve sheath fenestration (to protect vision) or CSF diversion (VP/LP shunt).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 44-year-old woman presents with headaches occurring on more than 20 days of the past month.
prompts.reasoning: Headache occurring on ≥15 days per month for ≥3 months defines chronic daily headache — an umbrella encompassing chronic migraine, chronic tension-type headache, medication overuse headache (MOH), new daily persistent headache, and secondary causes. A daily headache pattern requires systematic exclusion of secondary causes (raised ICP, SOL, CVT, hypertension) before attributing the pattern to a primary disorder. MOH is particularly common in this setting and must be actively sought by quantifying analgesic use.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headaches vary in character — sometimes severe and throbbing, sometimes a dull bilateral pressure — and are typically present on waking, partially relieved by analgesia, but return within a few hours.
prompts.reasoning: The variable and shifting headache phenotype — migrainous on some days, tension-type on others — is characteristic of MOH, where the headache loses its original discrete character and becomes featureless, pervasive, and difficult to classify. Morning-onset headache that is partially and temporarily relieved by analgesia but recurs within hours describes the 'rebound' or withdrawal headache cycle. Pure chronic migraine or tension-type headache typically maintains a consistent phenotype over time. New daily persistent headache has an acute, memorable onset date. Raised ICP is excluded by the variable, non-progressive pattern and absence of associated features — but examination is still required.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has a 15-year history of episodic migraine that was occurring 3 to 4 times per month before the current pattern developed; she takes triptans and over-the-counter ibuprofen/paracetamol combination tablets for headache relief on most days, which she says she cannot manage without.
prompts.reasoning: A prior episodic primary headache disorder (migraine) that has transformed into a daily pattern over months, coinciding with escalating analgesic and triptan use, is the classical MOH trajectory. Episodic migraine is the most common underlying primary headache in MOH — the transformation from episodic to chronic migraine is closely correlated with increasing analgesic frequency. The psychological dependency on medication ('cannot manage without') reflects the neuroadaptive changes of MOH. ICHD-3 MOH thresholds: triptans ≥10 days/month, or simple analgesics (NSAIDs, paracetamol) ≥15 days/month, or combination analgesics ≥10 days/month, for >3 months.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg; neurological examination: entirely normal; fundoscopy: sharp disc margins, no papilloedema; no meningism; no focal deficit; 4-week prospective headache diary confirms triptans used on 11 days and combination analgesics (paracetamol/ibuprofen) used on 8 days — total 19 medication use days in the 4-week diary period.
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination and no papilloedema exclude raised intracranial pressure, effectively ruling out SOL and IIH. The prospective headache diary objectively quantifies medication overuse: triptans on 11 days (threshold ≥10/month met) plus combination analgesics on 8 days — total of 19 medication use days. This formally meets ICHD-3 criteria for MOH: regular use of triptans ≥10 days/month for >3 months in the context of chronic daily headache. Chronic migraine alone (without overuse) would require the headache to meet migraine criteria on ≥8 days/month — possible here, but the overuse pattern takes diagnostic precedence and must be addressed first.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Medication History
prompts.text: MRI brain: normal — no structural pathology, no venous sinus abnormality; detailed medication history confirms triptan use escalated from 2 to 3 days/month 2 years ago to current daily use, correlating precisely with the increase in headache frequency over the same period.
prompts.reasoning: Normal MRI brain excludes all structural secondary causes of chronic daily headache. The temporal correlation between escalating triptan use and increasing headache frequency is the key discriminating feature of MOH versus chronic migraine without overuse. Neuroimaging is performed to exclude secondary causes in any patient with a change in headache pattern or new chronic daily headache. The mechanistic basis of MOH involves central sensitisation — chronic exposure to analgesics downregulates descending pain inhibitory pathways (serotonergic and noradrenergic) and upregulates pronociceptive pathways, leading to increased headache frequency and reduced analgesic effectiveness. MOH is not simply psychological dependency — it has a clear neurobiological substrate.

prompts.label: Withdrawal Trial & Outcome
prompts.text: A supervised withdrawal of all acute headache medications is undertaken over 4 weeks — headaches worsen significantly in the first 10 days (withdrawal period) then progressively reduce; at 6 weeks, headache frequency has fallen to 5 days/month; the residual headaches are exclusively migrainous in character (unilateral, pulsating, photophobic); diagnosis confirmed: medication overuse headache superimposed on underlying episodic migraine; topiramate initiated as migraine prophylaxis.
prompts.reasoning: The clinical response to medication withdrawal is the diagnostic confirmation of MOH — a reduction to near-baseline headache frequency after a withdrawal period is required to establish MOH as the diagnosis. Chronic migraine without overuse would not revert to low-frequency episodic pattern on medication withdrawal alone. The transient worsening during weeks 1–2 represents the neurobiological withdrawal syndrome (downregulated pain inhibitory pathways are unmasked). Rebound to the original underlying episodic migraine pattern confirms the primary diagnosis is migraine with superimposed MOH. Prophylaxis (topiramate, amitriptyline, propranolol, or CGRP monoclonal antibodies) addresses the underlying migraine. MOH will recur if analgesic overuse resumes.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 4-day history of progressive bilateral leg weakness and increasing difficulty walking.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive bilateral leg weakness developing over days requires urgent evaluation to distinguish peripheral from central causes. Spinal cord lesions (transverse myelitis, cord compression) cause UMN signs and a sensory level. Cauda equina syndrome causes LMN signs and bowel/bladder involvement. GBS (ascending polyradiculoneuropathy) causes progressive ascending LMN weakness. Botulism (toxin-mediated NMJ blockade) causes descending weakness. MS can present with acute spinal or multifocal lesions. Functional neurological disorder must be excluded. The rate of progression over days and the distribution of weakness are key discriminating axes.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The weakness started in both feet symmetrically and has ascended to involve her calves and now her thighs; she reports tingling and numbness in both feet but denies any back pain, urinary symptoms, or bowel disturbance.
prompts.reasoning: Symmetric ascending weakness with distal sensory symptoms and absent bladder/bowel disturbance narrows the differential. Absence of back pain and bladder involvement reduces the probability of cauda equina syndrome (which typically presents with urinary retention, saddle anaesthesia, and back pain). Botulism causes descending (cranial to caudal) weakness. The ascending pattern (distal to proximal, bilateral, symmetric) is characteristic of GBS. Transverse myelitis, MS, and cord compression remain — they may produce bilateral leg weakness without early bladder involvement, and sensory symptoms are present in all. Examination for UMN versus LMN signs will be the critical discriminating step.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Three weeks ago she had a 5-day illness with watery diarrhoea that resolved without treatment; she has no prior neurological diagnoses, takes no medications, and has received no vaccinations in the past 2 months; no family history of neuromuscular disease.
prompts.reasoning: A preceding gastrointestinal infection 2–4 weeks before onset of neurological symptoms is the classic post-infectious trigger pattern for GBS — the latency reflects the time required for an adaptive immune response to develop and then attack peripheral nerve components via molecular mimicry (antigens on the infectious organism share epitopes with peripheral nerve myelin or gangliosides). Post-infectious transverse myelitis is also possible (viral myelitis). MS relapses may be triggered by infections. However, the progressive ascending bilateral course over days (rather than stable or relapsing-remitting) and absence of prior neurological events favour GBS over MS. Physical examination will be the critical next step.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Lower limbs: bilateral symmetric flaccid weakness (MRC grade 3/5 legs, 2/5 feet); absent ankle and knee reflexes bilaterally; plantar responses absent; reduced pinprick and vibration sense in both feet distally; upper limb power 4/5 proximally with sluggish biceps reflexes; respiratory rate 18/min; SpO2 97% on air.
prompts.reasoning: The examination reveals a pure LMN pattern: flaccid weakness, areflexia, and sensory loss — without UMN signs (no hyperreflexia, no extensor plantar responses, no sensory level on the trunk). This effectively excludes cord compression and most cases of transverse myelitis (which characteristically produces a sensory level, early bladder dysfunction, and UMN signs — though the acute shock phase of TM can transiently produce flaccidity and areflexia). Upper limb areflexia indicates the process has ascended above L1. Respiratory monitoring (FVC) is critical — diaphragmatic involvement occurs as weakness ascends to cervical roots. Investigation of the peripheral nervous system is now required.

prompts.label: MRI Spine & Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: MRI brain and full spine with gadolinium: no cord signal abnormality, no enhancing cord lesion, no disc compression; nerve conduction studies: bilateral prolonged distal motor latencies, markedly reduced motor conduction velocities in lower limbs, absent F-waves, and conduction block at multiple sites.
prompts.reasoning: Normal MRI spine definitively excludes transverse myelitis (which shows cord T2 signal change spanning ≥1 vertebral level) and cord compression. Nerve conduction studies demonstrating prolonged distal latencies, reduced conduction velocities, absent F-waves, and conduction block are diagnostic of the demyelinating subtype of GBS (AIDP — acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy). F-wave absence indicates involvement of the most proximal nerve segments (nerve roots) — a key feature of GBS. In the axonal subtypes (AMAN, AMSAN), conduction velocity is preserved but amplitude is reduced. CSF examination will now confirm the diagnosis.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & Treatment
prompts.text: Lumbar puncture: CSF clear; protein 3.2 g/L (markedly elevated; normal <0.45 g/L); white cell count 3 cells/µL (normal); glucose normal — markedly elevated protein with a normal cell count; FVC 1.7 L (58% predicted — below 20 mL/kg threshold); IVIg 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days commenced; transferred to ICU for respiratory and autonomic monitoring.
prompts.reasoning: Albuminocytological dissociation — markedly elevated CSF protein with a normal or near-normal white cell count — is the pathognomonic CSF finding of GBS. It reflects leakage of plasma proteins across the inflamed blood-nerve barrier of the demyelinated nerve roots, while the CSF cell count remains low (inflammation is in the nerve roots, not the CSF space). Note: in the first week of GBS the protein may not yet be elevated. FVC <20 mL/kg indicates impending respiratory failure — intubation threshold is FVC <15–20 mL/kg. IVIg (0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days) or plasma exchange are equally effective first-line treatments. Steroids alone are not effective in GBS. Autonomic monitoring is critical — arrhythmias and blood pressure instability cause 3–5% mortality.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 57-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive numbness and tingling in both feet.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral progressive distal sensory symptoms represent the prototypical presentation of peripheral neuropathy — the common final pathway of many systemic and metabolic disorders. Diabetes mellitus is the single most common cause of peripheral neuropathy worldwide. Other important metabolic causes include B12 deficiency (especially in vegetarians, metformin users, and the elderly), alcohol-related neuropathy, hypothyroidism, and chronic kidney disease. CIDP and hereditary neuropathy (Charcot-Marie-Tooth) are structural/inflammatory causes. A detailed history will identify the most likely aetiology.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The sensory symptoms are symmetrical in both feet and soles, worst at night with a burning quality that disturbs his sleep, and have gradually ascended to just below the knees over 6 months; there is no weakness and no hand involvement.
prompts.reasoning: A symmetrical, length-dependent, ascending sensory neuropathy with neuropathic pain (burning, nocturnal — small fibre involvement) describes the classic 'glove-and-stocking' distribution. Length-dependent neuropathy reflects dying-back axonal degeneration — the longest axons (supplying the feet) are affected first. Neuropathic pain (burning, dysaesthesiae) indicates small unmyelinated C-fibre and thinly myelinated A-delta fibre involvement, seen prominently in diabetic and alcoholic neuropathy. Absence of weakness and hand involvement at this stage does not discriminate between aetiologies. At least five diagnoses remain plausible — metabolic workup and risk factor assessment are required.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has no known medical diagnoses; he does not drink alcohol; he follows a strict plant-based vegan diet without any vitamin supplements for the past 7 years; he has gained 10 kg over the past 4 years and reports increased thirst and frequent urination over the past 6 months.
prompts.reasoning: Polydipsia and polyuria over 6 months (coinciding with the neuropathy onset) suggest undiagnosed diabetes mellitus — the most common cause of peripheral neuropathy and a classic occult presentation in middle-aged men. A strict vegan diet for 7 years without B12 supplementation places him at significant risk of B12 deficiency — B12 is found exclusively in animal products and body stores deplete over 2–5 years of dietary restriction. Alcoholic neuropathy is excluded by confirmed abstinence. Uraemia requires renal function testing. Hypothyroid neuropathy remains possible and requires TSH measurement. At least three diagnoses remain plausible at this stage.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BMI 30 kg/m²; blood pressure 138/86 mmHg; vibration sense absent at the great toes and medial malleoli bilaterally; pinprick sensation reduced in a stocking distribution to mid-shin; ankle reflexes absent bilaterally; knee reflexes present; proprioception mildly impaired at the toes; power 5/5 throughout; no ataxia; no upper limb involvement.
prompts.reasoning: The examination confirms a length-dependent sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy (predominantly sensory at this stage) affecting the longest fibres first. Absent ankle reflexes reflect loss of afferent Ia sensory fibres (part of the reflex arc). The pattern — symmetrical, distal, predominantly sensory with preserved motor function — is consistent with any metabolic neuropathy (diabetic, B12, hypothyroid, uraemic). Absent ankle reflexes with present knee reflexes indicate involvement extending to the ankle level — a length-dependent gradient. No upper limb findings and no gait ataxia mean long-tract or cord involvement (as seen in subacute combined degeneration of the cord from B12 deficiency) is not yet evident. Blood tests are the key next investigation.

prompts.label: Blood Tests
prompts.text: Fasting plasma glucose 10.2 mmol/L; HbA1c 78 mmol/mol (9.3%); serum B12 228 pmol/L (borderline low-normal; laboratory reference range 180–900); TSH: normal; eGFR: 74 mL/min (mildly reduced, not in uraemic range); FBC: no macrocytosis (MCV 84 fL); LFTs: normal.
prompts.reasoning: A markedly elevated HbA1c (78 mmol/mol) confirms undiagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus — the patient was unaware of his diagnosis. Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is the most common complication of diabetes, affecting 50% of patients with a duration of 10+ years, but is also present at diagnosis in many patients with longstanding undetected hyperglycaemia. Borderline-low serum B12 (228 pmol/L) in a strict vegan without supplementation raises genuine concern for functional B12 deficiency — serum B12 levels can be misleadingly normal despite tissue deficiency. TSH normal excludes hypothyroid neuropathy. Normal eGFR excludes uraemic neuropathy. Further functional testing for B12 status is required.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies & B12 Functional Testing
prompts.text: Nerve conduction studies: symmetrically reduced sensory nerve action potential amplitudes in the sural nerves bilaterally; mildly reduced motor conduction velocities — axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy pattern; methylmalonic acid and homocysteine: both within normal limits (excluding functional B12 deficiency); diagnosis: diabetic peripheral neuropathy; metformin and dietary modifications commenced, B12 supplementation also initiated empirically given dietary restriction.
prompts.reasoning: An axonal polyneuropathy pattern on NCS (reduced amplitude without significant slowing) is characteristic of metabolic neuropathies including diabetic neuropathy — in contrast to demyelinating neuropathies (GBS, CIDP) which show markedly reduced conduction velocity and prolonged distal latencies. Normal methylmalonic acid (MMA) and homocysteine — which are more sensitive markers of functional B12 deficiency than serum B12 alone — exclude clinically significant B12 neuropathy. Elevated MMA and homocysteine accumulate when B12-dependent enzymatic reactions are impaired (MMA is substrate for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase; homocysteine for methionine synthase). The diagnosis is diabetic peripheral neuropathy in the context of newly diagnosed type 2 DM. Glycaemic control is the primary treatment — tight glucose control slows progression.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 29-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of intermittent drooping of one eyelid and occasional double vision.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral ptosis with diplopia in a young woman has a broad differential. Oculomotor nerve palsy (CN III) causes ptosis with pupillary dilation and restricted extraocular movements. Horner syndrome causes partial ptosis with miosis (never diplopia from the ptosis itself). Thyroid eye disease (Graves' orbitopathy) can cause lid retraction or restriction of extraocular muscles. Orbital myositis causes painful ophthalmoplegia. MS can cause internuclear ophthalmoplegia or CN VI palsy. Myasthenia gravis and Lambert-Eaton are NMJ disorders. The variability of symptoms across the day will be the key discriminating axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She notices the ptosis and diplopia are typically absent or minimal in the morning, progressively worsening throughout the day with sustained activity, and she also reports difficulty chewing through a full meal, with jaw fatigue developing towards the end of eating.
prompts.reasoning: Fatigability — symptoms that are absent or minimal at rest and worsen with sustained or repetitive activity — is the defining characteristic of neuromuscular junction disorders. CN III palsy, Horner syndrome, and thyroid eye disease produce fixed deficits that do not fluctuate in this pattern. Mitochondrial myopathy (e.g., CPEO — chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia) causes slowly progressive, fixed external ophthalmoplegia without the day-to-day variability or rapid fatigability of MG. Jaw fatigue with prolonged chewing suggests bulbar (cranial nerve) muscle involvement beyond the orbit, pointing to a generalised NMJ or myopathic process rather than a purely ocular structural lesion. At least four diagnoses remain plausible after P2.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Over the past month she has noticed increasing difficulty climbing stairs and rising from low chairs; she has no pain, no sensory symptoms, no family history of neuromuscular disease, and no history of autoimmune conditions; she is not taking any medications.
prompts.reasoning: Proximal limb weakness (stairs, rising from chairs) in addition to ocular and bulbar fatigability indicates generalised disease beyond pure ocular myasthenia. Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) characteristically affects proximal limbs (pre-synaptic VGCC autoantibodies) but rarely causes significant ocular or bulbar involvement — the prominent ocular and bulbar features here favour post-synaptic MG over LEMS. Mitochondrial myopathy can affect both ocular and proximal limb muscles. Absence of sensory symptoms excludes peripheral neuropathy. Absence of pain makes inflammatory myopathy less likely. Absence of myotonia (no grip difficulty) reduces the probability of myotonic dystrophy.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral asymmetric ptosis (right > left); diplopia on upgaze and right lateral gaze; sustained upgaze for 60 seconds causes progressive worsening of ptosis (fatigable on testing); pupillary responses equal and reactive bilaterally; facial muscle strength normal at rest; proximal limb power 4/5 bilaterally; deep tendon reflexes normal throughout; no fasciculations.
prompts.reasoning: Fatigable ptosis on sustained upgaze (worsening ptosis with maintained upward gaze) is a characteristic examination finding of myasthenia gravis, demonstrating the failure of ACh-mediated neuromuscular transmission when the NMJ is repeatedly activated. Pupil-sparing is important: CN III palsy with pupil involvement suggests posterior communicating artery aneurysm; pupil-sparing CN III palsy is typically ischaemic. Normal deep tendon reflexes argue strongly against LEMS (in which reflexes are typically reduced or absent at rest, improving with brief exercise — the Lambert sign). Mitochondrial myopathy (CPEO) causes slowly progressive, non-fatigable ophthalmoplegia without this rapid test-induced worsening. Neurophysiology and antibody testing are now required.

prompts.label: Neurophysiology
prompts.text: Repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz (facial and hypothenar muscles): >15% decrement in compound muscle action potential amplitude by the 4th to 5th stimulus — a significant decremental response; single-fibre EMG of the orbicularis oculi: markedly increased jitter (>55 µs in >10% of fibre pairs) — abnormal neuromuscular transmission variability.
prompts.reasoning: A decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation (>10% decrement at 3 Hz) reflects failure of neuromuscular transmission at successive stimuli — as ACh vesicles are repeatedly released and the margin of safety for end-plate potential generation is reduced, a proportion of muscle fibres fail to reach threshold. This is the hallmark of post-synaptic NMJ dysfunction (MG). LEMS shows the opposite — an incremental response at high-frequency stimulation (3–5-fold increase in amplitude at 20–50 Hz). Single-fibre EMG (SFEMG) is the most sensitive test for MG — increased jitter (variability in neuromuscular transmission timing) is the earliest and most sensitive indicator of NMJ dysfunction. Mitochondrial myopathy shows no NMJ abnormality on neurophysiology.

prompts.label: Serology & Imaging
prompts.text: Anti-acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies: strongly positive (titre 28 nmol/L; normal <0.4 nmol/L); anti-MuSK antibodies: not tested (AChR positive); CT chest: well-defined anterior mediastinal soft tissue mass measuring 3.8 cm — thymoma suspected; pyridostigmine 60 mg four times daily commenced with significant symptomatic improvement; thymectomy planned.
prompts.reasoning: Anti-AChR antibodies are present in 85% of patients with generalised MG (and 50% with purely ocular MG) — they are highly specific (>99% specificity) and confirm the post-synaptic autoimmune mechanism. Anti-AChR IgG antibodies bind to the alpha subunit of the nicotinic ACh receptor at the motor end-plate, leading to (1) complement-mediated destruction, (2) antigenic modulation (receptor internalisation), and (3) direct blockade of the ACh binding site — all reducing end-plate potential amplitude. Thymoma is associated with MG in 10–15% of cases; the anterior mediastinal mass requires surgical resection (thymectomy improves MG in both thymoma and hyperplastic thymus cases). Pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) reduces ACh breakdown, increasing end-plate potential amplitude — symptomatic treatment. Immunosuppression (prednisolone, azathioprine) for long-term control.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 46-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of progressive difficulty rising from chairs and climbing stairs.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive difficulty with activities requiring proximal lower limb strength (rising from a chair, climbing stairs) is the hallmark presentation of proximal myopathy. The differential is broad: inflammatory myopathies (polymyositis, dermatomyositis, inclusion body myositis), endocrine causes (hypothyroid, Cushing syndrome), drug-induced (statins, steroids), and neuromuscular junction disorders (LEMS). Careful characterisation of the weakness pattern, associated symptoms, and risk factors will allow progressive discrimination.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The weakness is symmetrical and affects both the upper limbs (she can no longer raise her arms above her head to wash her hair) and the lower limbs; she has no muscle pain at rest, no sensory symptoms, no ptosis, and no fatigable weakness.
prompts.reasoning: Symmetric proximal weakness in both upper and lower limb girdles without sensory symptoms or fatiguability narrows the differential toward myopathic rather than neuropathic or NMJ causes. No ptosis and no fatiguability argue against myasthenia gravis and LEMS (which cause fatigable weakness on examination). Absence of sensory symptoms excludes peripheral neuropathy. Inclusion body myositis (IBM) characteristically affects finger flexors and knee extensors asymmetrically — the symmetric shoulder girdle weakness here is less consistent with IBM. At least five diagnoses remain plausible at this stage.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has been taking atorvastatin 40 mg/day for 3 years for hypercholesterolaemia without prior muscle symptoms; she was treated for hypothyroidism 1 year ago and her most recent TSH was in the normal range 3 months ago; no family history of muscle disease and no known autoimmune conditions.
prompts.reasoning: Statin therapy is one of the most common causes of proximal myopathy — statins (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors) cause myopathy via mitochondrial dysfunction, reduced ubiquinone synthesis, and occasionally immune-mediated necrotising myopathy (anti-HMGCR antibodies). Three years of statin use without prior symptoms does not exclude statin myopathy — it can develop at any time, including after years of therapy. Previously treated hypothyroidism with recent normal TSH reduces the probability of hypothyroid myopathy but does not eliminate it (TSH can be normal while muscle damage persists). An inflammatory myopathy (PM, DM) must be actively excluded by measuring muscle enzymes, EMG, and ultimately muscle biopsy.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Symmetric proximal weakness: upper limb girdle 3/5, lower limb girdle 3/5; distal strength preserved (5/5 throughout); deep tendon reflexes intact; no fasciculations; no ptosis; skin examination reveals a faint pinkish-violet discolouration around both eyes and eyelids, and flat, rough, violaceous papules overlying the knuckles of both hands.
accepted: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms (DRESS)
prompts.reasoning: Periocular violaceous discolouration (heliotrope-coloured periorbital oedema) and roughened violaceous papules over the knuckles are characteristic cutaneous findings of dermatomyositis, not seen in polymyositis or statin myopathy. However, drug reactions (such as DRESS from atorvastatin) can cause cutaneous eruptions with systemic features, and a drug-induced myopathy with a coincidental skin reaction cannot be excluded without further testing. The skin findings narrow the differential substantially — statin-induced myopathy alone would not produce these specific skin changes, but the statin must still be stopped and the effect on CK and symptoms monitored before an inflammatory diagnosis is confirmed. A skin rash in dermatomyositis precedes or accompanies the myopathy.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & EMG
prompts.text: Serum CK: 4,600 U/L (markedly elevated; normal <200 U/L); LDH and aldolase elevated; TSH: normal; atorvastatin discontinued — repeat CK at 4 weeks: 4,100 U/L (persistently elevated); ANA positive 1:320; anti-dsDNA: negative; EMG: short-duration, polyphasic, low-amplitude motor unit potentials with early full recruitment and fibrillation potentials — myopathic pattern.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated CK (4,600 U/L) reflects active muscle fibre destruction — the degree of elevation is consistent with inflammatory myopathy. Persistent elevation of CK 4 weeks after statin withdrawal effectively excludes statin-induced myopathy as the primary cause — statin myopathy (without immune-mediated necrotising myopathy) normalises within weeks of stopping the statin. Normal TSH excludes hypothyroid myopathy. EMG showing a myopathic pattern (short-duration, polyphasic, low-amplitude MUPs with early recruitment) confirms primary muscle disease rather than a NMJ disorder or neuropathy. Positive ANA supports an autoimmune aetiology. The cutaneous findings and persistent myopathy together make dermatomyositis the leading diagnosis. Muscle biopsy is required for confirmation and to distinguish DM from PM.

prompts.label: Muscle Biopsy & Myositis Antibody Panel
prompts.text: Muscle biopsy (right deltoid): perifascicular atrophy with small, atrophic fibres at the periphery of muscle fascicles; perivascular and perimysial CD4+ lymphocytic infiltrate; membrane attack complex (C5b-9) deposition on capillaries; anti-Mi-2 antibodies positive; CT thorax/abdomen/pelvis: no occult malignancy; diagnosis: dermatomyositis; prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day commenced with methotrexate as steroid-sparing agent.
prompts.reasoning: The muscle biopsy features are pathognomonic of dermatomyositis: perifascicular atrophy (selective atrophy of muscle fibres at the periphery of fascicles — caused by ischaemia from microangiopathy) and MAC deposition on muscle capillaries (complement-mediated microvasculopathy). This distinguishes DM from polymyositis (which shows endomysial, predominantly CD8+ cytotoxic lymphocyte infiltrate around individual non-necrotic fibres, without perifascicular atrophy or vascular MAC deposition). Anti-Mi-2 antibodies are specific for classic dermatomyositis with prominent skin features and good treatment response. Malignancy screening is essential in adult dermatomyositis — dermatomyositis carries a 15–25% risk of underlying malignancy (particularly ovarian, lung, breast cancer). Management: high-dose corticosteroids; steroid-sparing immunosuppressants (methotrexate, azathioprine); sun protection for the skin.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 53-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of foot drop on the right side that developed over 2 to 3 days.
prompts.reasoning: Right foot drop developing subacutely over days encompasses a broad differential. Common peroneal nerve palsy (at the fibular head) is a common compressive mononeuropathy causing foot drop. L4/L5 radiculopathy from disc prolapse can cause identical motor deficits. Ischaemic stroke affecting the motor cortex can produce contralateral foot drop (UMN pattern). A peripheral neuropathy rarely presents with sudden unilateral foot drop — length-dependent neuropathy causes bilateral symmetric symptoms. Mononeuritis multiplex requires evidence of involvement of more than one peripheral nerve territory.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He now mentions that 4 weeks before the right foot drop developed, he noticed weakness and reduced sensation in his left forearm and hand — particularly difficulty extending his wrist — that began subacutely over 2 days and has only partially recovered; there was no preceding neck pain, back pain, or trauma.
prompts.reasoning: Sequential involvement of two anatomically separate peripheral nerve territories — the left radial/posterior interosseous nerve (wrist drop) followed by the right common peroneal nerve (foot drop) — occurring weeks apart and developing subacutely over days in each case defines mononeuritis multiplex. This pattern of asymmetric, sequential, multifocal peripheral nerve lesions cannot be explained by a single spinal lesion or peripheral neuropathy. MS can cause multifocal lesions but these would be in the central nervous system (producing UMN signs). Multifocal motor neuropathy is purely motor with no sensory loss. The subacute onset and partial recovery of the first lesion suggests a vasculitic or inflammatory mechanism. At least four diagnoses remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 80 mmol/mol, poorly controlled); hypertension; he has also noticed a 5 kg unintentional weight loss over the past 3 months, night sweats, and reduced appetite; no alcohol, no drug use.
prompts.reasoning: Diabetes mellitus is a recognised cause of mononeuritis multiplex (diabetic radiculoplexus neuropathy), but poorly controlled diabetes alone would typically cause a length-dependent sensorimotor polyneuropathy or cranial mononeuropathy rather than sequential asymmetric limb nerve lesions at distant sites. The constitutional features — unintentional weight loss, night sweats, and reduced appetite — in a middle-aged man raise the probability of an underlying systemic process: either malignancy (paraneoplastic neuropathy) or systemic vasculitis. Both can present with mononeuritis multiplex. Vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex occurs in systemic vasculitis (ANCA-associated, rheumatoid, PAN) — nerve ischaemia from vasculitis of the vasa nervorum causes acute, painful, step-wise nerve lesions.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right common peroneal territory: foot drop with absent ankle dorsiflexion and eversion; sensory loss over right dorsal foot; left radial nerve territory: wrist drop, absent finger extension, sensory loss over the left dorsal hand and anatomical snuffbox; reflexes: absent right ankle jerk; no upper motor neuron signs anywhere; no sensory level.
prompts.reasoning: The examination confirms two discrete peripheral nerve lesions in anatomically non-contiguous territories: right common peroneal nerve (foot drop + dorsal foot sensory loss) and left radial nerve (wrist drop + dorsal hand sensory loss). Purely peripheral nerve (LMN) deficits — no UMN signs, no sensory level — exclude cord and central causes (MS, stroke). Sensory involvement in both territories excludes multifocal motor neuropathy (which is purely motor). The pattern is now definitively mononeuritis multiplex — the aetiology (vasculitic vs. paraneoplastic vs. diabetic) requires investigation. Blood tests, inflammatory markers, ANCA, and CT staging are the key next investigations.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & CT Staging
prompts.text: ESR 82 mm/hour; CRP 61 mg/L; MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA): strongly positive; PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA): negative; rheumatoid factor: weakly positive; complement: normal; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no primary malignancy, no lymphadenopathy; chest X-ray: small bilateral pleural effusions.
prompts.reasoning: Strongly positive MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA) is associated with microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) — both ANCA-associated vasculitides that cause systemic small vessel vasculitis affecting the vasa nervorum (small arteries supplying peripheral nerves). Absence of malignancy on CT staging effectively excludes paraneoplastic neuropathy as the cause. Bilateral pleural effusions suggest systemic involvement consistent with a vasculitic process. The combination of ANCA positivity, elevated inflammatory markers, mononeuritis multiplex pattern, and constitutional symptoms is highly consistent with an ANCA-associated vasculitis. Sural nerve biopsy is the definitive investigation.

prompts.label: Sural Nerve Biopsy & Diagnosis
prompts.text: Sural nerve biopsy: epineural and endoneurial vasculitis with fibrinoid necrosis of small vessel walls and transmural neutrophilic infiltrate; asymmetric axonal degeneration across nerve fascicles (reflecting ischaemic infarction of individual fascicles); haemosiderin-laden macrophages in the perineurium; diagnosis: vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex (microscopic polyangiitis); high-dose prednisolone and cyclophosphamide commenced.
prompts.reasoning: Nerve biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosing vasculitic neuropathy — it demonstrates the pathological substrate: necrotising vasculitis of the vasa nervorum (epineural arterioles and endoneurial capillaries), causing ischaemic infarction of individual nerve fascicles. The asymmetric fascicular involvement on cross-section is a key distinguishing feature of vasculitic (ischaemic) neuropathy versus length-dependent neuropathy. Haemosiderin (iron from previous haemorrhage into the nerve) indicates prior ischaemic events. Microscopic polyangiitis (MPO-ANCA positive) is characterised by pauci-immune necrotising small vessel vasculitis without granulomas. Treatment: high-dose glucocorticoids for induction, followed by cyclophosphamide or rituximab for refractory disease; azathioprine or mycophenolate for maintenance. Nerve recovery is partial — axonal regeneration is slow.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 37-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe right shoulder pain that began overnight.
prompts.reasoning: Acute severe shoulder pain in a middle-aged man has a broad differential encompassing mechanical (rotator cuff tear, dislocation, AC joint), inflammatory (septic arthritis, crystal arthropathy), infectious (herpes zoster, which can cause severe prodromal pain before vesicle eruption), and neurological causes (cervical radiculopathy, brachial neuritis). The mode of onset (overnight, without trauma), severity, and character of pain will be key for the first discriminating step.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The pain is constant, excruciating, burning, and neuralgic in character, radiating from the shoulder to the upper arm; no position relieves it; there was no preceding trauma, and the pain began improving spontaneously after 3 to 4 days, but on day 5 he noticed weakness developing in the right arm.
prompts.reasoning: Non-mechanical, constant burning neuralgic pain without positional relief argues against a purely musculoskeletal cause (mechanical pain is typically worsened by specific movements and relieved by rest or offloading). The spontaneous onset without trauma reduces the probability of rotator cuff tear or dislocation. The sequential pattern — severe pain lasting days followed by improvement in pain concurrent with the onset of weakness — is the characteristic clinical signature of brachial neuritis (Parsonage-Turner syndrome): the initial inflammatory phase causes intense pain, and as the acute inflammation subsides, neurological damage (axonal degeneration) becomes manifest as weakness. Herpes zoster must be excluded by careful skin examination. Cervical radiculopathy is exacerbated by neck movement and position.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He had a mild viral upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks before the pain onset; he has had no prior shoulder or neurological problems; no recent vaccinations; no history of diabetes, malignancy, or autoimmune disease; no alcohol use; no family history of similar episodes.
prompts.reasoning: A preceding viral illness 2 weeks before onset is a recognised precipitant for brachial neuritis — an immune-mediated inflammatory demyelination and axonal injury of the brachial plexus triggered by infections, vaccinations, surgery, or occurring spontaneously. The post-infectious latency (2 weeks) reflects the time for adaptive immune activation to target peripheral nerve antigens. Cervical radiculopathy does not typically have a post-infectious pattern and is exacerbated by neck movement. Herpes zoster is excluded by the absence of a skin rash — with a 2-week history, vesicular eruption would be expected by now if zoster were the cause. At least three diagnoses remain plausible pending examination.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right shoulder: full passive range of movement (no joint restriction, no capsular tenderness); right arm: weakness of shoulder abduction (3/5), shoulder external rotation (3/5), and elbow flexion (4/5); wrist extension and finger movements fully preserved (5/5); biceps reflex reduced right; sensation mildly reduced over the right lateral arm; no skin vesicles; no Horner syndrome.
prompts.reasoning: Weakness of shoulder abduction (deltoid — axillary nerve/C5), external rotation (infraspinatus — suprascapular nerve/C5), and elbow flexion (biceps — musculocutaneous nerve/C5-C6), with preserved wrist and finger extension, localises to a C5-C6 distribution. Full passive shoulder range of motion excludes an intrinsic joint or rotator cuff lesion — if the shoulder joint were pathological, passive movement would also be restricted. The same distribution could arise from C5/C6 disc prolapse (cervical radiculopathy) or injury to the upper trunk of the brachial plexus. Absence of vesicles excludes herpes zoster. MRI cervical spine and neurophysiology are required to distinguish between these.

prompts.label: MRI Cervical Spine & Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: MRI cervical spine: no disc prolapse, no foraminal stenosis at C5/C6, no cord signal change; nerve conduction studies: normal median and ulnar motor and sensory studies; EMG of right deltoid, supraspinatus, and infraspinatus: fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves indicating acute denervation; reduced motor unit recruitment in right deltoid and biceps.
prompts.reasoning: Normal MRI cervical spine definitively excludes C5/C6 cervical radiculopathy from disc prolapse or foraminal stenosis — the most important structural differential. EMG findings of acute denervation (fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves develop 2–3 weeks after axonal injury) affecting multiple muscles supplied by different peripheral nerves (deltoid — axillary nerve; supraspinatus/infraspinatus — suprascapular nerve) but all arising from the C5/C6 brachial plexus distribution indicates a multifocal plexopathy rather than a single root lesion.
