Low synovial glucose reflects bacterial consumption. The findings confirm septic arthritis requiring urgent surgical washout.

prompts.label: Culture and Confirmation
prompts.text: Synovial fluid culture and blood cultures grow methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) in both samples. The patient undergoes arthroscopic joint washout and is started on intravenous flucloxacillin. At six-week follow-up, the joint has recovered with preserved range of motion.
prompts.reasoning: Isolation of S. aureus from synovial fluid and blood cultures confirms the diagnosis and identifies the causative organism. S. aureus is the most common cause of septic arthritis in adults, and intra-articular injection is a recognised portal of entry for skin-colonising S. aureus. Bacteraemia confirms haematogenous seeding or direct inoculation with systemic dissemination. The combination of acute monoarticular septic presentation after an intra-articular injection, high synovial fluid neutrophilia with no crystals, Gram-positive cocci, and positive culture confirms Septic Arthritis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 62-year-old man with diabetes mellitus presents with a non-healing wound on the sole of his right foot that has been present for six weeks.
accepted: Peripheral Arterial Disease with Ischaemic Ulceration
prompts.reasoning: A non-healing foot ulcer in a diabetic patient is one of the most common and serious presentations in clinical medicine. Osteomyelitis complicating a diabetic foot ulcer, an uninfected neuropathic ulcer, cellulitis, deep abscess, and ischaemic ulceration from peripheral arterial disease are all plausible. The discriminatory axis is the chronicity (six weeks) and the diabetic context. Any non-healing diabetic foot wound must be evaluated for underlying bone involvement.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The wound developed from a blister caused by ill-fitting footwear; it has been treated with dressings by his community nurse but has shown no sign of healing and appears to be enlarging. He reports no severe pain, which he attributes to always having had reduced foot sensation.
accepted: Peripheral Arterial Disease with Ischaemic Ulceration
prompts.reasoning: A foot blister progressing to a non-healing wound in a diabetic patient with reduced foot sensation (diabetic peripheral neuropathy) is the classic mechanism for a neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer. The absence of pain despite an enlarging wound reflects sensory neuropathy — a key risk factor that allows wounds to progress unnoticed. An uninfected neuropathic ulcer, infected ulcer with cellulitis, ulcer with underlying osteomyelitis, and ischaemic ulceration all remain plausible. Six weeks without healing and gradual enlargement raises the probability of deep infection or bony involvement. Four diagnoses remain.

prompts.label: Past Medical and Social History
prompts.text: He has had type 2 diabetes for 22 years with an HbA1c of 10.1%, peripheral neuropathy confirmed on nerve conduction studies, and peripheral arterial disease with an ankle-brachial pressure index of 0.7. He has had two prior foot ulcers in the last four years, both of which healed with aggressive wound care.
accepted: Peripheral Arterial Disease with Ischaemic Ulceration
prompts.reasoning: Longstanding poorly controlled diabetes with confirmed peripheral neuropathy and peripheral arterial disease (ABPI 0.7 indicating mild-moderate arterial compromise) creates a high-risk substrate for both neuropathic and ischaemic ulceration. The history of recurrent ulcers with full healing argues that the current non-healing is a change from his baseline, increasing concern for a complicating process such as osteomyelitis. Cellulitis is now less likely — the absence of spreading erythema and the chronicity of six weeks argues against primary cellulitis. Three diagnoses remain; clinical examination and probing of the wound are essential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: A 2.5 cm deep, punched-out ulcer with sloughy base and surrounding callus is present on the plantar surface of the right first metatarsal head. Surrounding erythema extends approximately 1.5 cm beyond the wound edge. Probing the wound with a sterile metal probe meets hard resistance at 1.8 cm depth. Foot pulses: dorsalis pedis absent, posterior tibial faint. Temperature sensation and monofilament testing: absent bilaterally.
accepted: Diabetic Foot Ulcer (Non-infected with Exposed Bone)
prompts.reasoning: Probing to bone with a sterile metal probe through the wound base — the 'probe-to-bone' test — is the key bedside clinical finding raising high suspicion for osteomyelitis. In a diabetic foot ulcer, a positive probe-to-bone test has a positive predictive value of approximately 89% for underlying osteomyelitis. The surrounding erythema within 2 cm of the ulcer edge is consistent with local periulcer infection but does not confirm deep bone involvement without imaging. Absent foot pulses confirm peripheral arterial disease contributing to impaired healing. Cellulitis extending beyond the wound is limited; deep abscess would produce fluctuance. Two diagnoses remain, requiring imaging to confirm bony involvement.

prompts.label: MRI of the Right Foot
prompts.text: MRI of the right foot (with gadolinium): T1-hypointense signal replacing the normal bright fatty marrow of the right first metatarsal head with corresponding T2-hyperintensity and post-gadolinium enhancement, extending into the metatarsal shaft. Cortical irregularity at the metatarsal head. A sinus tract connects the wound base to the metatarsal head.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is the gold-standard imaging investigation for osteomyelitis, with a sensitivity of approximately 90% and specificity of 82%. T1 marrow signal loss with T2 hyperintensity and gadolinium enhancement represents oedema and hyperaemia within infected bone, replacing normal fatty marrow. Cortical irregularity confirms destructive osteitis. The sinus tract connecting the ulcer to the bone surface confirms contiguous spread of infection from the skin wound to the bone. An uninfected neuropathic ulcer with exposed bone would not show marrow signal changes on MRI. The MRI confirms osteomyelitis.

prompts.label: Bone Biopsy and Microbiological Confirmation
prompts.text: Percutaneous CT-guided bone biopsy of the right first metatarsal is performed under fluoroscopic guidance. Histology shows necrotic bone trabeculae with an acute and chronic inflammatory infiltrate and surrounding fibrous tissue. Tissue culture grows Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus agalactiae. Wound swab from the surface grows a different polymicrobial flora, confirming that surface swab culture does not reliably reflect bone pathogens.
prompts.reasoning: Histology showing necrotic bone with acute and chronic inflammatory infiltrate is the pathological definition of osteomyelitis. Bone tissue culture — not wound swab — is the microbiological gold standard, as surface wound swabs reflect superficial colonisers rather than the true bone pathogen. The discordance between wound swab flora and bone culture organisms confirms the clinical principle that tissue culture must guide antibiotic selection. Staphylococcus aureus as the primary bone isolate is consistent with the most common causative organism in both haematogenous and contiguous osteomyelitis. These findings confirm Osteomyelitis (contiguous, diabetic foot type), requiring prolonged targeted antibiotic therapy and surgical debridement planning.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 54-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of fever and worsening cough.
prompts.reasoning: Acute onset fever with productive cough in an adult establishes a lower respiratory tract process. The differential at this stage is broad and includes bacterial pneumonia (lobar or bronchopneumonia), atypical pneumonia, TB, lung abscess, acute bronchitis, and viral pneumonia including COVID-19. No discriminatory axis beyond 'febrile respiratory illness' has been introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The cough is productive of rusty-brown sputum, associated with right-sided pleuritic chest pain and a single rigor on the first day. He has no haemoptysis and no weight loss.
prompts.reasoning: Pleuritic chest pain and a single rigor are consistent with an acute bacterial process involving the pleura. Rusty-brown sputum raises the probability of bacterial pneumonia but is not pathognomonic. TB is less likely given the acute onset (2 days), absence of haemoptysis, and no weight loss. Atypical pneumonia and COVID-19 remain plausible. Lung abscess can produce pleuritic pain if it involves the pleural surface.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has no known lung disease, is a non-smoker, and reports no recent hospitalisation or aspiration events. He works in an office and has had no known sick contacts.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of hospitalisation or aspiration significantly reduces the likelihood of lung abscess (which is strongly associated with aspiration or immunosuppression). No smoking history lowers concern for underlying structural lung disease. Lobar pneumonia from community-acquired pathogens, atypical pneumonia, and COVID-19 remain the leading considerations.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.2°C, HR 108 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 26 breaths/min, SpO2 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals bronchial breath sounds and dullness to percussion over the right lower zone. Vocal resonance is increased in the same area.
prompts.reasoning: Bronchial breath sounds, dullness to percussion, and increased vocal resonance localised to the right lower zone are classical signs of consolidation, consistent with lobar pneumonia. Atypical pneumonia can produce consolidation on examination, though the signs are classically less pronounced. COVID-19 becomes less likely as the predominant pattern here is consolidation with lobar signs rather than a bilateral diffuse process. A chest radiograph is required to confirm the distribution and extent.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates dense homogeneous opacification of the right lower lobe with a visible air bronchogram. No pleural effusion. No hilar lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Homogeneous right lower lobe consolidation with an air bronchogram on CXR confirms airspace disease consistent with lobar pneumonia. Air bronchograms indicate that the bronchi remain patent while surrounding alveoli are filled with exudate, which is characteristic of lobar bacterial pneumonia. Atypical pneumonia typically produces patchy bilateral interstitial infiltrates rather than lobar consolidation. The overall picture — acute onset, fever, pleuritic pain, rusty sputum, lobar consolidation — is consistent with community-acquired lobar pneumonia.

prompts.label: Microbiological Results
prompts.text: Sputum Gram stain shows Gram-positive diplococci in pairs and chains. Blood cultures grow an alpha-haemolytic organism. Urine pneumococcal antigen test is positive.
prompts.reasoning: Gram-positive diplococci on sputum Gram stain, combined with a positive urinary pneumococcal antigen, confirms Streptococcus pneumoniae as the causative organism. This establishes the diagnosis as pneumococcal lobar pneumonia. Blood culture positivity indicates bacteraemia, consistent with the severity of the presentation. The diagnosis is confirmed: community-acquired lobar pneumonia due to S. pneumoniae.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 67-year-old man presents to his family physician with a 6-week history of cough and progressive fatigue.
prompts.reasoning: A subacute 6-week cough with fatigue in an elderly man requires broad consideration. The prolonged time course distinguishes this from acute infection. Lung cancer, TB, COPD exacerbation, atypical pneumonia, bronchiectasis, and cardiac causes all need to be considered at this stage. The single discriminatory axis introduced is the subacute duration.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The cough is productive of small amounts of yellowish sputum. He has had unintentional weight loss of approximately 5 kg over the past 2 months and drenching night sweats on most nights.
prompts.reasoning: Constitutional symptoms — weight loss and night sweats — in combination with a subacute productive cough significantly raise concern for TB or malignancy. These systemic features are less consistent with COPD exacerbation (where systemic features are not a cardinal feature) or heart failure. Bronchiectasis can produce similar symptoms but weight loss and night sweats would be less typical without active infection. Both TB and lung cancer remain strongly in the differential.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He was born in Malaysia and migrated to Singapore 30 years ago. He has no history of prior TB treatment. He has type 2 diabetes, and lives in a one-room flat. He does not smoke.
prompts.reasoning: Origin from a high-TB-prevalence country, diabetes (an established TB risk factor), and lower socioeconomic housing conditions all elevate the pre-test probability of TB. However, lung cancer remains in the differential — it can produce identical systemic symptoms and is common in this age group. Non-smoking history lowers (but does not exclude) lung cancer risk. Atypical pneumonia is less likely over a 6-week course without antibiotic treatment response.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.8°C, HR 92 bpm, BMI 18.2 kg/m². Reduced breath sounds in the right upper zone. Dullness to percussion at the right apex. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No finger clubbing.
prompts.reasoning: Upper zone signs — dullness and reduced breath sounds at the right apex — are anatomically characteristic of TB reactivation, which favours the well-oxygenated apices. Lung cancer can also produce apical or upper lobe signs, especially with a Pancoast tumour, though the absence of clubbing or Horner syndrome reduces this probability. Atypical pneumonia is effectively excluded by the apical localisation and 6-week course. A chest X-ray is critical to differentiate the two remaining diagnoses.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray shows a heterogeneous opacity in the right upper lobe with irregular margins and a central area of lucency. No hilar lymphadenopathy. Left lung is clear.
prompts.reasoning: Right upper lobe opacity with central lucency raises strong suspicion for cavitation, which is characteristic of post-primary (reactivation) TB. However, primary lung cancer — particularly squamous cell carcinoma — can also cavitate and present in the upper lobe. Both diagnoses remain possible at this stage, and sputum investigations or bronchoscopy are essential to distinguish them.

prompts.label: Sputum Investigations
prompts.text: Sputum acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smear: positive (3+ bacilli). Sputum culture subsequently confirms Mycobacterium tuberculosis sensitive to first-line agents. Mantoux tuberculin skin test: 18 mm induration.
prompts.reasoning: Positive AFB smear and culture confirmation of Mycobacterium tuberculosis establish the definitive diagnosis of active pulmonary tuberculosis. The 3+ smear positivity indicates high bacillary load and infectivity. Strongly positive Mantoux test supports prior sensitisation. Lung cancer is excluded as the cause of the radiological findings. This is reactivation (secondary) pulmonary TB given the apical location, cavitation, and absence of primary complex features.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 19-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of breathlessness and chest tightness.
prompts.reasoning: Acute-onset breathlessness with chest tightness in a young woman has a broad differential. Asthma is common in young adults but pneumothorax (especially in tall thin individuals), pulmonary embolism, acute bronchitis, anaphylaxis, and panic disorder must all be considered. The single axis introduced is acute dyspnoea with chest tightness; no additional features have been provided.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The breathlessness is associated with audible wheeze, which she says has happened twice before in the past year. Both previous episodes resolved spontaneously. She has no chest pain and no leg swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent episodic wheeze that resolves spontaneously is characteristic of a condition with reversible airflow obstruction. Pneumothorax and PE are less likely to be recurrent and self-resolving. Absence of chest pain and leg swelling further lowers PE probability. Asthma and recurrent bronchospasm remain the top considerations. Anaphylaxis is possible if an allergen trigger is identified. Panic disorder typically does not produce wheeze.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has a personal history of eczema and allergic rhinitis. Her mother has asthma. Tonight's episode began approximately 30 minutes after she visited a friend who owns a cat.
prompts.reasoning: A personal history of eczema and allergic rhinitis (the atopic triad) combined with a family history of asthma strongly supports an atopic constitution. A clear allergen trigger (cat dander) with wheeze onset shortly after exposure is consistent with atopic asthma. Anaphylaxis remains possible given allergen exposure, though the absence of urticaria, angioedema, or cardiovascular compromise makes it less likely. Acute bronchitis is effectively excluded by the atopic context and rapid allergen-related onset.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: RR 28 breaths/min, SpO2 91% on room air, HR 110 bpm. She is visibly distressed with accessory muscle use. Bilateral expiratory wheeze is audible throughout all lung fields. Percussion is resonant. No urticaria, no facial swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral diffuse expiratory wheeze with accessory muscle use and oxygen desaturation confirms significant airflow obstruction. Resonant percussion excludes pneumothorax (which would produce hyper-resonance) and consolidation. Absence of urticaria and facial swelling effectively rules out anaphylaxis as the primary diagnosis. The clinical picture — atopic history, allergen trigger, bilateral wheeze, accessory muscle use — is diagnostic of an acute asthma exacerbation. The severity (SpO2 91%, accessory muscles, RR 28) indicates a moderate-to-severe attack.

prompts.label: Spirometry and Peak Flow
prompts.text: Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) measured at triage: 42% of predicted. Following administration of salbutamol via nebuliser, PEFR improves to 78% of predicted within 20 minutes.
prompts.reasoning: A PEFR of 42% of predicted indicates severe obstruction. The >15% improvement (36 percentage points) following short-acting bronchodilator administration confirms significant reversible airflow obstruction, which is the defining physiological feature of asthma. This degree of bronchodilator responsiveness is not characteristic of COPD (where reversibility is typically incomplete) and distinguishes asthma from fixed airway narrowing.

prompts.label: Formal Lung Function Testing (Outpatient)
prompts.text: Spirometry performed one week later (after resolution of the acute episode): FEV1/FVC ratio 0.68 (below lower limit of normal). Post-bronchodilator FEV1 increases by 19% from baseline. Serum total IgE elevated at 420 IU/mL. Skin-prick test positive for cat dander and house dust mite.
prompts.reasoning: Formal spirometry confirms obstructive pattern with significant post-bronchodilator reversibility (≥12% and 200 mL), consistent with asthma. Elevated total IgE and positive skin-prick tests to aeroallergens confirm an atopic, IgE-mediated (allergic) mechanism. The complete clinical picture — episodic reversible wheeze, atopic constitution, allergen trigger, bronchodilator reversibility, elevated IgE — confirms the diagnosis of atopic (allergic) asthma.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 62-year-old man presents to his general practitioner complaining of increasing difficulty breathing over the past year.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive dyspnoea over one year in an older male has a broad differential including COPD, heart failure, IPF, TB, lung cancer, and systemic causes such as anaemia. The single axis at this stage is progressive exertional dyspnoea without further characterisation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He reports a chronic daily cough productive of white sputum for the past 3 years, worse in the mornings. He has also had two episodes in the past year where his breathlessness worsened significantly and he required antibiotics.
prompts.reasoning: A daily productive cough lasting more than 3 months per year for at least 2 consecutive years meets the clinical definition of chronic bronchitis, a key component of COPD. Two infective exacerbations requiring antibiotics are consistent with COPD or bronchiectasis. Heart failure can produce chronic cough but it is typically non-productive. TB and lung cancer are less likely given 3 years of stable symptoms without haemoptysis or weight loss.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has smoked 20 cigarettes per day for 40 years (40 pack-years). He is a retired construction worker. He has no prior diagnosis of lung disease and takes no regular medications.
prompts.reasoning: A 40 pack-year smoking history is the single most important risk factor for COPD and strongly shifts the differential in that direction. Lung cancer must remain in the background given heavy smoking, but the chronic productive cough pattern without haemoptysis or weight loss is more consistent with COPD. Bronchiectasis is less typically driven by smoking alone. Heart failure is less likely with no known cardiac history.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: RR 20 breaths/min, SpO2 94% on room air, HR 84 bpm. Barrel-shaped chest. Percussion is hyper-resonant bilaterally. Breath sounds are globally reduced with a prolonged expiratory phase. Mild bilateral expiratory wheeze. No ankle oedema. No clubbing.
prompts.reasoning: Barrel-shaped chest, bilateral hyper-resonance, reduced breath sounds with prolonged expiratory phase, and expiratory wheeze are all consistent with airflow obstruction with air trapping — the hallmarks of COPD with emphysema. Absence of ankle oedema makes right heart failure less likely currently. Bronchiectasis would more typically produce coarse crackles from retained secretions rather than hyper-resonance. The constellation of signs points clearly to obstructive lung disease in the context of heavy smoking.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral hyperinflation with flattening of both hemidiaphragms, increased retrosternal airspace, and attenuation of peripheral vascular markings. No focal consolidation. No pleural effusion.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and attenuated peripheral vessels on CXR reflects air trapping and parenchymal destruction consistent with emphysema. These findings together with the clinical picture confirm obstructive lung disease. No consolidation excludes an acute infective process. No focal mass makes lung cancer less likely, though CT would be required for full exclusion given the smoking history.

prompts.label: Spirometry
prompts.text: Spirometry: FEV1 1.4 L (45% predicted), FVC 3.1 L (78% predicted), FEV1/FVC ratio 0.45. Post-bronchodilator FEV1 increases by 6% (80 mL), which does not meet reversibility criteria. DLCO is reduced at 52% predicted.
prompts.reasoning: Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms persistent airflow limitation, which is the spirometric definition of COPD (GOLD criteria). The lack of significant reversibility (<12% and <200 mL) distinguishes COPD from asthma. Reduced DLCO reflects loss of alveolar surface area from emphysema. The severity classification (FEV1 45% predicted) corresponds to GOLD Stage III (severe). The complete picture confirms COPD with emphysema as the primary diagnosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset left-sided chest pain.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden-onset unilateral chest pain in a young man requires urgent consideration of pneumothorax, PE, pericarditis, musculoskeletal causes, and aortic dissection. The acute onset and unilateral nature narrow the differential somewhat, but no discriminatory axis beyond laterality and acuity has been introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The pain is sharp and worsens on deep inspiration. It came on while he was sitting quietly at home and is associated with mild breathlessness. There is no cough, no fever, and no palpitations.
prompts.reasoning: Pleuritic pain (sharp, worsens on inspiration) that began at rest with associated breathlessness narrows the differential to conditions involving the pleura or lung. Aortic dissection and pericarditis are less likely with purely pleuritic pain at rest and no tearing quality. Absence of fever makes acute pleuritis from infection less likely. Pneumothorax, PE, and musculoskeletal pain all remain plausible.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has no known medical conditions and takes no medications. He does not smoke. He is 186 cm tall and weighs 68 kg.
prompts.reasoning: A tall, thin, young male with no prior medical history or smoking fits the classic demographic for spontaneous primary pneumothorax (apical bleb rupture). PE is less likely without risk factors (immobilisation, surgery, OCP, known thrombophilia), though it cannot be excluded on history alone. Musculoskeletal pain is possible but has no specific trigger here.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: HR 102 bpm, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 22 breaths/min, SpO2 95% on room air. Trachea is central. Reduced breath sounds over the left hemithorax. Percussion is hyper-resonant on the left. Chest wall is non-tender. No leg swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral hyper-resonance with reduced breath sounds and a central trachea is the hallmark triad of a pneumothorax without tension. A deviated trachea would indicate tension pneumothorax, which is not present here. Musculoskeletal pain would not produce percussion or auscultatory changes. PE would not produce hyper-resonance. The absence of leg swelling further reduces PE probability. A chest X-ray is required to confirm and quantify the pneumothorax.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Erect chest X-ray demonstrates a visible pleural edge in the left upper zone with complete absence of lung markings peripheral to it. Estimated left pneumothorax rim >2 cm. The mediastinum is central. No pleural effusion.
prompts.reasoning: A visible pleural line with absent peripheral lung markings confirms pneumothorax. A rim >2 cm indicates a large pneumothorax requiring intervention. Central mediastinum confirms the absence of tension. Combined with the clinical findings — tall young male, spontaneous onset, hyper-resonance, reduced breath sounds — this confirms primary spontaneous pneumothorax, consistent with rupture of a subpleural bleb at the lung apex.

prompts.label: CT Thorax
prompts.text: CT thorax (performed after aspiration): small subpleural bullae are identified at the left lung apex. No underlying parenchymal disease. No contralateral bullae. Diagnosis confirmed as primary spontaneous pneumothorax.
prompts.reasoning: CT thorax confirms the presence of apical subpleural bullae, establishing the underlying structural cause of primary spontaneous pneumothorax. Absence of underlying parenchymal lung disease (e.g. emphysema, TB, malignancy) confirms this is primary rather than secondary. The clinical management and recurrence risk counselling (approximately 50% recurrence rate) can now be guided by the CT findings.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 68-year-old man is referred to a respiratory physician with a one-year history of gradually worsening breathlessness on exertion.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive exertional dyspnoea over one year in an elderly man has a broad differential. IPF, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, COPD, heart failure, sarcoidosis, and lung cancer all present with this pattern. The single discriminatory axis is progressive dyspnoea without further characterisation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He also has a dry, persistent cough that has not responded to antitussive treatment. He has no wheezing, no haemoptysis, no fever, and no ankle swelling.
prompts.reasoning: A persistent dry cough unresponsive to treatment, combined with progressive dyspnoea without wheeze, fever, or cardiac symptoms, points away from COPD, heart failure, and infection toward an interstitial lung process. IPF, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and sarcoidosis all fit this profile. Lung cancer can produce dry cough and dyspnoea and remains in the differential.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He is a retired shipyard worker with a 20 pack-year smoking history (quit 15 years ago). He has no pets and no exposure to mouldy hay or organic dust. He has no known autoimmune disease and takes no medications known to cause pulmonary toxicity.
prompts.reasoning: Shipyard work raises concern for asbestos exposure, which can cause asbestosis — a form of restrictive lung disease that clinically resembles IPF. However, the absence of organic antigen exposure (no birds, no mouldy hay) reduces the likelihood of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. No autoimmune disease excludes connective tissue disease-associated ILD. No drug exposure reduces drug-induced ILD probability. IPF, sarcoidosis, and asbestosis remain the leading considerations.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 90% on room air, desaturating to 84% on minimal exertion. Fine bibasal end-inspiratory crackles (Velcro-like) heard on auscultation. Bilateral digital clubbing. No peripheral lymphadenopathy. No skin rash or joint swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Fine bibasal Velcro crackles and digital clubbing are characteristic findings in fibrotic interstitial lung disease, particularly IPF. Clubbing is common in IPF but uncommon in sarcoidosis and hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Absence of lymphadenopathy makes sarcoidosis less likely. Absence of autoimmune features (rash, arthritis) excludes connective tissue disease-related ILD. The two leading diagnoses now are IPF and hypersensitivity pneumonitis (which can also cause fibrosis), distinguishable primarily by exposure history and HRCT pattern.

prompts.label: HRCT Thorax
prompts.text: HRCT thorax shows bilateral subpleural and basal-predominant reticular opacities, honeycombing, and traction bronchiectasis. Upper lobes are relatively spared. No ground-glass attenuation. No lymphadenopathy. Pattern is classified as 'typical UIP'.
prompts.reasoning: A typical UIP (Usual Interstitial Pneumonia) pattern on HRCT — subpleural, basal-predominant honeycombing with traction bronchiectasis and no significant ground-glass opacity — is highly specific for IPF when combined with the clinical context (age >60, male, no identifiable cause). Hypersensitivity pneumonitis characteristically shows upper/mid-zone predominance, centrilobular nodules, and ground-glass opacity, which are absent here. A typical UIP pattern on HRCT in the appropriate clinical context is sufficient for diagnosis without surgical biopsy per current guidelines.

prompts.label: Pulmonary Function Tests and Serology
prompts.text: Spirometry: FVC 58% predicted, FEV1 61% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.84 (restrictive pattern). DLCO 38% predicted. ANA, anti-dScDNA, anti-Ro, anti-La, anti-Jo-1 all negative. Serum precipitins for avian proteins and Aspergillus: negative.
prompts.reasoning: Restrictive spirometry with severely reduced DLCO confirms the interstitial fibrotic process. Negative autoimmune serology excludes connective tissue disease-associated ILD. Negative serum precipitins against avian antigens and Aspergillus, combined with the absence of organic antigen exposure on history, definitively excludes hypersensitivity pneumonitis. All secondary causes of pulmonary fibrosis having been excluded, the diagnosis is confirmed as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset breathlessness and right-sided chest pain that began 2 hours ago.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden-onset dyspnoea and unilateral chest pain in a young woman requires urgent assessment. The acute onset favours vascular or structural causes over infective ones. PE, pneumothorax, pleuritis, pneumonia, musculoskeletal pain, and aortic dissection are all plausible at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The chest pain is pleuritic. She has no cough, no fever, and no haemoptysis. She noticed that her right calf has been tender and slightly swollen for the past 3 days, which she had attributed to a muscle strain.
prompts.reasoning: Right calf tenderness and swelling preceding the onset of pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea strongly suggests deep vein thrombosis with pulmonary embolism. Pleuritis and pneumonia can also produce pleuritic pain but would not explain leg swelling. Pneumothorax is less likely given no spontaneous risk factors. The Wells score is significantly elevated. Full risk factor history is needed to confirm the pre-test probability.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She is currently taking the combined oral contraceptive pill and returned from a 14-hour flight from Europe 5 days ago. She has no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism. She is a non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: Combined OCP use and prolonged immobility from long-haul air travel are two concurrent Virchow's triad risk factors (hypercoagulability and stasis), both of which significantly increase DVT/PE risk. This combination makes PE highly probable. Pleuritis is still possible as a less serious explanation for the pleuritic pain, though it would not account for the calf swelling or risk factors. An urgent D-dimer and CTPA are indicated.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: HR 112 bpm, BP 108/70 mmHg, RR 24 breaths/min, SpO2 92% on room air, temperature 37.1°C. Right calf is swollen, warm, and tender to palpation. Homan's sign is positive. Chest examination reveals reduced breath sounds in the right lower zone. No pleural rub audible.
prompts.reasoning: Clinical evidence of right DVT (calf swelling, warmth, tenderness) combined with tachycardia, tachypnoea, and hypoxia in the context of two concurrent prothrombotic risk factors constitutes high clinical probability for PE. The Wells score places this patient in the high-probability category (>6 points). Reduced breath sounds in the right lower zone may reflect a pulmonary infarct or associated small effusion. Pleuritis cannot explain the DVT signs and haemodynamic changes.

prompts.label: CT Pulmonary Angiography
prompts.text: CTPA demonstrates a filling defect in the right main pulmonary artery extending into the right lower lobe branches, consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. A small right pleural effusion is noted. No parenchymal consolidation. Right lower lobe peripheral wedge-shaped opacity consistent with pulmonary infarction.
prompts.reasoning: CTPA confirms thrombus in the right pulmonary arterial tree with evidence of pulmonary infarction (peripheral wedge opacity). The haemorrhagic infarct occurs in only approximately 10% of PE cases because of dual pulmonary arterial and bronchial artery supply, but is more likely when peripheral branches are occluded. The diagnosis of acute pulmonary embolism is established. This confirms the peripheral wedge opacity on the right as a pulmonary infarct rather than pneumonia.

prompts.label: D-Dimer, ECG and Troponin
prompts.text: D-dimer: 4.2 µg/mL (markedly elevated). ECG: sinus tachycardia with S1Q3T3 pattern. Troponin I: mildly elevated at 0.08 ng/mL. Echo: right ventricular dilatation with interventricular septal flattening, indicating right heart strain. Diagnosis confirmed as submassive pulmonary embolism.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated D-dimer confirms thrombotic activity. The S1Q3T3 ECG pattern, while non-specific, reflects right heart strain from acute pulmonary vascular obstruction. Mild troponin elevation and echocardiographic evidence of RV dysfunction (RV dilatation, septal flattening) confirm right heart strain, classifying this as submassive PE. The diagnosis of acute pulmonary thromboembolism is confirmed. Anticoagulation and close monitoring for haemodynamic deterioration are required.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old man presents to the acute medical unit with a 5-day history of fever and worsening breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: Fever and progressive dyspnoea over 5 days in a middle-aged man requires broad consideration of respiratory and cardiovascular causes. Infective pneumonias (typical, atypical, viral), TB, heart failure, and PE all fit this timeline. No discriminating feature has been introduced beyond febrile dyspnoea.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He reports a dry cough and myalgia for the first 3 days, and over the past 2 days his breathlessness has rapidly worsened at rest. He has had no haemoptysis, no pleuritic pain, and no diarrhoea.
prompts.reasoning: Initial dry cough and myalgia followed by rapid deterioration in respiratory status over 5 days is a pattern seen in viral pneumonias, including influenza and COVID-19, as well as atypical pneumonia. The dry cough and myalgia prodrome with rapid progression distinguishes this from typical lobar pneumonia, where productive purulent sputum and pleuritic pain are more prominent. TB is less likely with a 5-day course. The rapid deterioration after initial milder illness is a recognised feature of severe viral pneumonia.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has type 2 diabetes and obesity (BMI 34 kg/m²). He lives with his wife, who developed a similar illness 7 days ago. He works as a schoolteacher.
prompts.reasoning: Diabetes and obesity are both recognised risk factors for severe COVID-19 and for influenza pneumonia. A close contact (wife) with a preceding similar illness 7 days earlier is consistent with secondary transmission, fitting the incubation period of SARS-CoV-2 (2–14 days). Occupational exposure in a school setting also raises epidemic risk. Atypical pneumonia (e.g. Mycoplasma) can cause household clusters but less typically causes rapid deterioration in middle-aged adults.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.9°C, HR 116 bpm, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 32 breaths/min, SpO2 82% on room air (improving to 92% on 15 L/min oxygen via non-rebreather mask). Bilateral basal crackles. Percussion is dull bilaterally. No wheeze.
prompts.reasoning: Severe hypoxia (SpO2 82%) with bilateral basal crackles and dullness indicates bilateral consolidation or interstitial involvement. The degree of hypoxia is disproportionate to the clinical appearance (patient is conversational but deeply hypoxic), which is characteristic of 'silent hypoxia' — a recognised but not universal feature of COVID-19 pneumonia. This level of hypoxia is also consistent with severe atypical pneumonia. Bilateral involvement is less typical of classic lobar pneumonia.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray and CT Thorax
prompts.text: Chest X-ray shows bilateral peripheral patchy opacification predominantly in the lower and mid zones. CT thorax demonstrates bilateral peripheral and posterior ground-glass opacities with a 'crazy-paving' pattern in the lower lobes.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities with crazy-paving on CT — while not specific to COVID-19 — are the characteristic radiological hallmark of COVID-19 pneumonia when combined with the clinical context. Atypical pneumonia (e.g. Mycoplasma) produces bilateral interstitial infiltrates but the crazy-paving pattern and lower-lobe posterior predominance are much more characteristic of COVID-19. The clinical triad of contact history, risk factors, and this CT pattern establishes COVID-19 as the leading diagnosis pending PCR confirmation.

prompts.label: Nasopharyngeal PCR and Serology
prompts.text: SARS-CoV-2 nasopharyngeal PCR (RT-PCR): positive. Inflammatory markers: CRP 248 mg/L, ferritin 3,420 ng/mL, D-dimer 3.1 µg/mL, LDH 520 U/L. Lymphocyte count 0.6 × 10⁹/L (lymphopaenia).
prompts.reasoning: Positive SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR confirms the aetiological diagnosis. The inflammatory profile — markedly elevated CRP, ferritin, D-dimer, and LDH — reflects hyperinflammatory state (cytokine storm) characteristic of severe COVID-19. Lymphopaenia is a well-documented feature of COVID-19 resulting from direct lymphocyte destruction and cytokine-mediated sequestration. The diagnosis is confirmed as severe COVID-19 pneumonia with impending ARDS.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 32-year-old woman is referred to a respiratory clinic with a 3-month history of dry cough and mild exertional breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: Dry cough and exertional dyspnoea over 3 months in a young woman admits a broad differential. Sarcoidosis, asthma, atypical pneumonia, TB, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and mediastinal lymphoma all need to be considered. The subacute duration and dry character of the cough are the two discriminatory axes introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She also reports bilateral ankle swelling and tenderness, and painful red nodules on her shins that appeared 2 weeks ago. She has no fever, no night sweats, and no weight loss.
prompts.reasoning: Erythema nodosum (painful red nodules on shins) combined with bilateral ankle arthritis (periarthritis) and respiratory symptoms in a young woman is a classic presentation of Löfgren syndrome, an acute presentation of sarcoidosis. However, erythema nodosum has multiple causes including TB, streptococcal infection, and lymphoma. The absence of fever, night sweats, and weight loss reduces but does not eliminate TB and lymphoma. Asthma and HPP would not explain the skin and joint involvement.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She is of Indian ethnicity, born and raised in Singapore. She has no history of TB, no occupational dust exposure, no pet birds, and no contact with agricultural materials. She does not smoke and takes no medications.
prompts.reasoning: Sarcoidosis has higher prevalence in Indians and Black populations compared to Chinese and SE Asians. Absence of TB risk factors (no known contact, no travel to endemic area, Indian ethnicity in Singapore has moderate TB prevalence) does not eliminate TB but reduces probability somewhat. No organic antigen exposure makes hypersensitivity pneumonitis very unlikely. Lymphoma must remain in the differential as it can cause mediastinal disease, skin infiltration (though not erythema nodosum specifically), and systemic symptoms.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.2°C, SpO2 97% on room air. Multiple tender, indurated red-purple nodules on both shins consistent with erythema nodosum. Bilateral perimalleolar swelling without effusion. Chest examination is unremarkable. Bilateral parotid enlargement noted.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral parotid enlargement in combination with erythema nodosum, bilateral ankle periarthritis, and respiratory symptoms is essentially pathognomonic of sarcoidosis (Löfgren syndrome variant). Bilateral parotid involvement (Heerfordt syndrome component) is not a feature of TB or lymphoma. A clear chest examination with normal SpO2 is consistent with Stage I sarcoidosis (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without parenchymal involvement), which is typically associated with a good prognosis.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates symmetrical bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without pulmonary infiltrates. No pleural effusion. Cardiac silhouette normal.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral symmetrical hilar lymphadenopathy on CXR — the 'potato nodes' appearance — without parenchymal infiltrates is the classic Stage I sarcoidosis radiological pattern. Lymphoma can cause mediastinal lymphadenopathy but is more typically asymmetrical and associated with B symptoms. TB hilar lymphadenopathy is usually unilateral or asymmetrical. The combination of bilateral parotid enlargement, erythema nodosum, ankle periarthritis, and bilateral symmetric hilar lymphadenopathy establishes the diagnosis clinically.

prompts.label: Serum ACE, Calcium and Bronchoscopy
prompts.text: Serum ACE: 98 U/L (elevated, reference <52 U/L). Serum calcium: 2.71 mmol/L (mildly elevated). 24-hour urine calcium: elevated. Bronchoscopic bronchoalveolar lavage: CD4:CD8 ratio 5.2 (elevated). Transbronchial biopsy: non-caseating epithelioid granulomas without central necrosis, no AFBs.
prompts.reasoning: Elevated serum ACE and calcium (from 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production by granuloma macrophages) are consistent with active sarcoidosis. Elevated CD4:CD8 ratio on BAL (>3.5 is characteristic of sarcoidosis) provides additional supporting evidence. Most critically, transbronchial biopsy demonstrating non-caseating epithelioid granulomas without necrosis and negative AFBs confirms the histological diagnosis of sarcoidosis. Caseating granulomas with AFBs would indicate TB; non-caseating granulomas without organisms confirm sarcoidosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 20-year-old university student presents to the student health clinic with a 10-day history of cough and mild fever.
prompts.reasoning: Prolonged cough with mild fever over 10 days in a young adult has a broad differential. Acute bronchitis, viral URTI, atypical pneumonia, lobar pneumonia, and TB all need consideration. The 10-day duration exceeds typical URTI resolution but is shorter than TB typically presents.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The cough is non-productive and he describes it as 'relentless'. He has mild generalised myalgia and headache, but feels well enough to attend classes. He has no pleuritic chest pain and no haemoptysis.
prompts.reasoning: A persistent dry cough with myalgia and headache in a patient who remains ambulatory and functional ('walking pneumonia') is the characteristic presentation of atypical pneumonia, most commonly caused by Mycoplasma pneumoniae. The systemic symptoms — headache and myalgia — are more prominent than in acute bronchitis alone. Absence of pleuritic pain and high fever makes lobar bacterial pneumonia less likely. TB is less probable with only 10 days of symptoms.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He lives in a university hall of residence with shared facilities. Several of his housemates have had similar cough illnesses over the past 3 weeks. He is otherwise healthy, is a non-smoker, and takes no medications.
prompts.reasoning: A cluster of similar respiratory illnesses in a closed community (university hall) is characteristic of Mycoplasma pneumoniae outbreaks, which are well-documented in schools, military barracks, and dormitories. Mycoplasma spreads via respiratory droplets and causes community clusters. Acute bronchitis would not typically cause a cluster with this pattern. Lobar pneumonia rarely clusters. TB can cluster but over longer timeframes.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.8°C, HR 88 bpm, RR 18 breaths/min, SpO2 97% on room air. Chest examination: mild scattered crackles bilaterally in the lower zones. No dullness to percussion. No wheeze. Throat mildly erythematous. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Scattered bilateral lower zone crackles with preserved SpO2 and absent dullness to percussion indicates a bilateral interstitial or early airspace process without frank consolidation. This is consistent with atypical pneumonia. Acute bronchitis can produce crackles but typically without bilateral lower zone changes. Normal percussion excludes frank consolidation. The discordance between significant clinical symptoms and relatively benign examination findings is a recognised hallmark of atypical pneumonia.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray shows bilateral diffuse reticular (interstitial) infiltrates in the lower zones without lobar consolidation. No air bronchograms. No pleural effusion. No hilar lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral interstitial infiltrates without lobar consolidation on CXR — more extensive than expected from the clinical examination — is the classic radiological pattern of atypical pneumonia. This radiological-clinical discordance (greater radiological change than expected from examination) is a hallmark of Mycoplasma and other atypical organisms. Lobar pneumonia would show homogeneous lobar consolidation with air bronchograms. The bilateral interstitial pattern excludes bacterial lobar pneumonia.

prompts.label: Serology and PCR
prompts.text: Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM: positive. Paired IgG serology (4-fold rise between acute and convalescent sera): positive. Nasopharyngeal PCR for Mycoplasma pneumoniae: positive. Cold agglutinin titre: 1:64 (mildly elevated).
prompts.reasoning: Positive Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM and PCR confirm active Mycoplasma infection, establishing the aetiological diagnosis. A 4-fold rise in IgG on paired sera provides confirmatory evidence of acute infection. Cold agglutinins (non-specific autoantibodies) are produced by approximately 50% of Mycoplasma-infected patients and support the diagnosis. The case is confirmed as community-acquired atypical pneumonia due to Mycoplasma pneumoniae.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old man presents to hospital with a 3-week history of progressive breathlessness and dry cough.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute progressive breathlessness and dry cough over 3 weeks in a young man is non-specific at this stage. TB, atypical pneumonia, PCP, IPF, sarcoidosis, and lymphoma are all plausible. The 3-week subacute progression is the sole discriminatory axis introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The breathlessness has worsened significantly over the past week and he notes it is substantially worse on exertion. He has oral thrush that has been present for 2 weeks. He has a low-grade fever but no pleuritic chest pain.
prompts.reasoning: Oral candidiasis (thrush) in a young adult without a known cause (diabetes, antibiotics, steroid use) is a strong marker of T-cell immunodeficiency. In this clinical context, the combination of oral thrush and progressive respiratory symptoms raises urgent concern for an opportunistic respiratory infection, most notably PCP in the setting of undiagnosed HIV. TB and atypical pneumonia can also occur with immunosuppression but oral candidiasis is not a typical feature.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has no known medical conditions and takes no immunosuppressant medications. He has not had any recent antibiotic courses. On directed questioning, he reports unprotected sexual intercourse with male partners and no prior HIV testing.
prompts.reasoning: Sexual history revealing men who have sex with men (MSM) without prior HIV testing is a significant epidemiological factor. In the context of oral candidiasis and progressive respiratory failure, HIV with AIDS-defining illness must be strongly suspected. PCP is the most common AIDS-defining pulmonary illness and typically presents with this subacute progression pattern. TB is also an AIDS-defining illness and remains in the differential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.2°C, HR 118 bpm, RR 28 breaths/min, SpO2 86% on room air. Oral candidiasis confirmed on inspection. Bilateral fine crackles at lung bases. No dullness to percussion. No lymphadenopathy. No skin lesions.
prompts.reasoning: Severe hypoxia (SpO2 86%) with bilateral fine crackles but no percussion changes in a young man with oral candidiasis and high-risk sexual history is highly characteristic of Pneumocystis pneumonia in the setting of advanced HIV infection. PCP classically presents with bilateral interstitial infiltrates without consolidation, producing hypoxia that is disproportionate to the examination findings. TB would more typically produce consolidation or signs of cavitation. No lymphadenopathy or skin lesions reduces concern for lymphoma or Kaposi sarcoma, though these are not excluded.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray and HRCT
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: bilateral symmetrical perihilar ground-glass opacification with relative peripheral sparing. HRCT thorax: bilateral diffuse ground-glass attenuation in a perihilar distribution with a mosaic pattern. No lobar consolidation. No pleural effusion. No lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities with relative peripheral sparing on CXR and HRCT is the characteristic radiological pattern of PCP. TB would produce upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, or hilar lymphadenopathy, none of which are present. The clinical picture — immunosuppressed young man (probable HIV), oral candidiasis, bilateral ground-glass opacities, severe hypoxia without physical consolidation signs — is highly consistent with PCP. HIV testing and bronchoscopic confirmation are required.

prompts.label: HIV Test and Bronchoscopy
prompts.text: HIV antibody/antigen (4th generation) test: positive. CD4 count: 48 cells/µL. Viral load: 420,000 copies/mL. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL): Grocott methenamine silver (GMS) stain demonstrates cyst forms of Pneumocystis jirovecii. No AFBs on BAL.
prompts.reasoning: Positive HIV test, CD4 count of 48 cells/µL (well below the threshold of 200 for PCP prophylaxis), and high viral load confirm advanced AIDS. GMS stain of BAL fluid demonstrating Pneumocystis jirovecii cyst forms is the gold-standard diagnosis for PCP. This confirms the diagnosis as AIDS-defining Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia. Absence of AFBs on BAL makes concurrent TB less likely, though mycobacterial cultures are required given the severe immunosuppression.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old Chinese woman is referred to a respiratory clinic after a 2-month history of worsening breathlessness and non-productive cough.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive breathlessness and non-productive cough over 2 months in a middle-aged woman has a broad differential. Lung cancer, TB, IPF, heart failure, atypical pneumonia, and pleural effusion are all possible. No discriminatory axis beyond subacute dyspnoea and dry cough has been introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has also had right-sided dull aching chest discomfort for 3 weeks. She has lost approximately 4 kg over 2 months and reports no fever, no haemoptysis, and no night sweats.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral dull chest pain with weight loss and progressive dyspnoea over 2 months raises strong concern for malignancy, particularly lung cancer with pleural involvement. TB can also cause pleurisy and constitutional symptoms but night sweats are absent. Heart failure and IPF would not typically cause unilateral localised chest pain. Pleural effusion of any cause could explain the dyspnoea and dull chest aching.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She is a lifelong non-smoker and has no occupational dust exposure. She has no known lung disease and no family history of lung cancer. She has no history of TB or known TB contact.
prompts.reasoning: A non-smoking Chinese woman with progressive respiratory symptoms and weight loss is a clinically important demographic: in Singapore, 30–40% of women with lung cancer are lifelong non-smokers, and adenocarcinoma is the most common subtype in this group (often harbouring EGFR mutations). Lung cancer in non-smokers must be actively considered. TB remains in the differential given its prevalence in Singapore. IPF is less likely at 58 years without the characteristic crackles on examination described so far.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 94% on room air, RR 20 breaths/min. Trachea deviated to the left. Dullness to percussion over the entire right hemithorax. Breath sounds absent over the right lower zone with reduced vocal resonance. No finger clubbing. No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Tracheal deviation toward the opposite side, stony dullness, absent breath sounds, and reduced vocal resonance over the right hemithorax are signs consistent with a large right pleural effusion. Pleural effusions in the context of malignancy (particularly lung cancer) and TB pleuritis both present this way. Tracheal deviation toward the contralateral side indicates significant fluid accumulation causing mediastinal shift. This does not differentiate between malignant and TB-related effusion — chest imaging and fluid analysis are essential.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray and CT Thorax
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: large right pleural effusion with mediastinal shift to the left. CT thorax: large right pleural effusion associated with an irregular spiculated 3.5 cm peripheral mass in the right lower lobe. Multiple enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes. No chest wall invasion.
prompts.reasoning: A spiculated peripheral lung mass with associated malignant-appearing pleural effusion and mediastinal lymphadenopathy is highly characteristic of primary lung malignancy. The spiculated margins ('corona radiata') on CT indicate invasion of surrounding stroma, strongly suggesting malignancy rather than an inflammatory or infective process. TB pleuritis would not produce a discrete spiculated mass. The peripheral location of the mass in a non-smoker is consistent with adenocarcinoma, which tends to be peripheral compared to squamous cell carcinoma.

prompts.label: CT-Guided Biopsy and Molecular Testing
prompts.text: CT-guided biopsy of the right lower lobe mass: adenocarcinoma with acinar and lepidic growth pattern. TTF-1 positive. EGFR mutation testing: exon 19 deletion detected. Pleural fluid cytology: malignant cells consistent with adenocarcinoma. Staging: T2N2M1a (Stage IVA).
prompts.reasoning: Histopathology confirms lung adenocarcinoma with TTF-1 positivity confirming pulmonary origin. The exon 19 EGFR deletion is the most common activating EGFR mutation in Asian non-smoking women with lung adenocarcinoma, making this patient eligible for first-line tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy. Malignant cells in the pleural fluid confirm Stage IVA disease. The diagnosis is confirmed: metastatic EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with a 4-month history of progressive breathlessness and dry cough.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive subacute dyspnoea and dry cough over 4 months in a middle-aged man requires broad consideration. IPF, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, sarcoidosis, atypical pneumonia, heart failure, and TB are all in the differential. The duration and dry character are the only discriminatory axes introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He notes that his symptoms are consistently worse in the evenings after returning home from work but improve when he takes annual leave and spends time away from home. He has no fever during these episodes.
prompts.reasoning: The temporal pattern of symptoms — worse at home but improving away from home — is a classic pattern of exposure-related lung disease, pointing to an environmental or domestic antigen trigger. This pattern is characteristic of hypersensitivity pneumonitis (EAA) where symptoms fluctuate with antigen exposure. This pattern is inconsistent with IPF (progressively worsening regardless of environment) and heart failure. Sarcoidosis and atypical pneumonia do not show this exposure-related variability.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He keeps three budgerigars at home in his bedroom. He has no known prior lung disease, no occupational dust exposure, and no autoimmune disease. He is a non-smoker and takes no regular medications.
prompts.reasoning: Pet birds (budgerigars, pigeons, parrots) are the most common cause of hypersensitivity pneumonitis (bird fancier's lung) through sensitisation to avian serum proteins and feather dust. The combination of domestic bird exposure with symptoms that worsen at home but improve away strongly implicates bird-related hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Sarcoidosis remains in the differential as it can present with similar subacute symptoms, but the environmental exposure provides a clear alternative pathological mechanism.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 93% on room air, desaturating to 87% on a 6-minute walk test. Fine bibasal inspiratory crackles. No wheeze. No digital clubbing (or minimal). No peripheral lymphadenopathy. No skin rash.
prompts.reasoning: Fine inspiratory crackles with exertional desaturation indicate an interstitial lung process. At this stage, IPF and hypersensitivity pneumonitis are clinically difficult to distinguish on examination alone — both produce bibasal crackles and exertional hypoxia. Digital clubbing, if absent, slightly favours hypersensitivity pneumonitis (where clubbing is less common than in IPF). Sarcoidosis would less typically produce bibasal crackles in the absence of advanced fibrosis. HRCT and serology are key to differentiation.

prompts.label: HRCT Thorax
prompts.text: HRCT thorax: bilateral upper and mid-zone ground-glass opacities with centrilobular nodules. Mosaic attenuation pattern suggesting air trapping. Mild fibrotic changes in the mid-zones. No honeycombing. No subpleural predominance.
prompts.reasoning: HRCT findings of upper/mid-zone predominant ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodules, and air trapping without honeycombing or subpleural predominance are characteristic of subacute hypersensitivity pneumonitis. These features directly contrast with IPF, which produces subpleural basal-predominant honeycombing (UIP pattern) and lacks centrilobular nodules. The upper-zone predominance and centrilobular nodularity are explained by bronchiolocentric antigen deposition from inhaled bird antigen. This CT pattern, combined with bird exposure and the exposure-symptom temporal relationship, clinches the diagnosis.

prompts.label: Serum Precipitins and BAL
prompts.text: Serum precipitins (Ouchterlony technique) against budgerigar serum proteins and droppings: strongly positive. Bronchoalveolar lavage: lymphocytosis (45% lymphocytes, predominantly CD8+ T-cells). CD4:CD8 ratio 0.7 (reduced). No organisms identified.
prompts.reasoning: Strongly positive serum precipitins against budgerigar antigens confirm immunological sensitisation to the domestic bird antigens, providing the aetiological link. BAL lymphocytosis with a reduced CD4:CD8 ratio (<1.0 or <2.0) is characteristic of hypersensitivity pneumonitis, in contrast to sarcoidosis (where CD4:CD8 ratio is elevated >3.5). The complete picture — budgerigar exposure, exposure-related symptoms, upper-zone HRCT changes, positive precipitins, and BAL lymphocytosis with CD8 predominance — confirms the diagnosis of bird fancier's lung (subacute hypersensitivity pneumonitis).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 48-year-old man in the surgical high-dependency unit develops acute breathlessness and worsening hypoxia 36 hours after an emergency laparotomy for perforated sigmoid diverticulitis.
prompts.reasoning: Acute respiratory deterioration in a post-operative patient 36 hours after abdominal surgery raises several important diagnoses. ARDS secondary to abdominal sepsis, HAP, PE (post-operative immobilisation), cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, post-operative atelectasis, and aspiration pneumonia are all relevant. The temporal context — post-operative in the setting of intra-abdominal sepsis — is the key discriminatory axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The breathlessness developed rapidly over 4 hours and is worsening despite increasing supplemental oxygen. Nursing notes record that SpO2 was 98% on 4 L/min nasal cannula 6 hours ago, and is now 86% on 15 L/min oxygen.
prompts.reasoning: Rapidly progressive hypoxia that is refractory to increasing oxygen supplementation (oxygen-resistant hypoxaemia) is a cardinal feature of ARDS. The rate of deterioration from normal SpO2 to critical hypoxia within 6 hours suggests a fulminant process affecting gas exchange. Atelectasis and aspiration pneumonia would typically respond better to oxygen supplementation. PE and cardiogenic pulmonary oedema need urgent exclusion.

prompts.label: Clinical Background
prompts.text: The laparotomy identified faecal peritonitis with significant contamination. He developed a fever of 39.4°C and positive blood cultures for Gram-negative organisms 12 hours post-operatively, requiring broad-spectrum antibiotics. He has no pre-existing cardiac or lung disease.
prompts.reasoning: Gram-negative bacteraemia from faecal peritonitis is one of the most common precipitants of ARDS via the systemic inflammatory response. Systemic sepsis triggers diffuse alveolar damage through inflammatory cytokine release and endothelial injury. HAP is possible in a post-operative intubated patient, but the bilateral rapid onset in the context of severe sepsis is more consistent with ARDS. No cardiac history makes cardiogenic pulmonary oedema less likely, but fluid overload from resuscitation is possible.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.1°C, HR 124 bpm, BP 92/58 mmHg (requiring vasopressors), RR 38 breaths/min, SpO2 85% on 15 L/min oxygen (PaO2/FiO2 ratio calculated at 88 mmHg). Bilateral coarse crepitations throughout both lung fields. Trachea is central. JVP not elevated. No peripheral oedema.
prompts.reasoning: PaO2/FiO2 (P/F) ratio of 88 mmHg indicates severe ARDS (P/F <100). Bilateral crepitations without elevated JVP or peripheral oedema suggest the hypoxia is not primarily cardiogenic. The combination of systemic sepsis, bilateral lung involvement, and refractory hypoxia with low P/F ratio meets the Berlin Definition criteria for ARDS. HAP cannot be fully excluded at this stage as it can also produce bilateral infiltrates, but the clinical syndrome of diffuse bilateral injury in the context of sepsis is most consistent with ARDS.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Portable chest X-ray: bilateral diffuse alveolar opacification affecting all zones of both lung fields ('white-out' appearance). Air bronchograms visible bilaterally. No cardiomegaly. No pleural effusions. ET tube and central venous catheter in appropriate positions.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral diffuse alveolar opacification with air bronchograms ('whiteout') in a non-cardiogenic distribution (no cardiomegaly, no vascular redistribution, no effusions) is the hallmark CXR finding of ARDS. The Berlin Definition requires: acute onset within 7 days of a known clinical insult (sepsis), bilateral opacities on CXR not fully explained by effusions/collapse/nodules, and hypoxaemia with P/F <300. This case meets all Berlin criteria for severe ARDS. HAP produces more localised infiltrates rather than diffuse bilateral 'whiteout'.

prompts.label: Arterial Blood Gas and ECHO
prompts.text: ABG on FiO2 1.0 (100% oxygen): pH 7.26, PaCO2 52 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 22 mmol/L, PaO2/FiO2 = 58 mmHg (severe ARDS). Bedside ECHO: normal LV function, LVEF 60%. No wall motion abnormalities. No pericardial effusion.
prompts.reasoning: P/F ratio of 58 mmHg confirms severe ARDS (Berlin Definition: severe ARDS defined as P/F <100 with PEEP ≥5 cmH2O). Combined respiratory and metabolic acidosis reflects the severity of respiratory failure. Normal LV function on ECHO definitively excludes cardiogenic pulmonary oedema as the primary cause of bilateral opacification. The diagnosis is confirmed as severe ARDS secondary to systemic sepsis from Gram-negative bacteraemia following faecal peritonitis. Lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volumes is required.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 64-year-old man is brought to hospital by his wife because she has noticed he has become increasingly confused over the past 2 weeks.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute progressive confusion in an elderly man is initially a neurological diagnostic challenge. Structural brain lesions (stroke, tumour, haematoma), metabolic causes, CNS infection, and paraneoplastic syndromes are all plausible at this stage. The single axis introduced is subacute cognitive deterioration.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: On further history from his wife, she reports he has had a cough for 3 months, has lost 8 kg, and has complained of leg weakness for 3 weeks. He has no headache, no fever, and no history of trauma.
prompts.reasoning: Chronic cough, significant weight loss, and new neurological symptoms (confusion, leg weakness) occurring concurrently in an elderly man raises strong suspicion for lung malignancy with neurological involvement. The combination of pulmonary symptoms and multi-site neurological deficits (cognition and lower limb) could represent brain metastases, spinal cord compression, or paraneoplastic syndromes. SCLC is the lung cancer subtype most strongly associated with paraneoplastic neurological syndromes and tends to present with advanced/metastatic disease.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has smoked 30 cigarettes daily for 40 years (60 pack-years). He has no prior lung disease. His leg weakness began in the proximal lower limbs and he has noticed increased thirst and polyuria over the past week.
prompts.reasoning: A 60 pack-year smoking history significantly raises the probability of lung cancer. Proximal muscle weakness combined with confusion and polyuria/polydipsia may suggest a metabolic disturbance. In lung cancer, SCLC is associated with ectopic ACTH secretion (causing hyponatraemia or Cushing features) and SIADH (hyponatraemia causing confusion). Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS) — a paraneoplastic complication of SCLC — produces proximal muscle weakness. The convergence of respiratory and multi-system neurological/endocrine features is highly suggestive of SCLC with paraneoplastic manifestations.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 36.8°C. Confused (GCS 12/15). Proximal lower limb weakness (4/5) with areflexia. Power improves briefly with repeated testing. SpO2 94% on room air. Reduced breath sounds at the right hilum. No finger clubbing. Chest examination otherwise normal. No focal neurological deficit consistent with stroke.
prompts.reasoning: Power improvement with repeated muscle testing (facilitation) is the hallmark of Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS), which is caused by autoantibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels and is strongly associated with SCLC. This pattern is opposite to myasthenia gravis (weakness worsening with repetition). Areflexia that improves post-exercise is also a feature of LEMS. The combination of proximal weakness with facilitation, confusion (possibly from SIADH/electrolyte disturbance), hilar fullness, and heavy smoking history points clearly to SCLC with paraneoplastic LEMS.

prompts.label: CT Thorax and Abdomen
prompts.text: CT thorax: large central right hilar mass with involvement of the right main bronchus and extension into the mediastinum. Bulky bilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Small right pleural effusion. CT abdomen: bilateral adrenal metastases.
prompts.reasoning: A large central hilar mass with mediastinal invasion and bilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy is the classical CT appearance of SCLC. Bilateral adrenal metastases confirm extensive disease. SCLC characteristically presents as a central bulky mediastinal mass rather than a peripheral nodule (which is more typical of adenocarcinoma). The combination of central mass, mediastinal invasion, and distant metastases with paraneoplastic LEMS narrows the diagnosis firmly to SCLC.

prompts.label: Bronchoscopy, Biopsy and Serology
prompts.text: Bronchoscopic biopsy of the right hilar mass: small cells with scant cytoplasm, hyperchromatic nuclei, and nuclear moulding. IHC: synaptophysin positive, chromogranin positive, TTF-1 positive, Ki-67 >90%. Serum VGCC antibodies: positive. Sodium: 118 mmol/L (SIADH). SCLC staging: extensive-stage disease.
prompts.reasoning: Histopathology showing small cells with scant cytoplasm and nuclear moulding with neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin, TTF-1) and very high proliferative index (Ki-67 >90%) confirms SCLC. Positive voltage-gated calcium channel (VGCC) antibodies confirm paraneoplastic LEMS. Hyponatraemia confirms SIADH — another paraneoplastic complication of SCLC. Bilateral adrenal metastases confirm extensive-stage disease. The diagnosis is confirmed: extensive-stage small cell lung carcinoma with paraneoplastic LEMS and SIADH.

case_0135.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung
case_0135.finalDiagnosis: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 66-year-old man is referred to a respiratory clinic with a 3-month history of progressive cough and haemoptysis.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung
prompts.reasoning: Haemoptysis with progressive cough over 3 months in an elderly man demands urgent evaluation for malignancy and TB. Lung cancers of all major subtypes, TB, lung abscess, and bronchiectasis are all plausible at this stage. The discriminatory axis introduced is haemoptysis with a subacute progressive cough.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The haemoptysis consists of blood-streaked sputum, occurring on most days. He also has progressively worsening breathlessness and has lost 6 kg over the past 3 months. He has no fever and no night sweats.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung
prompts.reasoning: Persistent haemoptysis with weight loss and progressive dyspnoea over 3 months raises strong concern for malignancy. Absence of fever and night sweats makes active TB somewhat less likely but does not exclude it. Lung abscess would typically cause fever and purulent sputum. Bronchiectasis can cause haemoptysis but weight loss of 6 kg in 3 months is not characteristic without a superimposed illness. All lung cancer subtypes remain in the differential alongside TB.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has smoked 25 cigarettes per day for 45 years (56 pack-years) and worked as a welder for 30 years. He was diagnosed with COPD 5 years ago and uses a salbutamol inhaler.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung
prompts.reasoning: A 56 pack-year smoking history and occupational welding exposure (metal fume inhalation) are both recognised carcinogenic exposures. Known COPD is itself a risk factor for lung cancer independent of smoking. While all major lung cancer subtypes can occur in heavy smokers, squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC are most strongly associated with smoking and central airway involvement. SCLC can be provisionally reduced in probability given the 3-month course without rapid systemic progression or paraneoplastic features. TB remains possible given occupational crowded environments.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 91% on room air, RR 22 breaths/min. Reduced breath sounds and dullness to percussion in the right upper zone. No wheeze. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No finger clubbing. Right-sided Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) noted on careful inspection.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung
prompts.reasoning: Right-sided Horner syndrome indicates compression or invasion of the right sympathetic chain at the superior sulcus, strongly suggesting an apical or Pancoast tumour. This localises the pathology to the right upper lobe and makes an apical lung malignancy very likely. Both squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma can occur in the upper lobe, though squamous cell carcinoma is more commonly central and associated with heavy smoking. TB can also occur in the upper lobe but would not produce Horner syndrome. Imaging is critical.

prompts.label: CT Thorax
prompts.text: CT thorax: a 4.8 cm central right hilar mass invading the right main bronchus with post-obstructive collapse of the right upper lobe. The mass abuts but does not clearly invade the mediastinum. No pleural effusion. Ipsilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung
prompts.reasoning: A central hilar mass causing bronchial obstruction with post-obstructive lobar collapse is the characteristic CT presentation of squamous cell carcinoma, which arises in the central airways from squamous metaplasia of the bronchial epithelium. Adenocarcinoma is typically peripheral and would not cause central obstruction. The central location, heavy smoking history, male sex, and post-obstructive collapse firmly point to squamous cell carcinoma over adenocarcinoma. Biopsy is required for tissue confirmation.

prompts.label: Bronchoscopy and Biopsy
prompts.text: Flexible bronchoscopy: irregular, friable endobronchial mass in the right main bronchus. Endobronchial biopsy: invasive squamous cell carcinoma with keratin pearls and intercellular bridges. p40 and CK5/6 positive on immunohistochemistry. Serum calcium: 2.98 mmol/L (elevated).
accepted: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung
prompts.reasoning: Histopathology confirms squamous cell carcinoma with characteristic keratin pearls and squamous markers (p40, CK5/6). Hypercalcaemia — a paraneoplastic feature of squamous cell carcinoma mediated by PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related protein) secretion — further supports this diagnosis. This paraneoplastic association is characteristic of squamous cell carcinoma rather than adenocarcinoma or SCLC (which causes SIADH/ectopic ACTH). The diagnosis is confirmed: central squamous cell carcinoma of the lung with paraneoplastic hypercalcaemia.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 71-year-old man presents with a 5-month history of left-sided chest pain and worsening breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral chest pain with progressive dyspnoea over 5 months in an elderly man has multiple causes including pleural malignancy, lung cancer, TB pleuritis, empyema, and cardiac disease. The subacute unilateral nature and long duration are the only discriminatory features introduced at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The chest pain is dull and constant, not pleuritic. He has also had drenching night sweats and a 7 kg weight loss over 5 months. He has no cough and no haemoptysis.
prompts.reasoning: Constant (non-pleuritic) dull chest pain suggests pleural or chest wall infiltration rather than acute inflammation. Significant constitutional symptoms (night sweats, weight loss) combined with progressive dyspnoea strongly suggest either malignancy or TB. Absence of cough and haemoptysis makes primary lung cancer with endobronchial disease less likely, though pleural metastases could occur without these features.
