Heart failure and empyema would not typically cause night sweats over months.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He worked as a shipyard insulation installer for 25 years and retired 15 years ago. He is a light smoker (10 pack-years, quit 10 years ago). He has no prior diagnosis of lung cancer or TB.
prompts.reasoning: Shipyard insulation work is the highest-risk occupation for asbestos exposure, and the latency period for mesothelioma (typically 30–50 years after exposure) is consistent with this history. Asbestos exposure also significantly increases risk of lung carcinoma independently of smoking. However, the light smoking history, non-central symptoms, and occupational asbestos exposure shift the probability strongly toward mesothelioma. TB remains possible but less likely given the occupational context. This is a critical risk factor that will guide imaging interpretation.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.3°C, SpO2 91% on room air. Trachea deviated to the right. Stony dullness to percussion throughout the left hemithorax. Absent breath sounds on the left. No vocal resonance on the left. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Complete stony dullness with absent breath sounds throughout an entire hemithorax and contralateral tracheal deviation indicates a very large pleural effusion or pleural mass encasing the lung. This extent of involvement makes simple reactive effusion unlikely. The two leading diagnoses are mesothelioma (diffuse pleural tumour) and lung cancer with a malignant pleural effusion. TB pleuritis rarely causes this degree of total hemithoracic opacification. Imaging and pleural fluid analysis are essential.

prompts.label: CT Thorax
prompts.text: CT thorax: large left pleural effusion with extensive nodular pleural thickening encircling the left lung, extending into the interlobar fissures. The pleural thickening is circumferential and lobulated. Left lung is almost entirely encased. No parenchymal lung mass identified. Bilateral pleural plaques are noted.
prompts.reasoning: Circumferential, lobulated, nodular pleural thickening encasing the lung with associated pleural plaques (a marker of prior asbestos exposure) is the characteristic CT appearance of malignant mesothelioma. Lung cancer with pleural metastases produces discrete nodular pleural deposits rather than a continuous encasing sheet of thickening. The bilateral pleural plaques confirm significant prior asbestos exposure. The absence of a parenchymal lung mass makes primary lung cancer the source of pleural disease less likely. Pleural biopsy is required for definitive diagnosis.

prompts.label: Pleural Biopsy and Histopathology
prompts.text: CT-guided pleural biopsy: epithelioid mesothelioma with tubular and papillary architecture. Immunohistochemistry: calretinin positive, WT-1 positive, D2-40 positive, CEA negative, TTF-1 negative. Pleural fluid cytology: malignant mesothelial cells. Staging: T3N0M0.
prompts.reasoning: Histopathology confirms epithelioid mesothelioma — the most common subtype, with a relatively better prognosis compared to sarcomatoid or biphasic types. The immunoprofile is diagnostic: positive calretinin, WT-1, and D2-40 are mesothelial markers, while negative CEA and TTF-1 exclude metastatic adenocarcinoma from the lung or elsewhere. The diagnosis is confirmed: malignant pleural mesothelioma related to occupational asbestos exposure with a 35-year latency period.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 55-year-old man is admitted with a 3-week history of fever, productive cough, and right-sided chest pain.
prompts.reasoning: Fever, productive cough, and unilateral chest pain over 3 weeks spans both infective (pneumonia, TB, abscess, empyema) and malignant causes. The 3-week duration distinguishes this from typical acute pneumonia and introduces subacute progression as the discriminatory axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The sputum is copious, foul-smelling, and brown-coloured. He has sweated at night on most nights and lost approximately 4 kg over 3 weeks. He noticed the sputum became much more purulent after a sudden episode of coughing up a large volume 5 days ago.
prompts.reasoning: Foul-smelling, copious, brown sputum is strongly suggestive of anaerobic infection and necrotic tissue, pointing toward a suppurative lung process. The sudden large-volume expectoration 5 days ago suggests a cavity rupturing into a bronchus — a classic feature of lung abscess. Night sweats and weight loss are consistent with a significant infective or necrotic process. Empyema can drain via a bronchopleural fistula but the foul sputum and bronchial communication pattern are more typical of abscess. TB rarely produces this quality of sputum acutely.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has a history of excessive alcohol use and was found unresponsive at home 4 weeks ago, after which he was treated at a local clinic for a presumed URTI. He has poor dentition. He is a non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: Alcohol intoxication causing loss of consciousness is a classic predisposing factor for aspiration of oropharyngeal contents — the most common mechanism for anaerobic lung abscess. Poor dentition provides a reservoir of anaerobic organisms. A preceding 'URTI' (likely aspiration event) 4 weeks ago aligns with the typical 1–2 week incubation period before lung abscess development. Empyema can also follow aspiration pneumonia but the bronchial communication and sputum character are more consistent with abscess. TB is now less likely given the clear aspiration mechanism.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.8°C, HR 108 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, SpO2 93% on room air. Reduced breath sounds in the right lower zone with dullness to percussion. Coarse crackles in the same area. Foul-smelling breath noted. Dentition is visibly poor with several carious teeth.
prompts.reasoning: Reduced breath sounds with dullness and coarse crackles in the right lower zone are consistent with a right lower lobe process — the most common site for aspiration-related lung abscess (due to the anatomy of the right lower lobe bronchus in the supine position). Foul-smelling breath reflects anaerobic infection. Poor dentition confirms the anaerobic bacterial source. The combination of aspiration history, anaerobic characteristics, and right lower lobe localisation makes lung abscess the predominant diagnosis. Chest imaging is required to visualise the cavity.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates a round opacity with a thick irregular wall in the right lower lobe containing an air-fluid level.
prompts.reasoning: A thick-walled cavity with an air-fluid level in the right lower lobe is the pathognomonic radiological finding of a lung abscess. The air-fluid level forms when the abscess cavity communicates with a bronchus, allowing pus to partially drain and air to enter. This is the hallmark of suppurative necrosis of lung parenchyma. Empyema can also show an air-fluid level (if bronchopleural fistula) but would produce a lenticular pleural-based opacity. The parenchymal location and thick irregular wall confirm lung abscess.

prompts.label: Sputum Culture and Bronchoscopy
prompts.text: Sputum culture: mixed growth including Peptostreptococcus species, Fusobacterium nucleatum, and Prevotella melaninogenica (anaerobic oral flora). Blood cultures: negative. Bronchoscopy excludes an endobronchial obstructing lesion. No AFBs on sputum smear.
prompts.reasoning: Mixed anaerobic oropharyngeal flora (Peptostreptococcus, Fusobacterium, Prevotella) confirms anaerobic aspiration as the mechanism of infection. Negative blood cultures are typical of aspiration lung abscess, where bacteraemia is uncommon compared to haematogenous abscesses. Bronchoscopy excluding an obstructing lesion confirms that this is a primary infectious abscess rather than post-obstructive necrosis from a tumour — an important distinction in a smoker. Negative AFBs exclude TB. The diagnosis is confirmed: primary aspiration lung abscess due to mixed anaerobic infection.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner with a several-year history of daily productive cough and recurrent chest infections.
prompts.reasoning: Daily productive cough for several years with recurrent infections in a young woman demands consideration of structural lung disease. Bronchiectasis, COPD, asthma, TB, cystic fibrosis, and hypersensitivity pneumonitis all present with chronic cough. The multi-year duration and recurrent infective episodes are the key discriminatory axes at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She produces approximately two tablespoons of yellow-green sputum daily, increasing to large volumes during exacerbations. She has also had three episodes of haemoptysis in the past year, each self-limiting. She has no weight loss and no night sweats.
prompts.reasoning: Daily large-volume purulent sputum production with recurrent haemoptysis is characteristic of bronchiectasis, where chronically dilated bronchi pool secretions and develop friable engorged bronchial vessels. COPD can produce chronic productive cough but haemoptysis and this volume of sputum are not typical. Cystic fibrosis must remain in the differential as it is a major cause of bronchiectasis in younger patients and presents with this exact pattern. Absence of systemic features makes TB and HPP less likely.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She had severe whooping cough as an infant requiring hospitalisation, and has had recurrent sinusitis and ear infections throughout her life. She is a non-smoker and has no family history of lung disease. She has had no prior formal lung function testing.
prompts.reasoning: Severe childhood pertussis (whooping cough) is a recognised cause of post-infectious bronchiectasis through permanent bronchial wall damage. Recurrent sinusitis and ear infections alongside bronchiectasis suggests pan-airway mucociliary dysfunction, raising the possibility of Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD) or cystic fibrosis — both of which cause pan-sinopulmonary disease. COPD and asthma are effectively excluded by the non-smoker status, young age, and infective pattern. Cystic fibrosis remains in the differential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 96% on room air, RR 18 breaths/min. Bilateral coarse crackles in the lower and mid zones, most prominent on the left. Digital clubbing present (grade 2). No wheeze. Trachea is central. No situs inversus.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral coarse crackles (from pooled airway secretions) and digital clubbing are the classic examination findings of bronchiectasis. Clubbing in lung disease indicates chronic hypoxia and inflammation — more consistent with structural suppurative disease (bronchiectasis, CF) than with asthma or COPD. Central trachea and absence of situs inversus argues against Kartagener syndrome (a PCD variant), though PCD without situs inversus cannot be excluded. Cystic fibrosis is still possible given the young age and pan-sinopulmonary involvement. HRCT is required.

prompts.label: HRCT Thorax
prompts.text: HRCT thorax: bilateral cylindrical bronchial dilatation exceeding the diameter of the adjacent pulmonary artery ('signet ring' sign) in the lower and mid lobes. Bronchial wall thickening with retained secretions. No centrilobular nodules. Left lower lobe most severely affected.
prompts.reasoning: HRCT confirmation of bilateral cylindrical bronchiectasis (signet ring sign — dilated bronchus > adjacent artery) establishes the diagnosis of bronchiectasis. The lower lobe predominance is consistent with post-infectious aetiology (most commonly post-pertussis or post-pneumonia in childhood). The HRCT pattern distinguishes bronchiectasis from cystic fibrosis, which characteristically produces upper lobe-predominant disease. A sweat chloride test is still indicated to formally exclude CF, but the lower lobe distribution and HRCT pattern are more consistent with post-infectious bronchiectasis.

prompts.label: Sweat Chloride Test and Immune Workup
prompts.text: Sweat chloride test: 18 mmol/L (normal, <40 mmol/L — excludes cystic fibrosis). Serum immunoglobulins: normal. Ciliary function tests: normal ciliary beat frequency and ultrastructure on nasal brushings. Sputum culture during exacerbation: Haemophilus influenzae.
prompts.reasoning: Normal sweat chloride definitively excludes cystic fibrosis. Normal immunoglobulins exclude common variable immunodeficiency (another cause of recurrent infections and bronchiectasis). Normal ciliary function tests exclude primary ciliary dyskinesia. With all secondary causes excluded, the history of severe infantile pertussis is the most likely underlying aetiology of the bronchiectasis. Haemophilus influenzae is the most common organism in bronchiectasis exacerbations. The diagnosis is confirmed: post-infectious bronchiectasis (post-pertussis), lower lobe predominant.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with sudden severe breathlessness that woke her from sleep.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden severe dyspnoea waking a patient from sleep (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea) in an elderly woman is an urgent presentation. Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, severe asthma, PE, pneumonia, and pneumothorax are all plausible at this stage. The nocturnal onset from sleep is the single discriminatory axis introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has been sleeping with three pillows for the past 2 weeks and has had ankle swelling. Tonight she woke gasping and is unable to lie flat. She describes a feeling of drowning.
prompts.reasoning: Orthopnoea (3-pillow use), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, bilateral ankle swelling, and inability to lie flat point strongly toward a fluid overload or cardiac decompensation syndrome. Acute asthma can also cause sudden nocturnal breathlessness. Pneumonia with significant hypoxia can produce severe dyspnoea and inability to lie flat in an elderly patient. PE and pneumothorax would not produce the progressive multi-week orthopnoea pattern. At least three diagnoses remain plausible pending medical history and examination.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has known hypertension and ischaemic heart disease, and had a myocardial infarction 2 years ago. She ran out of her furosemide 10 days ago and has not been able to refill it.
prompts.reasoning: Known ischaemic cardiomyopathy post-MI combined with 10 days of missed diuretic therapy provides a clear mechanism for acute-on-chronic cardiac decompensation and cardiogenic pulmonary oedema. This history makes heart failure the predominant concern. Asthma remains in the differential as nocturnal bronchospasm can coexist with cardiac disease. Pneumonia becomes less likely with no fever or infective prodrome mentioned. Physical examination will be decisive.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 36.7°C, HR 118 bpm (irregular), BP 162/98 mmHg, RR 34 breaths/min, SpO2 80% on room air. Patient is diaphoretic and sitting upright. Bilateral fine crackles to the mid-zones bilaterally. Bilateral pitting oedema to the knees. JVP elevated at 6 cm above sternal angle.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral fine crackles, elevated JVP, bilateral pitting oedema, and an irregular pulse in the context of known ischaemic cardiomyopathy and missed diuresis confirms acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema. Elevated JVP confirms raised venous pressure from right heart congestion. Bilateral crackles without wheeze distinguish this from asthma. No fever makes pneumonia less likely. The clinical diagnosis is now established — CXR and echocardiography will confirm and quantify cardiac dysfunction.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Portable chest X-ray: cardiomegaly, bilateral perihilar 'bat-wing' shadowing, bilateral pleural effusions (right greater than left), and horizontal linear opacities in the costophrenic angles. Upper lobe venous diversion noted.
prompts.reasoning: The radiological triad of cardiomegaly, bat-wing perihilar oedema, pleural effusions, and upper lobe blood diversion is the classical CXR appearance of cardiogenic pulmonary oedema. Upper lobe venous diversion reflects redistribution of pulmonary blood flow due to elevated LVEDP causing lower lobe venous hypertension. Kerley B lines (horizontal linear opacities) represent fluid in interlobular septa. This is distinct from ARDS (which produces bilateral oedema without cardiomegaly or Kerley B lines) and from pneumonia (which produces focal consolidation).

prompts.label: Echocardiogram and BNP
prompts.text: BNP: 1,840 pg/mL (markedly elevated). 12-lead ECG: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. Bedside echo: severely impaired LV systolic function (LVEF 25%), global hypokinesis, dilated LV. No significant valvular disease.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated BNP (>400 pg/mL is highly sensitive for cardiac failure) confirms a cardiac origin for the pulmonary oedema. Severely reduced LVEF (25%) with global hypokinesis confirms ischaemic cardiomyopathy as the underlying substrate. New atrial fibrillation likely precipitated the acute decompensation by reducing diastolic filling time and increasing heart rate. The diagnosis is confirmed: acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema secondary to decompensated ischaemic cardiomyopathy, precipitated by AF and missed diuretic therapy.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 48-year-old man is referred to a sleep clinic by his general practitioner because of excessive daytime sleepiness for the past year.
prompts.reasoning: Excessive daytime sleepiness over a year in a middle-aged man admits a broad differential including OSA, narcolepsy, mood disorder, hypothyroidism, anaemia, and chronic fatigue syndrome. Daytime hypersomnolence is the single discriminatory axis introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His wife reports that he snores loudly every night and that she has been disturbed by his restless, unrefreshing sleep. He wakes feeling tired regardless of sleep duration and has had two near-miss road traffic accidents due to falling asleep at the wheel.
prompts.reasoning: Loud habitual snoring, unrefreshing sleep, and dangerous daytime sleepiness are characteristic of obstructive sleep apnoea. However, irresistible daytime sleep episodes causing functional impairment are also a feature of narcolepsy (which can present without cataplexy). Depression commonly causes unrefreshing sleep and daytime fatigue. Hypothyroidism and anaemia remain possible but are less specifically tied to nocturnal symptoms with snoring. Three plausible diagnoses remain pending further history and examination.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has hypertension treated with amlodipine and type 2 diabetes. He does not smoke. He drinks two glasses of wine most evenings. He does not take sedative medications.
prompts.reasoning: Hypertension and type 2 diabetes are both associated with OSA and the metabolic syndrome, increasing pre-test probability. Evening alcohol use is a recognised exacerbating factor through upper airway muscle relaxation. Depression causing hypersomnia remains possible, though the cardiac and metabolic comorbidities align more with OSA. Narcolepsy becomes less likely without cataplexy and given the strong metabolic context. Physical examination to assess upper airway anatomy is the key next step.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BMI 36.4 kg/m², neck circumference 46 cm. BP 148/92 mmHg. Mallampati class III oropharynx (soft palate partially visible). Macroglossia noted. Deviated nasal septum. SpO2 96% awake. Mental state examination is normal with no depressive features.
prompts.reasoning: Obesity (BMI >30), increased neck circumference (>43 cm), crowded oropharynx (Mallampati III), macroglossia, and nasal septal deviation confirm a high anatomical risk for upper airway collapse during sleep. Normal mental state examination with no depressive features effectively excludes depression as the cause of hypersomnolence. Normal waking SpO2 confirms hypoxaemia is sleep-specific. Overnight polysomnography is required to establish the diagnosis and quantify severity.

prompts.label: Overnight Oximetry
prompts.text: Home overnight pulse oximetry: oxygen desaturation index (ODI) of 42 events per hour with nadir SpO2 of 72%. Multiple prolonged desaturation episodes lasting 20–40 seconds correlating with snoring peaks.
prompts.reasoning: An ODI >30 events per hour with a nadir SpO2 of 72% indicates severe nocturnal hypoxaemia consistent with severe obstructive sleep apnoea. The temporal correlation between desaturation events and snoring peaks confirms the obstructive mechanism. ODI ≥15 has high sensitivity and specificity for clinically significant OSA. Formal polysomnography would quantify the Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI), but home oximetry findings in this clinical context are sufficient to initiate CPAP therapy pending full study.

prompts.label: Polysomnography
prompts.text: In-laboratory polysomnography: Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI) 68 events per hour (severe OSA, defined as >30). All events are obstructive in type. Average oxygen desaturation 18%, nadir 71%. Rapid eye movement (REM) sleep severely disrupted. Central apnoeas absent.
prompts.reasoning: AHI of 68 events per hour with exclusively obstructive events confirms severe obstructive sleep apnoea. The absence of central apnoeas excludes central sleep apnoea or Cheyne-Stokes respiration (associated with heart failure). Severe REM disruption explains unrefreshing sleep and cognitive impairment. The diagnosis is confirmed: severe obstructive sleep apnoea requiring CPAP therapy. The hypertension and metabolic syndrome are likely partially driven by the chronic intermittent nocturnal hypoxia.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric emergency department at midnight by his parents because of a barking cough and noisy breathing that began 3 hours ago.
prompts.reasoning: Acute onset of barking cough with noisy breathing (stridor) in a toddler at night requires urgent assessment. Croup, acute epiglottitis, foreign body aspiration, bronchiolitis, anaphylaxis, and bacterial tracheitis are all potentially serious causes. The barking character of the cough and the age and time of presentation are the only discriminatory features introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His parents report he had a runny nose and mild fever for 2 days prior to the onset of tonight's cough. The noisy breathing is louder when he is upset and slightly better when he is calm. There is no drooling and he has been eating and drinking normally during the day.
prompts.reasoning: A 2-day prodrome of coryzal symptoms (runny nose, mild fever) before the onset of barking cough and stridor is classic for viral croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), where upper respiratory viral infection causes subglottic mucosal oedema. Epiglottitis is effectively excluded: it presents without a coryzal prodrome, the child is typically toxic and drooling, and does not tolerate lying down. Absence of drooling and maintained oral intake are reassuring features against epiglottitis. Foreign body aspiration would not be preceded by coryzal symptoms.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has had one previous similar episode 8 months ago that resolved overnight without treatment. He is fully vaccinated including DTaP-IPV+Hib. He has no known allergies and no prior history of airway surgery.
prompts.reasoning: A prior similar episode that resolved spontaneously strongly supports recurrent viral croup, which is a recognised pattern in susceptible children. Complete immunisation history (including Hib) effectively eliminates acute epiglottitis caused by H. influenzae type b and diphtheria. Bacterial tracheitis is much rarer, typically does not recur episodically, and is more likely to cause a toxic appearance. No allergen exposure and no prior airway instrumentation exclude anaphylaxis and subglottic stenosis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.1°C, HR 128 bpm, RR 36 breaths/min, SpO2 96% on room air. Mild inspiratory stridor audible at rest. Mild intercostal and subcostal retractions. Child is alert, interactive, and not drooling. No neck stiffness. Chest: clear with no wheeze. Tonsils normal.
prompts.reasoning: Mild inspiratory stridor with mild retractions and maintained SpO2 in an alert, interactive, non-drooling child is consistent with mild-to-moderate croup (Westley Croup Score approximately 3). The absence of drooling, toxic appearance, or tripod positioning excludes epiglottitis definitively. Bacterial tracheitis produces a much more toxic child with high fever and severe obstruction that does not respond to standard croup treatment. The Westley criteria place this as mild croup, suitable for single-dose oral dexamethasone and observation.

prompts.label: AP Neck X-Ray
prompts.text: AP neck X-ray: subglottic narrowing producing a symmetrical tapering below the vocal cords — the 'steeple sign'. Epiglottis appears normal. No retropharyngeal soft tissue swelling.
prompts.reasoning: The 'steeple sign' — symmetrical subglottic tapering on AP neck X-ray — is produced by circumferential subglottic mucosal oedema, the pathological hallmark of viral croup. A normal epiglottis on lateral neck X-ray effectively excludes epiglottitis (where the epiglottis would appear swollen — 'thumbprint sign'). Absence of retropharyngeal swelling excludes retropharyngeal abscess. The steeple sign confirms the clinical diagnosis of croup.

prompts.label: Response to Treatment and Viral Testing
prompts.text: Stridor and retractions improve significantly within 30 minutes of a single oral dose of dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg. Nasopharyngeal swab (respiratory viral PCR panel): positive for parainfluenza virus type 1. Child is discharged home after 4 hours of observation.
prompts.reasoning: Rapid clinical response to a single dose of oral dexamethasone (which reduces subglottic mucosal oedema) is the expected therapeutic response in viral croup and confirms the diagnosis. Positive parainfluenza virus type 1 on PCR is the most common aetiological agent in croup, accounting for approximately 75% of cases. Bacterial tracheitis would not respond to steroids alone and would require antibiotic therapy and airway management. The diagnosis is confirmed: mild viral croup secondary to parainfluenza virus type 1.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 44-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with a painless lump in the right side of his neck noticed 6 weeks ago.
prompts.reasoning: A painless cervical neck lump in a middle-aged man requires urgent evaluation to exclude malignancy. NPC, lymphoma, other head and neck cancers with metastatic nodes, TB lymphadenitis, reactive lymphadenopathy, and branchial cyst are all in the differential. The painless nature and 6-week duration are the only discriminatory axes introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He also reports intermittent right-sided nasal obstruction and a small amount of blood-stained nasal discharge for the past 3 months. He has had no sore throat, no weight loss, and no fever.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral nasal obstruction and blood-stained nasal discharge (epistaxis) combined with a cervical neck mass in a middle-aged man are the classic presenting symptoms of nasopharyngeal carcinoma. These local nasal symptoms indicate a nasopharyngeal primary. Lymphoma can cause cervical nodes but would not explain unilateral nasal obstruction and epistaxis. Reactive lymphadenopathy would not persist for 6 weeks without fever or systemic symptoms. TB lymphadenitis can occur without fever in immunocompetent patients.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He is of Cantonese-speaking Chinese ethnicity and was born in southern China. He grew up eating preserved salted fish regularly. He is a non-smoker and has no family history of cancer.
prompts.reasoning: Southern Chinese ethnicity and childhood consumption of salted fish (nitrosamines) are the two most strongly established environmental and genetic risk factors for EBV-associated NPC. The incidence of NPC is 20–30 times higher in Southern Chinese populations compared to most other ethnic groups. TB lymphadenitis is also relevant in a patient from southern China where TB prevalence is higher. However, the nasal symptoms (obstruction and epistaxis) that preceded the neck mass point strongly toward a nasopharyngeal primary rather than isolated TB lymphadenitis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: A firm, non-tender, 3 × 4 cm lymph node in the right posterior cervical triangle (level V). No other palpable lymphadenopathy. Ear examination: right middle ear effusion (conductive hearing loss confirmed on tuning fork testing). No trismus. Cranial nerves intact.
prompts.reasoning: Right-sided conductive hearing loss from middle ear effusion (glue ear in an adult) combined with a right posterior cervical node and unilateral nasal symptoms forms the classic triad of NPC. The effusion results from obstruction of the Eustachian tube orifice by the nasopharyngeal tumour. Posterior triangle (level V) nodal involvement is characteristic of NPC due to the lymphatic drainage of the nasopharynx. This clinical triad in a Southern Chinese man with salted fish exposure makes NPC the overwhelmingly likely diagnosis.

prompts.label: MRI Nasopharynx
prompts.text: MRI nasopharynx with contrast: an enhancing mass in the right lateral wall of the nasopharynx (fossa of Rosenmüller) with involvement of the right parapharyngeal space. Right middle ear effusion confirmed. No skull base invasion. Right posterior cervical lymph node mass confirmed.
prompts.reasoning: An enhancing mass in the fossa of Rosenmüller (the lateral recess of the nasopharynx — the most common site of origin for NPC) with ipsilateral middle ear effusion and cervical lymphadenopathy is diagnostic of NPC pending histological confirmation. MRI is the modality of choice for NPC staging due to superior soft tissue resolution and evaluation of skull base, parapharyngeal space, and perineural invasion. Biopsy is required to confirm histological subtype and guide treatment.

prompts.label: Nasopharyngeal Biopsy and EBV Serology
prompts.text: Endoscopic nasopharyngeal biopsy: non-keratinising undifferentiated carcinoma (WHO Type III). In situ hybridisation: EBER (EBV-encoded RNA) positive in tumour cells. Serum EBV viral capsid antigen (VCA) IgA: markedly elevated. Staging: T2N1M0 (Stage II).
prompts.reasoning: Histopathology confirms WHO Type III NPC (non-keratinising undifferentiated carcinoma), the most common subtype in endemic populations and the subtype most strongly associated with EBV. EBER positivity confirms EBV integration in the tumour cells. Elevated EBV VCA IgA is a highly sensitive and specific serological marker for NPC and is used for screening and surveillance in high-risk populations. The diagnosis is confirmed: EBV-associated NPC Stage II, amenable to concurrent chemoradiotherapy.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 69-year-old man is referred with a 2-year history of gradually worsening breathlessness on exertion.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive exertional dyspnoea over 2 years in an elderly man requires consideration of all major causes of progressive breathlessness. Fibrotic lung diseases (IPF, asbestosis, HPP), COPD, heart failure, and sarcoidosis are all plausible at this stage. No discriminatory axis beyond progressive exertional dyspnoea has been introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He also has a dry cough that has persisted for over a year. He has no wheeze, no haemoptysis, and no ankle swelling. He describes exertional breathlessness that initially occurred only on climbing stairs but now limits him on flat ground.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive exertional dyspnoea with a persistent dry cough without wheeze or haemoptysis describes a restrictive interstitial process. Absence of ankle oedema or orthopnoea reduces cardiac cause probability. IPF, asbestosis, and hypersensitivity pneumonitis are clinically indistinguishable at this stage. COPD is less likely without wheeze. Occupational history is essential to differentiate asbestosis from IPF.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He worked as a boilermaker in a power station for 30 years, retiring 12 years ago. He is an ex-smoker (25 pack-years, quit 15 years ago). He has never kept pet birds and has no exposure to agricultural materials or organic dusts.
prompts.reasoning: Boilermaker work in a power station involves close contact with lagged pipework, insulation, and boiler seals — historically among the highest-exposure occupations for asbestos after its widespread use until the 1980s. The 30-year occupational history with a latency period of approximately 30+ years (occupational exposure plus 12 years retirement) is consistent with asbestosis, which typically presents decades after exposure. Absence of organic antigen exposure effectively excludes hypersensitivity pneumonitis. IPF remains a close differential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 91% on room air, desaturating to 83% on a 6-minute walk test. Fine bibasal end-inspiratory crackles (Velcro-type). Digital clubbing (grade 2). No wheeze. Bilateral pleural plaques palpable along the lateral chest walls.
prompts.reasoning: Palpable pleural plaques along the lateral chest walls are a specific physical finding of asbestos exposure, confirming significant prior inhalation. In conjunction with bilateral Velcro crackles and digital clubbing (indicating chronic interstitial fibrosis), the combination is characteristic of asbestosis. IPF would produce the same crackles and clubbing but without pleural plaques. The physical finding of pleural plaques in an ex-boilermaker with progressive fibrotic breathlessness establishes asbestosis as the primary diagnosis over IPF.

prompts.label: HRCT Thorax
prompts.text: HRCT thorax: bilateral basal subpleural reticular fibrosis with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis. Multiple calcified pleural plaques on the diaphragmatic and costal pleural surfaces bilaterally. Parenchymal bands extending from the pleural plaques into the lower lobe parenchyma.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral basal subpleural fibrosis with honeycombing combined with calcified pleural plaques on HRCT is the characteristic imaging pattern of asbestosis. Calcified pleural plaques are pathognomonic of prior asbestos exposure. Parenchymal bands extending from pleural plaques into adjacent lung parenchyma represent fibrosis related to asbestos fibre deposition. While the parenchymal fibrosis pattern (basal subpleural honeycombing) can resemble IPF's UIP pattern, the pleural plaques confirm the asbestos aetiology. No discrete lung mass is identified, but surveillance is required given the significantly elevated lung cancer risk in asbestos-exposed individuals.

prompts.label: Pulmonary Function Tests and BAL
prompts.text: Spirometry: FVC 54% predicted, FEV1 58% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.83 (restrictive pattern). DLCO 42% predicted. Bronchoalveolar lavage: ferruginous bodies (asbestos bodies) identified — golden-brown beaded rods with dumbbell-shaped ends on iron stain. Asbestos body count elevated.
prompts.reasoning: Restrictive spirometry with reduced DLCO confirms interstitial fibrotic disease. Identification of ferruginous bodies (asbestos fibres coated with iron-protein — the BAL correlate of asbestos bodies seen on histopathology) in BAL confirms asbestos as the specific aetiological agent. Elevated asbestos body count in BAL reflects the cumulative asbestos fibre burden. The complete picture — occupational exposure, bilateral pleural plaques, basal subpleural fibrosis on HRCT, restrictive PFTs, and ferruginous bodies on BAL — establishes the definitive diagnosis of asbestosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old man is referred to a chest clinic with a 3-year history of exertional breathlessness and persistent cough.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive exertional breathlessness with chronic cough over 3 years in a middle-aged man has a broad differential including occupational lung diseases, COPD, TB, sarcoidosis, IPF, and heart failure. No discriminatory features beyond subacute progressive dyspnoea have been introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His cough is non-productive. He has had no fever, night sweats, haemoptysis, or weight loss. He reports the breathlessness is steadily progressive and has not improved with bronchodilator inhalers prescribed empirically by his GP.
prompts.reasoning: Non-productive cough without constitutional symptoms makes TB less likely, though it cannot be excluded without microbiological evaluation. Failure to respond to bronchodilators effectively rules out asthma and reduces COPD probability. Progressive non-reversible exertional dyspnoea with a dry cough points toward an interstitial or parenchymal process — sarcoidosis, silicosis, or IPF. Occupational history is essential at this point.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He worked as a granite stone cutter in a quarry for 22 years, from age 25 to 47. He is a non-smoker. He was born in India and migrated to Singapore 10 years ago. He has no autoimmune disease and no organic antigen exposure.
prompts.reasoning: Granite stone cutting is one of the highest-risk occupations for silica dust inhalation due to the high crystalline silica content of granite. Chronic silicosis typically develops after 10+ years of exposure at lower dust concentrations, consistent with the 22-year history. Origin from India (a TB-endemic region) combined with the silicosis risk adds a compound exposure risk — silicosis is itself a major risk factor for pulmonary TB (>3-fold increased risk). Both silicosis and TB must be actively considered. No organic antigen exposure eliminates HPP.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 93% on room air. Bilateral reduced breath sounds in the upper zones. Percussion slightly dull bilaterally in the upper zones. No crackles or wheeze. No digital clubbing. No peripheral lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Upper zone signs (reduced breath sounds and dullness bilaterally) are an important localising finding. Both silicosis and TB favour the upper lobes — silicosis because silica nodules are more concentrated in the upper lobes due to differential lymphatic drainage, and TB due to higher oxygen tension in the apices. Absent crackles and no clubbing distinguish this from IPF and asbestosis, which produce bibasal crackles. Sarcoidosis is less likely without lymphadenopathy. Chest imaging is essential.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: bilateral symmetrical small rounded nodules predominantly in the upper and mid zones. Enlarged and partially calcified hilar lymph nodes with a rim of calcification ('eggshell' calcification). No cavitation.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral symmetrical upper-zone small rounded nodules combined with eggshell calcification of hilar lymph nodes is the pathognomonic radiological pattern of silicosis. Eggshell calcification — calcification at the periphery of a lymph node — is highly specific for silicosis (and occasionally sarcoidosis). TB would produce asymmetrical infiltrates with possible cavitation rather than symmetrical round nodules. The nodular pattern and eggshell calcification in a granite cutter effectively confirms silicosis. No cavitation reduces the probability of concomitant active TB, but sputum testing is still indicated given the elevated co-morbid risk.

prompts.label: Pulmonary Function Tests and Sputum Testing
prompts.text: Spirometry: FVC 61% predicted, FEV1 64% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.82 (restrictive pattern). DLCO 55% predicted. Sputum AFB smear: negative on three separate specimens. IGRA (interferon-gamma release assay): negative. Silica exposure assessment confirms levels exceeding safe threshold during working years.
prompts.reasoning: Restrictive spirometry confirms parenchymal (not airway) disease. Negative AFB smears and negative IGRA effectively exclude active TB and TB co-infection. Documented silica overexposure during working years provides the occupational causation link. The complete picture — granite cutter, bilateral upper-zone nodules, eggshell hilar calcification, restrictive PFTs, and negative TB workup — confirms the diagnosis of chronic silicosis. Annual surveillance for development of progressive massive fibrosis and TB is required.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A male infant born at 29 weeks gestation develops grunting, nasal flaring, and increased work of breathing within 30 minutes of birth.
prompts.reasoning: Early-onset respiratory distress in a premature neonate within the first hour of life requires urgent assessment. NRDS, transient tachypnoea, neonatal pneumonia, meconium aspiration, congenital diaphragmatic hernia, and congenital heart disease all present with respiratory distress at birth. The single discriminatory axis is early-onset respiratory distress in a premature infant.

prompts.label: Perinatal History
prompts.text: The mother had an emergency caesarean section due to antepartum haemorrhage. She received a single course of antenatal corticosteroids 36 hours before delivery. The amniotic fluid was clear with no meconium staining.
prompts.reasoning: Emergency caesarean section without labour predisposes to transient tachypnoea of the newborn (TTN) due to failure of resorption of lung fluid. However, TTN is more typical at term or near-term — at 29 weeks, surfactant deficiency (NRDS) is the dominant concern. The partial corticosteroid course (single dose, 36 hours prior) may provide incomplete lung maturation benefit. Clear amniotic fluid excludes meconium aspiration syndrome. Congenital diaphragmatic hernia would typically show bowel sounds in the chest and absent breath sounds.

prompts.label: Gestational and Maternal History
prompts.text: Gestational age was confirmed at 29+2 weeks by first-trimester ultrasound. Maternal Group B Streptococcus (GBS) swab was negative. No maternal fever or chorioamnionitis. Maternal diabetes mellitus is absent.
prompts.reasoning: Negative maternal GBS and absence of maternal fever or chorioamnionitis significantly reduces the probability of early-onset neonatal pneumonia (GBS being the most common pathogen). At 29 weeks, surfactant production by type II pneumocytes is markedly immature — the critical window for surfactant deficiency-related NRDS is before 34 weeks. TTN can occur at 29 weeks but is less likely than NRDS. No maternal diabetes excludes a key risk factor for delayed surfactant maturation.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: RR 74 breaths/min, SpO2 72% in room air (requiring FiO2 0.65 to maintain SpO2 >90%). Audible expiratory grunting. Subcostal and intercostal retractions. Bilateral diminished breath sounds. No bowel sounds in chest. Cardiovascular exam: no murmur, pulses equal.
prompts.reasoning: Severe hypoxia requiring high FiO2 (0.65), expiratory grunting (auto-PEEP mechanism), and subcostal retractions in a 29-week premature infant are consistent with severe NRDS. Grunting in premature neonates is a compensatory mechanism to prevent alveolar collapse between breaths (equivalent to PEEP). Bilaterally diminished breath sounds reflect widespread alveolar collapse from surfactant deficiency. No bowel sounds in chest excludes congenital diaphragmatic hernia. Equal pulses and no murmur make congenital heart disease less likely. CXR is essential.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacification with prominent air bronchograms throughout both lungs. Low lung volumes. Heart border obscured bilaterally. No pleural effusion. Endotracheal tube tip in correct position.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacification with air bronchograms and low lung volumes is the classic CXR appearance of NRDS, reflecting diffuse alveolar collapse and fluid-filled alveoli in the absence of surfactant. Air bronchograms are visible because the major airways remain patent while surrounding alveoli are collapsed. Low lung volumes contrast with the hyperinflation seen in meconium aspiration or TTN (which has a more heterogeneous or hazy appearance with normal/increased volumes). This CXR pattern in a premature infant confirms NRDS.

prompts.label: Arterial Blood Gas and Response to Surfactant
prompts.text: Arterial blood gas before treatment: pH 7.18, PaCO2 68 mmHg, PaO2 42 mmHg, HCO3 19 mmol/L (combined respiratory and metabolic acidosis). Following intratracheal surfactant instillation (200 mg/kg poractant alfa), FiO2 requirement falls from 0.65 to 0.30 within 2 hours, and repeat CXR shows improved lung aeration.
prompts.reasoning: Combined respiratory and metabolic acidosis confirms severe respiratory failure from alveolar hypoventilation and inadequate gas exchange due to surfactant deficiency. The dramatic and rapid improvement in oxygenation (FiO2 reduced by 50%) following exogenous surfactant therapy is the definitive therapeutic confirmation of NRDS — this response is specific to surfactant-deficient states and would not occur in neonatal pneumonia, congenital heart disease, or diaphragmatic hernia. The diagnosis is confirmed: neonatal respiratory distress syndrome due to surfactant deficiency in a 29-week premature infant.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 47-year-old woman presents to her doctor with a 4-month history of progressive breathlessness and dull anterior chest discomfort.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive breathlessness with anterior chest discomfort over 4 months in a middle-aged woman has multiple causes including anterior mediastinal masses (thymoma, lymphoma, germ cell tumour), lung cancer, pericarditis, and aortic pathology. No discriminatory features beyond anterior chest symptoms have been introduced.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She also reports intermittent drooping of her right eyelid and double vision that tends to worsen in the evenings and with sustained activity. She has noticed difficulty chewing tough food towards the end of meals.
prompts.reasoning: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, and jaw fatigue worsening with sustained activity and towards the end of the day are classic features of myasthenia gravis — the most important paraneoplastic association of thymoma. Myasthenia gravis is present in approximately 30–50% of thymoma cases and results from anti-AChR antibodies. Lymphoma and germ cell tumours can cause anterior mediastinal masses but are not associated with myasthenia gravis. The combination of anterior chest symptoms and fatigable ocular and bulbar muscle weakness must raise thymoma as the leading diagnosis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has no known medical conditions, takes no regular medications, and has no family history of neuromuscular or autoimmune disease. She is a non-smoker. She has no prior history of radiotherapy.
prompts.reasoning: No prior immunosuppression or radiotherapy excludes secondary thymic changes. In the context of anterior mediastinal symptoms with possible myasthenia gravis, the leading diagnoses remain thymoma, lymphoma, and germ cell tumour — the three most common causes of anterior mediastinal masses in adults. In middle-aged women, thymoma is the most common cause of an anterior mediastinal mass, while germ cell tumours and T-cell lymphomas are more common in younger males. Smoking history is not relevant here.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: HR 82 bpm, SpO2 96% on room air. Right-sided ptosis present, more pronounced after sustained upgaze for 60 seconds (fatigable ptosis). No palpable neck masses. Mild facial and proximal limb weakness on formal testing. JVP normal. Chest: dull to percussion in the upper anterior chest bilaterally.
prompts.reasoning: Fatigable ptosis — worsening with sustained upgaze — is the cardinal sign of myasthenia gravis and distinguishes neuromuscular fatigue from fixed ptosis (e.g. Horner syndrome, cranial nerve palsy). Proximal limb weakness further supports myasthenia. Anterior chest dullness suggests a retrosternal mass. This combination — anterior mediastinal physical signs with myasthenia gravis features — effectively distinguishes thymoma from lymphoma and germ cell tumour, neither of which is associated with myasthenia. CT imaging of the chest is diagnostic.

prompts.label: CT Thorax
prompts.text: CT thorax with contrast: a well-defined, lobulated, 5 × 4 cm anterior mediastinal mass with smooth margins and internal septations. No invasion of adjacent structures. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Lung fields clear.
prompts.reasoning: A well-defined, lobulated, encapsulated anterior mediastinal mass with smooth margins and no adjacent invasion or lymphadenopathy is the characteristic CT appearance of thymoma. Lymphoma would typically produce bulky bilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy rather than a discrete encapsulated mass. Germ cell tumours often contain fat or calcification (teratoma) or are homogeneous and aggressive (seminoma/non-seminomatous). The encapsulated appearance is a favourable prognostic feature in thymoma, suggesting a potentially resectable lesion.

prompts.label: Anti-AChR Antibodies and Biopsy
prompts.text: Serum anti-acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies: positive (elevated at 12 nmol/L, reference <0.25 nmol/L). Electromyography: decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, confirming neuromuscular junction dysfunction. CT-guided core biopsy: thymoma (WHO Type AB) — mixed population of neoplastic thymic epithelial cells and lymphocytes. No invasion of the capsule.
prompts.reasoning: Highly elevated anti-AChR antibodies confirm autoimmune myasthenia gravis as the paraneoplastic syndrome. The decremental EMG response confirms neuromuscular junction dysfunction. Histopathology confirms WHO Type AB thymoma (mixed type) with an intact capsule (encapsulated thymoma — Masaoka Stage I), which is associated with the best prognosis and is potentially curable by complete surgical resection (thymectomy). The diagnosis is confirmed: encapsulated thymoma with paraneoplastic myasthenia gravis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 61-year-old man is admitted with a 2-week history of right-sided chest pain, fever, and breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: Fever, chest pain, and breathlessness over 2 weeks in a middle-aged man spans infective (pneumonia, empyema, TB, abscess) and non-infective (malignant effusion) diagnoses. The combination of all three symptoms over 2 weeks is the discriminatory axis at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He was treated for community-acquired pneumonia at another hospital 3 weeks ago and was discharged on oral antibiotics, but has remained febrile and his breathlessness has worsened rather than improved since then.
prompts.reasoning: Failure to improve — or deterioration — following adequate antibiotic treatment for pneumonia is a major red flag for a complicated pleural infection. Empyema is the most important complication of bacterial pneumonia in this clinical scenario. Lung abscess can also develop following inadequately treated pneumonia. TB should be considered in pneumonia that does not respond to standard antibiotics. Malignant effusion becomes less likely given the preceding infectious illness, but must not be entirely dismissed.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has type 2 diabetes and is on insulin. He does not smoke. He has no prior history of TB and no known contacts with TB. He completed 7 days of amoxicillin-clavulanate without improvement.
prompts.reasoning: Diabetes is a recognised risk factor for complicated bacterial infections including empyema and lung abscess. Failure to respond to a standard antibiotic course in a diabetic patient with persistent fever and breathlessness following pneumonia strongly suggests a contained suppurative process — either empyema or lung abscess. These require drainage in addition to antibiotic therapy. TB is less likely given the clear acute bacterial pneumonia history, though negative cultures do not exclude it.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.0°C, HR 116 bpm, BP 108/66 mmHg, RR 24 breaths/min, SpO2 91% on room air. Stony dullness to percussion in the right lower hemithorax. Absent breath sounds in the right lower zone. Reduced vocal resonance. No bronchial breathing. No contralateral tracheal deviation.
prompts.reasoning: Stony dullness, absent breath sounds, and reduced vocal resonance in the right lower zone in a febrile patient who has not responded to pneumonia treatment is consistent with a right-sided pleural collection (likely empyema). Absent bronchial breathing (which would suggest consolidation) and stony dullness (which distinguishes fluid from consolidation) point to a fluid-filled pleural space. Lung abscess would more typically produce bronchial breathing and coarse crackles within the parenchyma. Urgent imaging and pleural aspiration are required.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray and Ultrasound
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: large right pleural opacity with a meniscus shape and mediastinal shift to the left. Thoracic ultrasound: complex, echogenic pleural collection with internal septations — features consistent with an exudate or empyema rather than a simple transudate.
prompts.reasoning: A large complex septated pleural collection on ultrasound in a febrile patient who failed antibiotic therapy for pneumonia is highly consistent with empyema. Septations on ultrasound indicate fibrin deposition and organisation — a feature of exudative, infected fluid. Simple transudates (heart failure) appear anechoic. The combination of a post-pneumonia course, persistent fever, treatment failure, and complex septated pleural collection definitively points to empyema. Pleural fluid aspiration and analysis are required to confirm.

prompts.label: Pleural Fluid Analysis
prompts.text: Ultrasound-guided thoracocentesis: frank turbid yellow-green fluid aspirated. pH 6.9, glucose 0.8 mmol/L, LDH 4,200 U/L. White cell count 48,000/µL (predominantly neutrophils). Gram stain: Gram-positive cocci in clusters. Culture: Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). Blood cultures: positive for S. aureus.
prompts.reasoning: Frank pus with pH <7.2, very low glucose, high LDH, and >25,000 neutrophils meets the biochemical and cytological criteria for empyema (BTS guidelines). Staphylococcus aureus cultured from both pleural fluid and blood confirms bacteraemic Staphylococcal empyema, often a complication of haematogenous seeding or direct extension. The very low pH (6.9) indicates a highly acidic, metabolically active infected space requiring chest tube drainage in addition to intravenous antistaphylococcal antibiotics. The diagnosis is confirmed: empyema thoracis due to Staphylococcus aureus.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 16-year-old boy is reviewed in a paediatric respiratory clinic with a lifelong history of recurrent chest infections and poor growth.
prompts.reasoning: Lifelong recurrent chest infections with growth failure in a teenager requires consideration of congenital or hereditary conditions. Cystic fibrosis, primary ciliary dyskinesia, congenital immunodeficiency, bronchiectasis, and coeliac disease (as a cause of malabsorption and growth failure) are all plausible. The lifelong pattern and combined pulmonary-systemic features are the key axes at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has had at least three to four episodes of purulent bronchitis requiring antibiotics each year since infancy. His parents report his stools have always been pale, bulky, and difficult to flush. He has had no improvement with regular bronchodilators.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent purulent bronchitis from infancy combined with steatorrhoea (pale, bulky, difficult to flush stools) from pancreatic exocrine insufficiency is highly characteristic of cystic fibrosis. The involvement of both lungs (recurrent infections) and the gastrointestinal tract (malabsorption) reflects the multiorgan nature of CFTR dysfunction. PCD causes recurrent pulmonary infections but does not cause GI malabsorption. Congenital immunodeficiency can cause recurrent infections but not steatorrhoea. Asthma does not cause recurrent bacterial bronchitis or GI symptoms.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He was diagnosed with meconium ileus at birth requiring surgical intervention. He is of Northern European descent. There is no consanguinity. His younger sister is healthy.
prompts.reasoning: Meconium ileus at birth is a highly specific early presentation of cystic fibrosis, occurring in approximately 15–20% of CF newborns due to abnormally thick meconium from pancreatic insufficiency. Northern European descent is consistent with the highest prevalence of CFTR mutations (F508del is most common in Caucasians, with a carrier frequency of ~1 in 25). PCD is excluded by the meconium ileus — this is not a feature of PCD. The combination of meconium ileus, steatorrhoea, recurrent bronchitis, and growth failure is essentially diagnostic of CF pending genetic confirmation.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Height on 5th centile, weight on 3rd centile (both below expected for age). SpO2 93% on room air. Digital clubbing (grade 3). Bilateral coarse crackles throughout both lung fields. Barrel-shaped chest. Abdomen: mild hepatomegaly.
prompts.reasoning: Growth retardation, severe digital clubbing (grade 3), bilateral coarse crackles, barrel chest (from chronic air trapping), and hepatomegaly (from CF-related liver disease or biliary cirrhosis) are all consistent with established cystic fibrosis in an adolescent. The severity of clubbing (grade 3) reflects chronic hypoxia from persistent bronchiectasis and pulmonary suppuration. Hepatomegaly from CF liver disease further distinguishes this from PCD, which primarily affects the airways and fertility. HRCT and sweat test will confirm the diagnosis.

prompts.label: HRCT Thorax and Sweat Chloride
prompts.text: HRCT thorax: bilateral upper lobe-predominant bronchiectasis with mucus plugging, tree-in-bud nodularity, and peribronchial thickening. Sweat chloride test (pilocarpine iontophoresis): 94 mmol/L (positive, >60 mmol/L confirms CF).
prompts.reasoning: Upper lobe-predominant bronchiectasis on HRCT is characteristic of CF, distinguishing it from post-infectious bronchiectasis (typically lower lobe). Tree-in-bud nodularity reflects small airway mucus plugging and peribronchiolar inflammation. Sweat chloride >60 mmol/L on two separate occasions is the diagnostic gold standard for cystic fibrosis, reflecting defective CFTR chloride channel function. Upper-lobe bronchiectasis distinguishes CF from PCD (which is more uniform) and from post-infectious bronchiectasis (lower lobe). The diagnosis is confirmed: cystic fibrosis.

prompts.label: Genetic Testing and Sputum Culture
prompts.text: CFTR mutation analysis: homozygous F508del mutation (the most common CF-causing mutation). Sputum culture: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (mucoid phenotype). Pulmonary function tests: FEV1 48% predicted (moderate obstruction). Faecal elastase: markedly reduced, confirming pancreatic exocrine insufficiency.
prompts.reasoning: Homozygous F508del mutation is the most common genotype in Caucasian CF patients, confirming the molecular diagnosis. Mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonisation is a hallmark of advanced CF pulmonary disease — the mucoid phenotype is uniquely associated with CF airways and results from alginate overproduction driven by the CF airway microenvironment. Reduced faecal elastase confirms pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, explaining the steatorrhoea and malabsorption. The diagnosis is confirmed: cystic fibrosis (homozygous F508del) with established bronchiectasis, pancreatic insufficiency, and Pseudomonas colonisation.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old man is referred to an ENT clinic by his general practitioner with a 10-week history of persistent hoarseness.
prompts.reasoning: Persistent hoarseness for 10 weeks in a middle-aged man requires urgent evaluation to exclude laryngeal malignancy. The differential includes laryngeal carcinoma, benign vocal cord lesions (nodules, papillomatosis), hypothyroidism causing vocal fold oedema, laryngopharyngeal reflux, and vocal cord palsy from a compressive lesion. Duration of 10 weeks is the single discriminatory axis — benign and functional causes typically resolve within 3 weeks.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The hoarseness has been continuously progressive and he now describes his voice as very rough and quiet. He has also developed mild odynophagia (pain on swallowing) over the past 3 weeks. He has no stridor and no dysphagia to solids.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive hoarseness over 10 weeks that is continuously worsening, combined with odynophagia, requires urgent exclusion of laryngeal malignancy. Odynophagia (painful swallowing) suggests supraglottic or paraglottic involvement. Vocal cord nodules cause intermittent hoarseness that varies with voice use and would not cause odynophagia. Laryngopharyngeal reflux causes hoarseness that is typically worse in the morning and responds to treatment. Progressive worsening over 10 weeks without improvement argues strongly against benign functional causes.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has smoked 30 cigarettes per day for 35 years (52 pack-years) and drinks approximately 30 units of alcohol per week. He works as a school caretaker. He has no prior history of laryngeal surgery or radiation.
prompts.reasoning: Heavy smoking (52 pack-years) and significant alcohol consumption are the two most important risk factors for laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma, and their combination has a synergistic carcinogenic effect. Squamous cell carcinoma accounts for 95% of laryngeal malignancies. Laryngeal papillomatosis (caused by HPV) can produce recurrent hoarseness but is more common in younger patients and is not typically associated with heavy smoking and alcohol. The risk factor profile strongly supports malignancy as the primary concern.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: No stridor. Voice is rough and low volume. Right-sided non-tender 2 cm lymph node in the level III cervical chain. No contralateral lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasendoscopy: an irregular exophytic lesion on the right true vocal cord with restricted cord mobility. Left cord moves normally.
prompts.reasoning: An irregular exophytic lesion on the true vocal cord with restricted cord mobility on flexible nasendoscopy in a heavy smoker with progressive hoarseness is highly indicative of glottic squamous cell carcinoma. Restricted cord mobility indicates involvement of the paraglottic space or intrinsic laryngeal musculature, suggesting at least T2 disease. A right-sided level III cervical lymph node suggests possible regional nodal spread. Vocal cord nodules and papillomatosis would appear as smooth or warty lesions without cord fixity. Histological confirmation under general anaesthesia is required.

prompts.label: CT Neck and Thorax
prompts.text: CT neck with contrast: enhancing irregular mass centred on the right true vocal cord with extension into the right paraglottic space and subglottis. No thyroid cartilage invasion. Right level III lymph node 1.8 cm with ill-defined margins. No distant metastases.
prompts.reasoning: CT confirms the extent of the right glottic mass with paraglottic and subglottic extension (T3 disease if cord fixation is confirmed), and a suspicious ipsilateral cervical lymph node (N1 disease). Absence of thyroid cartilage invasion on CT is a favourable finding for potential organ-preservation therapy. No distant metastases suggest potentially curable locoregional disease. CT chest excluded a synchronous primary lung cancer — important in a heavy smoker. The clinical and radiological picture establishes laryngeal SCC as the diagnosis pending biopsy.

prompts.label: Biopsy Under General Anaesthesia
prompts.text: Microlaryngoscopy under general anaesthesia: right glottic lesion biopsied. Histopathology: moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with keratin pearls. p16 immunostaining: negative (HPV-unrelated). Staging: T3N1M0, Stage III laryngeal SCC.
prompts.reasoning: Histopathology confirms moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx, consistent with tobacco-alcohol-related carcinogenesis. Negative p16 (a surrogate marker for HPV infection) confirms this is HPV-unrelated laryngeal SCC — the typical tobacco-driven phenotype. Stage III disease (T3N1M0) indicates locally advanced disease with restricted cord mobility and ipsilateral nodal involvement, managed with concurrent chemoradiotherapy or total laryngectomy. The diagnosis is confirmed: HPV-negative Stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the glottic larynx.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 12-hour history of severe sore throat and rapidly worsening difficulty swallowing.
prompts.reasoning: Severe sore throat with rapidly progressive dysphagia over 12 hours requires urgent exclusion of deep space airway infections and supraglottic pathology. Acute epiglottitis, peritonsillar abscess, retropharyngeal abscess, Ludwig's angina, severe pharyngitis, and foreign body ingestion all present with throat pain and swallowing difficulty. The rapid progression over 12 hours is the key discriminatory axis at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He describes the pain as severe and constant, located deep in the throat rather than at the tonsils. He has developed noisy breathing in the last 2 hours and is now drooling because swallowing is too painful. He has a high fever.
prompts.reasoning: Drooling (inability to swallow secretions), deep throat pain rather than tonsillar pain, and new-onset noisy breathing indicate supraglottic or deep pharyngeal obstruction. Peritonsillar abscess and pharyngitis cause more localised tonsillar/peritonsillar pain without this degree of airway compromise. Retropharyngeal abscess and Ludwig's angina can both produce deep throat pain with airway compromise. The absence of neck swelling or dental history is needed to differentiate further.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has no known dental problems and no recent dental procedures. He received childhood vaccinations in Singapore but has no documentation of specific vaccines. He is immunocompetent, takes no medications, and is a non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of dental disease or recent dental procedures makes Ludwig's angina (which typically originates from mandibular dental infection) less likely. Retropharyngeal abscess and epiglottitis remain the leading candidates for deep-space infection with airway compromise in an adult. Childhood vaccination history in Singapore likely includes Hib, but adult epiglottitis can be caused by other organisms (including Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Candida in immunocompromised patients). Examination of the airway and neck is the critical next step.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.6°C, HR 124 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, SpO2 95% on room air. Patient is sitting upright in a tripod position, drooling, and speaking in a muffled 'hot potato' voice. Inspiratory stridor is audible. Anterior neck examination is normal with no submandibular or anterior neck swelling. Oropharynx appears normal on careful inspection.
prompts.reasoning: The tripod position, drooling, muffled 'hot potato' voice, and inspiratory stridor in a toxic-appearing adult constitute the classic presentation of acute epiglottitis. The tripod position (leaning forward on hands) maximises supraglottic airway diameter and is a hallmark of impending supraglottic obstruction. A normal oropharynx excludes peritonsillar and tonsillar pathology as the primary source. Normal anterior neck examination makes Ludwig's angina and retropharyngeal abscess less likely. Direct examination of the epiglottis must not be attempted without airway backup — imaging is required.

prompts.label: Lateral Neck X-Ray
prompts.text: Lateral neck X-ray: marked thickening and rounding of the epiglottis producing an enlarged thumb-shaped supraglottic shadow. Normal subglottic airway. No retropharyngeal soft tissue thickening.
prompts.reasoning: The 'thumbprint sign' — a markedly swollen, rounded epiglottis visible on lateral neck X-ray — is the classic radiological sign of acute epiglottitis, resulting from oedema of the epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds. Normal subglottic airway distinguishes this from croup (which produces subglottic narrowing). No retropharyngeal soft tissue thickening excludes retropharyngeal abscess (which would show prevertebral soft tissue widening). This confirms acute epiglottitis requiring immediate airway management.

prompts.label: Blood Cultures and Throat Swab
prompts.text: Blood cultures taken following airway securing in the operating theatre: Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Streptococcus) isolated. Throat swab culture: Group A Streptococcus. CRP: 248 mg/L. Flexible laryngoscopy (post-intubation): markedly oedematous, cherry-red epiglottis confirmed.
prompts.reasoning: Group A Streptococcus is among the important bacterial causes of epiglottitis in vaccinated adults (alongside Staphylococcus aureus and non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae). The airway was secured first as per protocol before any manipulation or investigation of the oropharynx. Laryngoscopic confirmation of a cherry-red, swollen epiglottis following intubation establishes the definitive diagnosis. The diagnosis is confirmed: acute bacterial epiglottitis due to Group A Streptococcus.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 9-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric emergency department with a 3-day history of sore throat, low-grade fever, and a hoarse voice.
prompts.reasoning: Sore throat, hoarseness, and low-grade fever over 3 days in a child has a broad differential. Pharyngitis (viral or streptococcal), infectious mononucleosis, croup, peritonsillar abscess, epiglottitis, and diphtheria are all plausible at this stage. The combination of sore throat and hoarseness is the discriminatory axis — the 3-day subacute course is noted.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Her parents report she has been increasingly lethargic over the past 2 days and is refusing to eat. They noticed a whitish coating in her throat yesterday. She has no barking cough and no sudden onset of symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: A whitish coating in the throat combined with progressive lethargy and dysphagia in a child is an important finding. Infectious mononucleosis can produce a white tonsillar exudate. Peritonsillar abscess produces a white-yellow unilateral exudate. Diphtheria produces a distinctive membrane in the throat, but early in the disease it may not be clearly characterised. Absence of barking cough and gradual onset makes croup less likely. Epiglottitis does not produce visible throat coating.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Review of her vaccination record shows she received only one dose of diphtheria-containing vaccine at 2 months of age, with subsequent doses missed due to her family's relocations across multiple countries with poor vaccine infrastructure.
prompts.reasoning: Incomplete diphtheria vaccination (one of five required doses) leaves the child with inadequate immunological protection against Corynebacterium diphtheriae. This is the single most critical risk factor for diphtheria in the modern era, where the disease is almost exclusively seen in unvaccinated or under-vaccinated individuals. Infectious mononucleosis remains in the differential as vaccination status does not affect EBV susceptibility. Peritonsillar abscess becomes less likely given the subacute generalised illness without localised fluctuance.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.1°C, HR 118 bpm, BP 88/56 mmHg. Child appears pale and lethargic. A grey-white membrane is visible adherent to both tonsils and the posterior pharynx; attempts to remove it with a spatula cause bleeding. Bilateral soft cervical lymphadenopathy producing a 'bull neck' appearance. Mild inspiratory stridor.
prompts.reasoning: A grey-white firmly adherent pharyngeal membrane that bleeds on attempted removal is pathognomonic of diphtheria. This fibrinopurulent pseudomembrane is composed of necrotic epithelium, fibrin, and bacteria and is distinct from the removable white exudate of streptococcal pharyngitis or mononucleosis. Bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy producing a 'bull neck' appearance and early stridor reflecting extension toward the larynx confirm significant diphtheria. Hypotension suggests early cardiac involvement from diphtheria exotoxin. This is a notifiable disease requiring immediate isolation.

prompts.label: Throat Swab and Toxin Testing
prompts.text: Throat swab culture on Loeffler's and tellurite selective media: growth of Gram-positive club-shaped bacilli with metachromatic granules on Albert's stain, arranged in 'Chinese letter' formations. Elek immunoprecipitation test: positive for diphtheria toxin production.
prompts.reasoning: Isolation of toxigenic Corynebacterium diphtheriae on selective media with characteristic morphology (club-shaped bacilli, metachromatic granules, Chinese letter arrangement) combined with a positive Elek test confirming toxin production establishes the definitive microbiological diagnosis. The Elek test is the gold-standard method for detecting diphtheria toxin. The diagnosis is confirmed: respiratory diphtheria due to toxigenic Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Diphtheria antitoxin must be administered urgently on clinical grounds without waiting for microbiological confirmation.

prompts.label: ECG and Cardiac Monitoring
prompts.text: 12-lead ECG: prolonged PR interval (0.24 sec) and first-degree AV block. Serum troponin: mildly elevated. Echocardiogram: mildly impaired LV systolic function (LVEF 48%). Nasal swab for close contacts: two household members identified as asymptomatic carriers.
prompts.reasoning: First-degree AV block, mild troponin elevation, and reduced LVEF confirm diphtheric myocarditis — the most dangerous systemic complication of diphtheria exotoxin, which acts by inhibiting protein synthesis (ADP-ribosylation of EF-2). Myocarditis occurs in approximately 10–25% of diphtheria cases and is the leading cause of diphtheria mortality. Identification of asymptomatic household carriers underscores the need for contact tracing, erythromycin prophylaxis, and vaccination catch-up. The diagnosis is confirmed: respiratory diphtheria with diphtheric myocarditis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 17-year-old student presents to a GP clinic with a 2-day history of sore throat and difficulty swallowing.
prompts.reasoning: Acute sore throat and odynophagia over 2 days in a teenager has a broad differential. Streptococcal pharyngitis, viral pharyngitis, infectious mononucleosis, peritonsillar abscess, epiglottitis, and diphtheria are all possible. The 2-day duration and age are the only discriminatory features at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has a high fever and headache but no cough, no rhinorrhoea, and no nasal congestion. She has not had any difficulty breathing and has no drooling. Her symptoms began abruptly.
prompts.reasoning: Abrupt onset severe sore throat with high fever, headache, and notably absent cough and coryza ('absence of coryza' is a Centor criterion) is the hallmark pattern that raises bacterial pharyngitis above viral. Viral pharyngitis is typically associated with rhinorrhoea and cough. Absence of breathing difficulty and drooling makes epiglottitis and diphtheria very unlikely. Infectious mononucleosis and peritonsillar abscess can present similarly and remain in the differential.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has no prior history of recurrent tonsillitis. She is fully vaccinated. Two of her classmates have had similar sore throat illnesses this week. She takes no regular medications.
prompts.reasoning: A small classroom cluster of sore throat illnesses is consistent with Group A Streptococcus (GAS) transmission in close-contact settings, a recognised pattern during school outbreaks. EBV mononucleosis can also cluster in schools, particularly among adolescents. No prior recurrent tonsillitis makes peritonsillar abscess less likely as a de novo event. Full vaccination status and absence of immunocompromise makes diphtheria effectively excluded. All three differentials remain plausible pending examination.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.9°C, HR 102 bpm. Markedly erythematous and swollen tonsils with bilateral white exudate. Uvula is midline. Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally (level II nodes). No posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. No hepatosplenomegaly. No skin rash.
prompts.reasoning: Tonsillar exudate with tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, high fever, and absent cough gives a Centor score of 4, indicating high probability of Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis. However, infectious mononucleosis can produce an identical examination picture. Key differentiating features at this stage: posterior cervical lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly would favour EBV, which are absent here. Midline uvula and no peritonsillar bulge excludes peritonsillar abscess. A Monospot test will help differentiate.

prompts.label: Rapid Antigen Test and Monospot
prompts.text: Rapid streptococcal antigen detection test (RADT): positive for Group A Streptococcus antigen. Monospot (heterophile antibody) test: negative. FBC: white cell count 11.2 × 10⁹/L with neutrophilia (82%). No atypical lymphocytes on film.
prompts.reasoning: A positive RADT for GAS antigen has a specificity of approximately 98–99%, making a false positive very unlikely. A negative Monospot test effectively excludes acute EBV infectious mononucleosis (though early in illness sensitivity is reduced — this is assessed further in P6). Neutrophilia on FBC supports a bacterial rather than viral aetiology. Atypical lymphocytosis would be expected in EBV mononucleosis. The diagnosis is strongly consistent with Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis.

prompts.label: Throat Culture and ASO Titre
