prompts.text: Throat swab culture on blood agar: beta-haemolytic colonies identified as Group A Streptococcus (Lancefield Group A, bacitracin sensitive). ASO (antistreptolysin O) titre: elevated at 480 IU/mL (reference <200 IU/mL). EBV VCA IgM: negative.
prompts.reasoning: Culture confirmation of beta-haemolytic Group A Streptococcus from throat swab is the gold standard for streptococcal pharyngitis. Elevated ASO titre confirms a streptococcal immune response. Negative EBV VCA IgM definitively excludes acute EBV infectious mononucleosis. The diagnosis is confirmed: acute Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis. Antibiotic treatment with phenoxymethylpenicillin is indicated to prevent rheumatic fever, which can complicate untreated GAS pharyngitis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old woman presents to a GP clinic with a 3-day history of runny nose, nasal congestion, and sneezing.
prompts.reasoning: Acute nasal symptoms over 3 days in a young adult requires differentiation between infective, allergic, and structural causes. Infective rhinitis, allergic rhinitis, sinusitis, influenza, COVID-19, and vasomotor rhinitis are all plausible. The 3-day course and nasal symptom complex are the discriminatory axes at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has a sore throat, watery eyes, and a low-grade fever since yesterday. The nasal discharge was initially clear and watery but has become slightly thicker over the last day. She has no significant facial pain and no severe headache.
prompts.reasoning: Low-grade fever with sore throat, watery eyes, and nasal symptoms progressing from clear to slightly thickened discharge over days is typical of viral upper respiratory tract infection. Influenza and COVID-19 can both cause upper respiratory symptoms. Allergic rhinitis causes clear watery discharge without fever or sore throat. Vasomotor rhinitis lacks systemic features. Sinusitis would more typically cause facial pain and pressure. Influenza tends to produce more severe systemic symptoms (high fever, myalgia) than this presentation suggests.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She works in a childcare centre. Two children in her classroom had similar symptoms last week. She has no history of seasonal allergies or eczema. She is up to date with her vaccinations including influenza.
prompts.reasoning: Occupational exposure in childcare with antecedent illness in contacts and a consistent incubation period supports viral transmission. Common cold viruses (rhinovirus, coronavirus, adenovirus) are the most prevalent causes of infective rhinitis and spread readily in childcare settings. Up-to-date influenza vaccination significantly reduces the probability of influenza, although breakthrough infections are possible. COVID-19 remains in the differential given its similar presentation. The mild severity and predominant nasal symptoms favour a common cold over COVID-19, which more frequently causes anosmia and lower respiratory symptoms.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.6°C, HR 78 bpm, RR 16 breaths/min, SpO2 99% on room air. Bilateral nasal mucosal oedema and erythema with clear-to-slightly-turbid discharge. Mild bilateral conjunctival injection. Throat mildly erythematous, no exudate. Tonsils normal. No facial tenderness over sinuses. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Chest clear.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral nasal mucosal oedema, mild conjunctival injection, and throat erythema without exudate in the absence of sinus tenderness, lymphadenopathy, or lower respiratory signs constitute the classic examination findings of uncomplicated viral infective rhinitis. Absence of sinus tenderness makes acute bacterial sinusitis unlikely. Clear chest excludes lower respiratory tract extension. A normal SpO2 and mild fever argue against COVID-19 with lower tract involvement. The clinical picture is consistent with a common cold from a community-transmitted respiratory virus.

prompts.label: Rapid Antigen Testing
prompts.text: SARS-CoV-2 rapid antigen test (RAT): negative on two tests performed 24 hours apart. Influenza A/B rapid antigen test: negative. Nasopharyngeal multiplex respiratory PCR panel: rhinovirus type C detected. No bacterial pathogens isolated.
prompts.reasoning: Negative SARS-CoV-2 and influenza rapid antigen tests on two sequential occasions effectively exclude COVID-19 and influenza as the cause of the current illness. Detection of rhinovirus type C on multiplex PCR confirms viral infective rhinitis — rhinovirus is the most common cause of the common cold globally, responsible for approximately 30–50% of adult upper respiratory illnesses. No bacterial pathogens detected. The diagnosis is confirmed: viral infective rhinitis due to rhinovirus, requiring only symptomatic management.

prompts.label: Clinical Follow-Up and Allergy Testing
prompts.text: Symptoms resolve fully within 7 days without antibiotic treatment. Allergen skin-prick testing performed 6 weeks later (at patient's request due to concern about recurrence): negative for house dust mite, grass pollen, pet dander, and mould. Total serum IgE: within normal limits.
prompts.reasoning: Complete resolution within 7 days is consistent with the natural history of viral rhinitis, which is self-limiting as the innate and adaptive immune responses clear the infection. Negative allergy testing and normal IgE definitively exclude allergic rhinitis as an underlying or contributing diagnosis. This rules out the possibility that the acute episode was an allergic event precipitated by a new environmental allergen. The diagnosis is confirmed: self-limiting viral infective rhinitis, without an allergic component.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 84-year-old woman in a nursing home is brought to hospital by ambulance with a 2-day history of increasing confusion and cough.
prompts.reasoning: Acute confusion with cough in an elderly nursing home resident requires broad consideration. Bronchopneumonia, aspiration pneumonia, UTI causing delirium, heart failure, PE, and stroke are all relevant. Confusion may be the only presenting feature of severe infection in the elderly ('atypical presentation'). The two-day course and dual symptoms (cognitive change plus respiratory) are the discriminatory axes.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Nursing staff report she has been off her food for 3 days, has had a low-grade fever, and her cough has produced some yellow-green phlegm. She has no urinary symptoms and has not complained of chest pain or leg swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Yellow-green productive cough with fever and reduced oral intake is consistent with a pulmonary infective process. Aspiration pneumonia is particularly important in elderly nursing home residents with potential swallowing difficulties. UTI becomes less likely without urinary symptoms. Heart failure can produce cough but not typically productive purulent sputum. PE is less likely without leg swelling or pleuritic pain. The infective focus appears pulmonary.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has advanced dementia, type 2 diabetes, and a prior ischaemic stroke with mild residual dysphagia. She is bedbound and requires full nursing care. She has been hospitalised twice in the past year for similar presentations.
prompts.reasoning: Dementia, residual post-stroke dysphagia, and being bedbound constitute the classic triad of risk factors for aspiration pneumonia — impaired consciousness and swallow reflex allow oropharyngeal contents to enter the lower airways. However, debility and bedbound status also predispose to bronchopneumonia from haematogenous spread of organisms or impaired ciliary clearance. Recurrent hospital admissions for similar presentations suggest a chronic underlying predisposition. Bronchopneumonia and aspiration pneumonia remain the two leading diagnoses — physical examination and imaging will distinguish them.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.4°C, HR 106 bpm, BP 104/66 mmHg, RR 26 breaths/min, SpO2 88% on room air. Patient is confused (GCS 13/15). Bilateral scattered crackles in the lower and mid zones on both sides. Dullness to percussion in the right lower zone only. No wheeze. No peripheral oedema.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral scattered crackles in a diffuse multifocal pattern with only localised dullness — rather than homogeneous lobar dullness — is consistent with bronchopneumonia, which characteristically produces patchy, multifocal consolidation centred around bronchi rather than a homogeneous lobar distribution. Aspiration pneumonia typically produces localised consolidation in a dependent lung segment (right lower or middle lobe) rather than bilateral scattered crackles. The bilateral multifocal pattern in a debilitated, bedridden elderly patient favours bronchopneumonia.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: bilateral patchy airspace opacities predominantly in the lower zones, with no single lobe completely consolidated. Right lower zone most prominently affected, with additional patchy changes in the left lower zone. No pleural effusion. No cardiomegaly.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral patchy, multifocal lower-zone airspace opacities without lobar consolidation is the characteristic CXR appearance of bronchopneumonia. This reflects the pathological pattern of bronchopneumonia — peribronchial consolidation radiating outward from the bronchi in multiple foci, in contrast to lobar pneumonia's homogeneous single-lobe opacification. Aspiration pneumonia would produce a more localised consolidation in a specific dependent segment. The bilateral lower-zone patchy pattern in a debilitated, bedbound elderly patient confirms bronchopneumonia.

prompts.label: Blood Cultures and Sputum
prompts.text: Blood cultures: Klebsiella pneumoniae isolated (2 of 2 bottles). Sputum Gram stain: Gram-negative bacilli predominant. Sputum culture: Klebsiella pneumoniae, sensitive to third-generation cephalosporins. CRP: 312 mg/L. WBC: 18.4 × 10⁹/L with left shift.
prompts.reasoning: Klebsiella pneumoniae bacteraemia with matching sputum isolate confirms a Gram-negative bronchopneumonia. Klebsiella is an important pathogen in elderly debilitated or institutionalised patients with aspiration risk, where it can cause both bronchopneumonia and lobar pneumonia. Elevated CRP and neutrophilia with left shift confirm significant systemic bacterial infection. The diagnosis is confirmed: Klebsiella bronchopneumonia in an elderly bedbound patient with dementia and dysphagia. IV antibiotics, aspiration precautions, and goals-of-care discussion are required.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 67-year-old man who is on day 6 of admission following elective sigmoid colectomy develops new fever and worsening breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: New fever and breathlessness on post-operative day 6 requires systematic consideration of common post-surgical complications. HAP, PE, aspiration pneumonia, atelectasis, anastomotic leak, and UTI are all relevant at this stage. The temporal relationship to surgery and the dual features of fever and breathlessness are the key discriminatory axes.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has developed a productive cough yielding yellow sputum over the past 24 hours. He had been managed with a nasogastric tube for post-operative ileus and required brief supplemental oxygen on days 2 and 3. He has no calf swelling and no pleuritic chest pain.
prompts.reasoning: New productive cough with purulent sputum developing on post-operative day 6 in a patient who had a nasogastric tube (aspiration risk) and supplemental oxygen requirements points toward a pulmonary infective process. HAP and aspiration pneumonia are the leading candidates. Atelectasis causes fever and dyspnoea in the early post-operative period (days 1–3) but does not typically produce purulent sputum. PE remains less likely without pleuritic pain or leg swelling, though immobilisation is a risk factor.

prompts.label: Clinical Background
prompts.text: He was intubated for 14 hours post-operatively in the ICU before extubation. He has type 2 diabetes and was treated with prophylactic antibiotics (cefazolin) perioperatively, which were discontinued on post-operative day 2.
prompts.reasoning: Post-operative mechanical ventilation (14 hours) is a major risk factor for ventilator-associated pneumonia and hospital-acquired pneumonia. The disruption of normal airway defences during intubation allows colonisation with hospital flora. Diabetes further impairs immune defences. HAP developing after day 4–5 (late-onset HAP) is more likely to involve Gram-negative organisms (Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, Enterobacter) or MRSA. Aspiration pneumonia remains plausible given the NG tube exposure. The 6-day timeline and ICU stay confirm this as HAP or ventilator-associated pneumonia by definition.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.1°C, HR 112 bpm, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 28 breaths/min, SpO2 90% on room air. Dullness to percussion in the right lower zone. Bronchial breath sounds and increased vocal resonance in the right lower zone. No wheeze. No leg swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Right lower zone consolidation (bronchial breath sounds, dullness, increased vocal resonance) developing in a post-operative patient with an ICU stay and NG tube is consistent with HAP or aspiration pneumonia. However, the right lower lobe is the most common site for both HAP and aspiration pneumonia — differentiation requires imaging and microbiological data. The bilateral-lobar consolidation pattern of classic community-acquired pneumonia is not present here. Atelectasis would produce reduced (not bronchial) breath sounds. Imaging is required.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: new dense airspace consolidation in the right lower lobe with air bronchograms. No pleural effusion. Comparison with the admission CXR confirms this is a new finding. No cardiomegaly.
prompts.reasoning: New lobar consolidation confirmed on CXR in comparison with the admission baseline, developing on hospital day 6 in a post-operative patient, meets the radiological criterion for hospital-acquired pneumonia (new infiltrate ≥48 hours after admission). The right lower lobe location is anatomically consistent with dependent aspiration and HAP. Absence of pre-existing infiltrate on admission CXR excludes aspiration prior to admission. The clinical picture — fever, productive cough, new consolidation >48 hours post-admission — confirms HAP by CDC definition.

prompts.label: Sputum Culture and Blood Cultures
prompts.text: Endotracheal sputum aspirate culture: Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolated (>10⁵ CFU/mL), sensitive to piperacillin-tazobactam and meropenem, resistant to ceftazidime. Blood cultures: negative. CRP: 278 mg/L. Procalcitonin: 4.2 ng/mL (elevated, consistent with bacterial infection).
prompts.reasoning: Pseudomonas aeruginosa at >10⁵ CFU/mL from a lower respiratory tract specimen meets microbiological criteria for significant respiratory infection. Pseudomonas is among the most important Gram-negative pathogens in late-onset HAP (>5 days), particularly in patients with prior antibiotics, ICU exposure, and structural lung disease risk. Elevated procalcitonin (>2 ng/mL) supports bacterial over viral aetiology. Negative blood cultures are typical of HAP — bacteraemia occurs less frequently than in community-acquired pneumonia. The diagnosis is confirmed: late-onset HAP due to Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his daughter with a 3-day history of fever and productive cough.
prompts.reasoning: Fever and productive cough over 3 days in an elderly man has a broad differential including aspiration pneumonia, CAP, bronchopneumonia, TB, heart failure, and lung abscess. The 3-day course and febrile respiratory symptoms are the only discriminatory features at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His daughter reports he had a choking episode 5 days ago during dinner and seemed unwell since. The sputum has been brownish with an unpleasant odour. He has no haemoptysis and no night sweats.
prompts.reasoning: A choking event 5 days prior followed by fever and foul-smelling brown sputum raises strong suspicion for aspiration of oropharyngeal contents. The 5-day interval reflects the incubation period for aspiration pneumonia. However, foul sputum alone does not exclude CAP with poor oral hygiene, and lung abscess can develop from aspiration — full history is needed to determine the degree of aspiration risk. TB and heart failure are now less likely. The three remaining diagnoses all involve pulmonary infection or necrosis requiring further characterisation.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He had a left hemispheric stroke 3 months ago resulting in mild dysphagia, which has been under speech therapy review. He lives at home and takes aspirin and atorvastatin. He is a non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: Post-stroke dysphagia is one of the most important predisposing factors for aspiration pneumonia — impaired swallowing coordination allows food and secretions to enter the airway. Lung abscess can develop as a complication of aspiration if not treated early. CAP becomes less likely now that a clear aspiration mechanism is established: the respiratory illness has a definite structural cause rather than community-acquired pathogens. The aspiration event combined with post-stroke dysphagia makes aspiration pneumonia or early abscess the two leading diagnoses.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.7°C, HR 108 bpm, RR 22 breaths/min, SpO2 92% on room air. Dullness to percussion and bronchial breath sounds in the right lower and middle zones. Decreased vocal resonance in the same areas. Foul-smelling breath. No peripheral oedema.
prompts.reasoning: Right lower and middle zone consolidation is the anatomically expected location for aspiration pneumonia — when aspiration occurs in the semi-recumbent position (eating), the right lower and middle lobes are most affected due to the dependent anatomy of the right lower lobe bronchus. Foul-smelling breath confirms anaerobic oral flora infection. Absence of focal hyper-resonance or any sign of cavity formation argues against established lung abscess at this stage. The clinical picture is consistent with aspiration pneumonia: consolidation in the dependent right zones following a witnessed aspiration event in a patient with post-stroke dysphagia.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: right lower and middle lobe consolidation. No cavitation. No pleural effusion. Left lung clear.
prompts.reasoning: Right lower and middle lobe consolidation on CXR without cavitation is consistent with early aspiration pneumonia. Absence of cavitation confirms that lung abscess formation has not yet occurred, which is an important management distinction — aspiration pneumonia can progress to abscess in 8–10% of cases if untreated. Left lung clarity is consistent with the witnessed right-side aspiration event. The CXR pattern and clinical history confirm the diagnosis, and antibiotics covering anaerobic organisms are required.

prompts.label: Sputum Culture and Swallow Assessment
prompts.text: Sputum culture: mixed growth including Fusobacterium nucleatum, Prevotella intermedia, and Streptococcus anginosus (anaerobic oral flora). Blood cultures: negative. CRP: 198 mg/L. Formal modified barium swallow assessment: silent aspiration confirmed on thin liquids with impaired pharyngeal phase.
prompts.reasoning: Mixed anaerobic oral flora on sputum culture confirms the microbiological pattern of aspiration pneumonia — the oropharyngeal anaerobic flora (Fusobacterium, Prevotella, Streptococcus anginosus group) are the characteristic pathogens when aspirated material from the oropharynx reaches the lungs. Modified barium swallow confirms silent aspiration on thin liquids — silent aspiration (aspiration without coughing or choking) is particularly dangerous as it bypasses the protective airway reflex. The diagnosis is confirmed: aspiration pneumonia secondary to post-stroke silent aspiration.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 47-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 4-day history of fever, cough, and feeling generally unwell.
prompts.reasoning: Fever, cough, and systemic illness over 4 days in a middle-aged woman is a common presentation with a wide differential. CAP, influenza, COVID-19, TB, atypical pneumonia, and cardiac causes all need consideration. The 4-day course and febrile respiratory illness are the discriminatory axes at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The cough is productive of green sputum and is associated with right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began 2 days ago. She has rigors and has had to take paracetamol every 4 hours. She has no haemoptysis and no weight loss.
prompts.reasoning: Purulent sputum, pleuritic chest pain, and rigors over 4 days are characteristic of acute bacterial pneumonia. Rigors indicate bacteraemic illness. Influenza and COVID-19 more typically cause dry cough without the localised pleuritic component. TB is less likely with only 4 days of symptoms and absent systemic weight loss. Both typical CAP and atypical pneumonia remain in the differential — atypical organisms can occasionally produce productive cough, though dry cough is more characteristic.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has well-controlled type 2 diabetes. She does not smoke and has no prior lung disease. She works as an accountant in an office. She received influenza vaccination 6 months ago but has not received pneumococcal vaccination.
prompts.reasoning: Diabetes is a risk factor for more severe CAP and for Klebsiella pneumonia in particular. No prior vaccinations against Streptococcus pneumoniae leaves her susceptible to the most common CAP pathogen. Office environment without specific occupational exposures does not suggest TB or atypical organism exposure. Influenza vaccination further reduces influenza probability. The clinical picture — purulent sputum, pleuritic pain, rigors, diabetes, no prior pneumococcal vaccination — strongly suggests bacterial CAP.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.3°C, HR 118 bpm, BP 106/70 mmHg, RR 28 breaths/min, SpO2 92% on room air. Dullness to percussion over the right lower zone. Bronchial breath sounds and increased vocal resonance in the same area. No wheeze. Mild bibasal crackles. No peripheral oedema.
prompts.reasoning: Right lower zone consolidation (bronchial breath sounds, dullness, increased vocal resonance) in a febrile patient with purulent sputum, pleuritic pain, and rigors confirms lobar or segmental pneumonia. Atypical pneumonia characteristically produces fewer localising signs on examination relative to the radiological changes. The focal consolidation with classic signs of lobar involvement narrows the diagnosis to typical bacterial CAP. CURB-65 scoring (confusion absent, urea required, RR 28, BP 106/70, age <65) should guide disposition. CXR is required to characterise the extent.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: right lower lobe segmental consolidation with an air bronchogram. No pleural effusion. No hilar lymphadenopathy. No other zones affected.
prompts.reasoning: Right lower lobe segmental consolidation with air bronchogram confirms pneumonia. The pattern is consistent with typical bacterial pneumonia causing lobar alveolar filling. This confirms the clinical diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia. Blood and sputum cultures are required to identify the causative organism and guide targeted antibiotic therapy, particularly in a patient with diabetes who may have an atypical organism or Gram-negative pathogen.

prompts.label: Blood Cultures and Urine Antigen
prompts.text: Blood cultures: Streptococcus pneumoniae isolated from 2 of 2 sets. Urine pneumococcal antigen: positive. Sputum Gram stain: Gram-positive diplococci in pairs. CRP: 312 mg/L. CURB-65 score: 2 (RR ≥30, BP <90 systolic) — moderate severity.
prompts.reasoning: Blood culture isolation of Streptococcus pneumoniae with concurrent positive urinary pneumococcal antigen and matching Gram stain confirms pneumococcal bacteraemic community-acquired pneumonia. S. pneumoniae is the most common identifiable cause of CAP requiring hospitalisation globally. CURB-65 score of 2 indicates moderate severity requiring hospital admission with IV antibiotics. The diagnosis is confirmed: moderately severe community-acquired pneumonia due to Streptococcus pneumoniae.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of fever and worsening breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: Fever and progressive dyspnoea over 5 days in a middle-aged man requires consideration of infective and non-infective causes. Influenza pneumonia, COVID-19, CAP, atypical pneumonia, ARDS, and PE are all plausible at this stage. The 5-day course without further characterisation is the single discriminatory axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: His illness began abruptly with high fever, severe myalgia, headache, and dry cough on day 1. Over the past 2 days the breathlessness has rapidly worsened and he now has difficulty completing a sentence. He has no pleuritic chest pain.
prompts.reasoning: Abrupt onset of high fever with severe myalgia and headache ('influenza-like illness') followed by rapid respiratory deterioration over days is the characteristic pattern of influenza complicated by pneumonia. Community-acquired bacterial pneumonia does not typically produce the abrupt systemic illness prodrome of influenza. COVID-19 can present similarly. Atypical pneumonia typically has a more gradual systemic onset. PE does not produce febrile prodrome. The influenza-like illness pattern followed by progressive respiratory failure strongly raises viral pneumonia.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has morbid obesity (BMI 42 kg/m²) and type 2 diabetes. He did not receive the current season's influenza vaccination. He works as a bus driver and uses public transport regularly. No known sick contacts with similar illness.
prompts.reasoning: Morbid obesity and diabetes are the two strongest risk factors for severe influenza pneumonia (both are associated with impaired immune response and mechanical respiratory compromise). Absent influenza vaccination and high community exposure (bus driver, public transport) increase acquisition risk during influenza season. COVID-19 shares these risk factors. The risk factor profile is consistent with both diagnoses — testing is required. CAP is now less likely given the classic influenza prodrome.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.8°C, HR 126 bpm, RR 32 breaths/min, SpO2 87% on room air. Patient using accessory muscles. Bilateral diffuse crackles throughout all zones. No focal dullness to percussion. No wheeze. No leg swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Severe hypoxia with bilateral diffuse crackles and no focal percussion changes indicates bilateral interstitial or alveolar disease — consistent with viral pneumonia affecting both lungs diffusely rather than lobar bacterial consolidation. This degree of bilateral involvement and hypoxia severity (SpO2 87%) in a previously well man raises concern for progression to ARDS. Both influenza and COVID-19 cause this bilateral pattern. Differentiation requires virological testing.

prompts.label: Rapid Viral Testing and CXR
prompts.text: SARS-CoV-2 rapid antigen test: negative (confirmed by nasopharyngeal PCR, also negative). Influenza A rapid antigen test: positive. Chest X-ray: bilateral patchy airspace opacities in a perihilar and lower-zone distribution. P/F ratio: 168 mmHg (moderate ARDS threshold).
prompts.reasoning: Positive influenza A rapid antigen test with negative COVID-19 confirms influenza A as the causative viral pathogen. Bilateral patchy airspace opacities on CXR with a P/F ratio of 168 mmHg meets the Berlin Definition criteria for moderate ARDS, indicating severe influenza pneumonia with secondary diffuse alveolar damage. This is the most serious complication of influenza, occurring through direct viral pneumonitis or secondary bacterial superinfection. Early oseltamivir treatment and supportive care are required.

prompts.label: Nasopharyngeal PCR and Bacterial Cultures
prompts.text: Nasopharyngeal PCR: influenza A (H3N2) confirmed. Sputum culture: Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) isolated from sputum on day 3 of admission, consistent with secondary bacterial superinfection. Blood cultures: negative. Serum ferritin: elevated at 2,840 ng/mL.
prompts.reasoning: Nasopharyngeal PCR confirms influenza A (H3N2) subtypes. Secondary Staphylococcus aureus pneumonia is the most feared and well-documented bacterial superinfection complicating influenza, occurring due to influenza-mediated disruption of mucosal barriers and impaired phagocyte function. Markedly elevated ferritin reflects a hyperinflammatory state. The diagnosis is confirmed: influenza A (H3N2) pneumonia with secondary Staphylococcal superinfection, complicated by moderate ARDS.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 44-year-old woman who has never smoked is referred to a respiratory clinic with a 5-year history of progressively worsening breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive dyspnoea over 5 years in a young non-smoking woman is unusual and requires careful consideration. Emphysema in a non-smoker, asthma, IPF, pulmonary arterial hypertension, anaemia, and heart failure are all in the differential. The young age, non-smoking status, and 5-year progressive course are the discriminatory axes that make this presentation atypical and diagnostically rich.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has chronic daily cough but no wheeze and no sputum production. She becomes breathless at rest during exacerbations. She has had three hospital admissions in the past 2 years, each time with worsening dyspnoea but no identifiable precipitant.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent exacerbations with worsening dyspnoea without wheeze or sputum production, requiring hospitalisation in a young non-smoking woman, is an unusual pattern. Asthma remains possible as it can present without wheeze. PAH causes recurrent exertional dyspnoea in young women without respiratory infection. Emphysema in a non-smoker should raise suspicion for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency — a hereditary cause of panacinar emphysema that classically affects the lower lobes and presents at a younger age. Absence of wheeze makes classic asthma less likely.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has no known medical conditions and takes no medications. She has a brother, aged 40, who was recently diagnosed with COPD despite being a non-smoker. Her father died of liver cirrhosis at the age of 52.
prompts.reasoning: A sibling with non-smoking COPD and a father with liver cirrhosis at a young age are two critical family history clues for alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) deficiency. AAT deficiency is an autosomal codominant inherited condition in which the PiZZ genotype causes both early-onset panacinar emphysema (from uninhibited neutrophil elastase destroying alveolar walls) and liver disease (from PiZ polymer accumulation in hepatocytes). The familial clustering of young-onset COPD plus liver disease strongly points to a heritable enzyme deficiency causing emphysema rather than PAH or asthma.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 89% on room air, RR 22 breaths/min. Barrel-shaped chest. Bilateral hyper-resonance on percussion. Globally reduced breath sounds with prolonged expiration. No crackles, no wheeze. No digital clubbing. No signs of pulmonary hypertension (no loud P2, no RV heave).
prompts.reasoning: Barrel chest, bilateral hyper-resonance, globally reduced breath sounds, and prolonged expiration in a non-smoking 44-year-old woman confirms severe emphysema with air trapping. The absence of a loud P2 or RV heave makes pulmonary arterial hypertension as the primary diagnosis less likely (PAH would present with signs of right heart pressure overload). Normal findings on cardiac auscultation further reduce PAH probability. Asthma would typically produce wheeze and would not cause barrel chest from permanent air trapping at this age without smoking.

prompts.label: CT Thorax and Spirometry
prompts.text: CT thorax: severe bilateral panacinar emphysema with predominant lower-lobe involvement and significant air trapping. No honeycombing. No interstitial fibrosis. Spirometry: FEV1 31% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.41. Post-bronchodilator FEV1 improves by only 5% (no significant reversibility). DLCO: 28% predicted.
prompts.reasoning: Lower lobe-predominant panacinar emphysema on CT is characteristic of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, in contrast to centrilobular upper-lobe emphysema seen in smoking-related COPD. Severe irreversible airflow obstruction (FEV1/FVC 0.41, minimal bronchodilator response) and markedly reduced DLCO (reflecting loss of alveolar surface area) confirm severe emphysema with impaired gas transfer. This pattern of severe panacinar lower-lobe emphysema in a non-smoking woman with familial clustering demands immediate testing for AAT deficiency.

prompts.label: Serum Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Level and Genotyping
prompts.text: Serum alpha-1 antitrypsin level: 4 µmol/L (severely reduced; reference range 20–53 µmol/L). AAT phenotyping: PiZZ genotype confirmed. Liver function tests: mildly elevated ALT and AST, consistent with early hepatic involvement. Brother's serum AAT: also severely reduced.
prompts.reasoning: Severely reduced serum AAT at 4 µmol/L and homozygous PiZZ genotype confirms alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency — the cause of this patient's panacinar emphysema at age 44 without smoking. PiZZ produces the most severe AAT deficiency; the threshold protective AAT level is approximately 11 µmol/L. Mild hepatic transaminase elevation reflects hepatocyte polymer accumulation, consistent with the father's cirrhosis. The affected brother's result confirms autosomal codominant inheritance. The diagnosis is confirmed: severe emphysema due to homozygous alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (PiZZ).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 57-year-old man is referred to his GP with a persistent daily cough that has been present for the past 3 years.
prompts.reasoning: Persistent daily cough for 3 years in a middle-aged man requires systematic evaluation. Chronic bronchitis, bronchiectasis, TB, lung cancer, gastro-oesophageal reflux, and ACE inhibitor cough are all plausible at this stage. The chronic daily duration is the primary discriminatory axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The cough is productive of white or grey mucoid sputum, typically worst in the mornings. He has no haemoptysis, no weight loss, and no dyspnoea at rest. He has had two episodes in the past year where the sputum turned yellow-green and he felt more breathless, each requiring a course of antibiotics.
prompts.reasoning: A daily productive cough of mucoid sputum for over 2 consecutive years in two separate periods of at least 3 months satisfies the clinical definition of chronic bronchitis. Recurrent infective exacerbations requiring antibiotics are consistent with either chronic bronchitis or bronchiectasis. Absence of haemoptysis and weight loss lowers (but does not exclude) lung cancer probability. ACE inhibitor cough is dry, not productive. GORD cough is typically dry and nocturnal. Occupational and smoking history is critical next.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has smoked 20 cigarettes per day for 35 years (35 pack-years). He is a construction supervisor with some occupational dust exposure. He takes no regular medications and has no known allergies. He has no family history of lung disease.
prompts.reasoning: A 35 pack-year smoking history is the strongest risk factor for chronic bronchitis — cigarette smoke causes mucous gland hypertrophy, goblet cell metaplasia, and impaired mucociliary clearance, which is the pathological basis of chronic bronchitis. Heavy smoking also significantly elevates lung cancer risk. Bronchiectasis is less typically driven primarily by smoking. Without haemoptysis or systemic symptoms, lung cancer remains a background consideration requiring imaging to exclude rather than the leading diagnosis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 93% on room air, BMI 31 kg/m², HR 92 bpm. Mild bilateral expiratory wheeze. Coarse crackles bilaterally in the lower zones, clearing partially after coughing. No hyperinflation. No digital clubbing. Mild cyanosis of the lips noted. No ankle oedema.
prompts.reasoning: Crackles that partially clear with coughing are characteristic of secretion-related crackles from mucus pooling in the central airways — the hallmark of chronic bronchitis rather than parenchymal crackles (which do not clear with coughing). Mild cyanosis reflects V/Q mismatch from airway inflammation and mucous plugging causing hypoxaemia. Absence of barrel chest and hyperinflation distinguishes this from emphysema as the dominant process. Absence of clubbing makes lung cancer and bronchiectasis less likely. Lung cancer and endobronchial disease still require CXR exclusion.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray and Spirometry
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: increased bronchovascular markings bilaterally ('dirty chest'). No focal consolidation. No mass lesion. No cavitation. Spirometry: FEV1 62% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.65, post-bronchodilator FEV1 improvement 8% (not significantly reversible). No hyperinflation on CXR.
prompts.reasoning: Increased bronchovascular markings ('dirty chest') on CXR reflect bronchial wall thickening and peribronchial fibrosis from chronic airway inflammation in chronic bronchitis. Absence of a mass lesion or cavitation makes lung cancer and TB less likely — though CT would be needed to exclude early lung cancer definitively. Mild irreversible airflow obstruction (FEV1/FVC 0.65) reflects the chronic airway inflammation. The combination of daily productive cough for 3 years, smoking history, increased bronchovascular markings, and airflow obstruction confirms the diagnosis of chronic bronchitis.

prompts.label: Sputum Culture and Reid Index Assessment
prompts.text: Sputum culture during exacerbation: Haemophilus influenzae isolated. Arterial blood gas (resting): pH 7.38, PaCO2 48 mmHg, PaO2 61 mmHg — compensated type 2 respiratory failure. Complete blood count: haemoglobin 178 g/L (polycythaemia from chronic hypoxia).
prompts.reasoning: Haemophilus influenzae is the most common bacterial pathogen in chronic bronchitis exacerbations. Compensated type 2 respiratory failure (chronic CO2 retention with normal pH) and secondary polycythaemia from chronic hypoxia confirm the physiological consequences of long-standing V/Q mismatch and airway disease. These are the characteristic complications of chronic bronchitis — the 'blue bloater' phenotype. The complete picture — daily productive cough over 3 years, smoking, increased bronchovascular markings, mucus hypersecretion physiology, and chronic hypercapnia — confirms the diagnosis of chronic bronchitis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 65-year-old man is referred to a respiratory clinic with a 4-year history of increasing breathlessness and persistent cough.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive breathlessness and cough over 4 years in a middle-aged man requires broad consideration. COPD, IPF, CWP, silicosis, sarcoidosis, and heart failure are all plausible at this stage. Without occupational history, the differential is identical to other progressive dyspnoea presentations.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The cough is productive of black-tinged sputum on some days. He has no haemoptysis, no night sweats, and no weight loss. He reports breathlessness that worsens on exertion but is stable at rest.
prompts.reasoning: Black-tinged sputum (melanoptysis) is a specific finding in coal workers' pneumoconiosis — carbon-laden macrophages from the lungs are expectorated, producing dark sputum. This is not a feature of silicosis (which produces dusty grey sputum if any) or COPD. Melanoptysis narrows the differential considerably toward CWP, though occupational history is required to confirm. Night sweats and weight loss absence reduces TB probability. IPF and sarcoidosis would not produce melanoptysis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He worked as an underground coal miner for 28 years, retiring 10 years ago. He is a non-smoker. He has no known autoimmune disease and no exposure to crystalline silica-containing materials.
prompts.reasoning: Twenty-eight years of underground coal mining with retirement 10 years ago provides definitive occupational coal dust exposure. Coal mines may also contain some silica, so both CWP and mixed dust pneumoconiosis (including some silicosis component) are possible. However, primary coal dust exposure without specifically silica-containing operations is more consistent with CWP. Non-smoker status reduces COPD probability. The clinical triad of coal mining exposure, melanoptysis, and progressive dyspnoea is highly characteristic of CWP.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 91% on room air. Reduced breath sounds bilaterally in the upper and mid zones. Percussion mildly dull in the upper zones. Occasional crackles at the lung bases. No wheeze. No digital clubbing. No lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Upper zone signs (dullness and reduced breath sounds) with preserved lower zones are consistent with the upper-zone nodular distribution of CWP and silicosis. Absence of clubbing makes IPF less likely. No lymphadenopathy reduces sarcoidosis probability. The combination of upper-zone pulmonary signs in a retired coal miner with melanoptysis and progressive dyspnoea is characteristic of coal workers' pneumoconiosis rather than COPD (which produces diffuse hyperinflation) or IPF (which produces bibasal crackles). Chest imaging will confirm.

prompts.label: Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: multiple small rounded opacities (p/q type) predominantly in the upper and mid zones bilaterally, consistent with ILO Category 2/3 classification. A large confluent opacity (>1 cm) is visible in the right upper zone. No eggshell hilar calcification. No pleural plaques.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple small rounded upper-zone nodules (profusion category 2/3 on ILO classification) with a large confluent opacity represent simple CWP progressing to complicated CWP (progressive massive fibrosis, PMF). PMF is defined as a coalescence of nodules into a mass >1 cm, and represents an irreversible, progressive complication of CWP. Absence of eggshell hilar calcification argues against silicosis (where this is more characteristic). No pleural plaques exclude asbestosis. The imaging pattern combined with the coal mining history confirms CWP with early PMF.

prompts.label: Pulmonary Function Tests and HRCT
prompts.text: Spirometry: FVC 58% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.79 (mixed restrictive and obstructive pattern). DLCO: 48% predicted. HRCT thorax: bilateral upper-zone nodules with coalescence into a right upper lobe mass, consistent with progressive massive fibrosis. Coal-dust macules and nodules confirmed histologically on prior surgical biopsy from another institution. Occupational health exposure records confirm 28-year underground coal dust exposure above threshold limits.
prompts.reasoning: Mixed restrictive-obstructive spirometry and reduced DLCO reflect the combined airway and parenchymal involvement of CWP. HRCT confirms PMF — the major complication of CWP characterised by coalescence of coal dust macules and nodules into large fibrotic masses. Historical biopsy showing coal-dust macules and nodules (carbon-laden macrophages around respiratory bronchioles) provides histological confirmation. Occupational exposure records confirm the causative exposure. The diagnosis is confirmed: complicated coal workers' pneumoconiosis with progressive massive fibrosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 61-year-old woman is referred by her GP with a 6-week history of gradually worsening breathlessness.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive breathlessness over 6 weeks in an older woman has a broad differential. Pleural effusion (malignant or transudative), heart failure, TB, PE, and IPF are all plausible. The subacute 6-week progressive course without further characterisation is the only discriminatory axis at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has also had a persistent dry cough and has lost approximately 5 kg over the past 2 months without intentional dietary change. She has no pleuritic pain, no fever, and no ankle swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Unintentional weight loss of 5 kg over 2 months combined with progressive dyspnoea and dry cough points toward a malignant or mycobacterial cause of a pleural process. Transudative effusions (heart failure) would not cause this degree of weight loss. Absence of fever makes TB pleuritis less likely but does not exclude it. Malignant mesothelioma causes progressive breathlessness with weight loss, as does metastatic pleural disease. TB pleuritis can cause weight loss and dry cough. All three diagnoses remain plausible without a known primary malignancy established yet.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She completed treatment for breast cancer (hormone receptor positive, HER2 negative) 4 years ago and was declared disease-free at her last oncology review 18 months ago. She is a non-smoker and has no occupational exposures.
prompts.reasoning: A history of breast cancer is a critically important risk factor for late pleural metastases — breast cancer is the most common cause of malignant pleural effusion in women, with late recurrence well-documented in hormone receptor-positive disease. This history makes malignant pleural disease the leading diagnosis. Mesothelioma is now less likely given the absence of occupational asbestos exposure. TB remains in the differential as an exudative pleural effusion cause, particularly given the weight loss and dry cough. Imaging and pleural fluid analysis will be decisive.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 93% on room air, RR 22 breaths/min. Trachea deviated slightly to the left. Stony dullness to percussion in the right hemithorax (lower two-thirds). Absent breath sounds in the same area. Reduced vocal resonance. No peripheral oedema. No axillary lymphadenopathy palpable.
prompts.reasoning: Stony dullness with absent breath sounds and reduced vocal resonance confirm a large right pleural effusion. Contralateral tracheal deviation indicates significant volume. The physical findings confirm pleural effusion but do not distinguish malignant from TB-related exudate at this stage. Both remain plausible — a woman with treated breast cancer and new pleural effusion has a high pre-test probability of malignant effusion, but TB should not be excluded without microbiological and cytological evidence. Imaging and diagnostic thoracocentesis are required.

prompts.label: CT Thorax and Abdomen
prompts.text: CT thorax with contrast: large right pleural effusion with multiple pleural nodules and irregular pleural thickening. No primary lung mass. Multiple small pulmonary nodules bilaterally. CT abdomen: two hepatic lesions (segments IV and VI) with rim enhancement consistent with metastases.
prompts.reasoning: Pleural nodularity and irregular thickening with concurrent pulmonary nodules and hepatic lesions in a woman with prior breast cancer constitutes a classical pattern of systemic metastatic disease with malignant pleural involvement. TB pleuritis does not produce pleural nodules or hepatic lesions. The CT findings establish metastatic disease as the cause of the pleural effusion, with breast cancer as the most likely primary given the history. Pleural fluid analysis and biopsy are required to confirm cytological diagnosis and receptor status.

prompts.label: Pleural Fluid Analysis and Biopsy
prompts.text: Thoracocentesis: straw-coloured fluid. Pleural fluid protein: 52 g/L, LDH: 380 U/L (exudate by Light's criteria). Pleural fluid cytology: malignant epithelial cells, ER positive, PR positive, HER2 negative — consistent with metastatic breast carcinoma. Serum CA 15-3: markedly elevated at 340 U/mL.
prompts.reasoning: Exudative pleural fluid (by Light's criteria: protein >30 g/L or fluid-to-serum LDH >0.6) with malignant epithelial cells matching the prior breast cancer receptor profile (ER+/PR+/HER2-) confirms metastatic breast carcinoma as the cause of the pleural effusion. Elevated CA 15-3 provides a serological marker of disease burden. The diagnosis is confirmed: malignant pleural effusion from metastatic hormone receptor-positive breast carcinoma, representing late distant recurrence 4 years after primary treatment.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 26-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of sharp right-sided chest pain.
prompts.reasoning: Acute unilateral sharp chest pain over 2 days in a young woman has multiple causes. Pleuritis, PE, pneumothorax, pneumonia, musculoskeletal pain, and pericarditis are all plausible. The 2-day acute course and unilateral sharp character are the discriminatory axes at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The pain worsens sharply with deep breathing and coughing and is constant at rest. She also has a mild dry cough and low-grade fever for the past 3 days. She has no haemoptysis and no leg swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Pleuritic chest pain (worsening with breathing and coughing) with a dry cough and low-grade fever is a presentation that fits pleuritis, pneumonia with pleuritis, and PE. PE can produce pleuritic pain from pulmonary infarction. The absence of haemoptysis, leg swelling, or prothrombotic risk factors lowers PE probability. The fever and preceding viral-like illness are more consistent with infective pleuritis or viral pleuropneumonia. Musculoskeletal pain is less likely with fever.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She had an upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks ago that resolved. She takes the oral contraceptive pill but has no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism. She has no autoimmune conditions and is a non-smoker.
prompts.reasoning: A preceding URTI 2 weeks ago with onset of pleuritic pain is consistent with viral or post-viral pleuritis — viral pleural inflammation can follow respiratory viral infection by 1–2 weeks. OCP use is a prothrombotic risk factor that keeps PE in the differential despite no DVT symptoms. Absence of autoimmune history makes lupus pleuritis less likely but it cannot be excluded in a young woman without serological testing. Pneumonia with pleuritis is possible if consolidation is found on examination or imaging.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.7°C, HR 90 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 18 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air. A pleural friction rub audible in the right lower zone on both inspiration and expiration. No dullness to percussion. Normal breath sounds. No leg swelling.
prompts.reasoning: A pleural friction rub — a leathery, creaking sound heard on both inspiration and expiration due to inflamed visceral and parietal pleura rubbing against each other — is the cardinal physical sign of pleuritis. Normal percussion and breath sounds exclude significant consolidation or effusion. Normal SpO2 and heart rate at rest with no leg swelling make PE less likely, though not excluded. The friction rub localising to the right lower zone with normal underlying lung signs confirms localised pleural inflammation as the primary process.

prompts.label: CXR and D-Dimer
prompts.text: Chest X-ray: normal lung fields, no consolidation, no pleural effusion. A small area of pleural thickening in the right lower zone. D-dimer: 0.4 µg/mL (normal, <0.5 µg/mL). ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no S1Q3T3 pattern.
prompts.reasoning: Normal CXR without consolidation, effusion, or Hampton's hump confirms uncomplicated pleuritis without pneumonia or infarction. A negative D-dimer in a patient with low-to-moderate pre-test probability for PE effectively excludes clinically significant PE (sensitivity ~95%). Normal ECG without right heart strain patterns further reduces PE probability. The diagnosis is confirmed as isolated pleuritis — likely viral or post-infectious in aetiology given the preceding URTI. Analgesics and NSAIDs are appropriate management.

prompts.label: Autoimmune Serology
prompts.text: ANA: weakly positive (1:80, speckled pattern). Anti-dsDNA: negative. Anti-Sm: negative. Complement C3 and C4: normal. ESR: 42 mm/hr (mildly elevated). CRP: 28 mg/L. Viral respiratory PCR panel (nasopharyngeal): Coxsackie B virus detected.
prompts.reasoning: Positive ANA at low titres (1:80) is non-specific and found in 5–10% of the normal population; negative anti-dsDNA and anti-Sm with normal complement levels effectively exclude SLE as the aetiology at this stage. Detection of Coxsackie B virus on respiratory PCR confirms viral pleuritis (Coxsackie B is a well-established cause of pleuritis and pleurodynia — Bornholm disease). Mildly elevated inflammatory markers reflect the acute viral inflammatory response. The diagnosis is confirmed: acute viral pleuritis due to Coxsackie B virus, following a preceding respiratory tract infection.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 33-year-old woman is referred by her cardiologist with an 18-month history of progressive breathlessness on exertion.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive exertional breathlessness over 18 months in a young woman requires broad evaluation. PAH, heart failure, chronic PE, IPF, anaemia, and asthma are all plausible at this stage. Young women with unexplained progressive dyspnoea must be assessed for PAH, which disproportionately affects this demographic.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She reports near-syncope on exertion twice in the past 3 months and has had palpitations intermittently. She has no cough, no wheeze, and no orthopnoea. She has not had any episodes of leg swelling or prolonged immobility.
prompts.reasoning: Exertional pre-syncope in a young woman with progressive dyspnoea is a concerning symptom of inadequate cardiac output during effort — characteristic of fixed pulmonary vascular obstruction or severe right ventricular outflow limitation, as seen in PAH and chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH). Absence of cough, wheeze, and orthopnoea makes asthma and left heart failure less likely. Absent leg swelling or immobility lowers (but does not exclude) CTEPH. Palpitations suggest right heart strain or arrhythmia.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with hydroxychloroquine. She takes no other medications. She is a non-smoker. Her maternal aunt also has SLE.
prompts.reasoning: SLE is one of the most important connective tissue diseases associated with PAH (connective tissue disease-associated PAH, CTD-PAH). SLE causes PAH through immune-mediated endothelial injury, vasoconstriction, and vascular remodelling. SLE also increases the risk of antiphospholipid syndrome and thrombosis, making CTEPH from recurrent PEs another important possibility. Both diagnoses need active investigation. IPF and heart failure are less likely in a 33-year-old with connective tissue disease.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: HR 96 bpm, BP 104/68 mmHg, SpO2 91% on room air. Loud P2 component on auscultation. Right ventricular heave palpable at the left sternal edge. Raised JVP with prominent 'a' waves. Mild peripheral oedema bilaterally. Chest clear. No skin rash or active synovitis.
prompts.reasoning: Loud P2 (reflecting increased pulmonary artery closure pressure), right ventricular heave (RV pressure overload and hypertrophy), elevated JVP with prominent a waves (reduced RV compliance requiring high RA pressure), and peripheral oedema (right heart failure) constitute the clinical syndrome of pulmonary hypertension with right heart failure. These findings confirm significant pulmonary hypertension. The clear chest makes pulmonary fibrosis or pulmonary oedema less likely. This constellation in a young woman with SLE is most consistent with CTD-associated PAH requiring formal haemodynamic assessment.

prompts.label: Echocardiogram and CT Pulmonary Angiography
prompts.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram: estimated right ventricular systolic pressure (RVSP) 72 mmHg (severely elevated, normal <35 mmHg). Dilated right ventricle with septal flattening. LVEF preserved at 62%. CTPA: no filling defects in the pulmonary arteries. Enlarged central pulmonary arteries with peripheral pruning.
prompts.reasoning: RVSP of 72 mmHg on echo confirms severe pulmonary hypertension. Preserved LVEF with RV dilatation and septal flattening confirms a pre-capillary (pulmonary arterial) rather than post-capillary (left heart) mechanism. CTPA showing no filling defects definitively excludes CTEPH as the cause of pulmonary hypertension. Enlarged central pulmonary arteries with 'pruning' of peripheral vessels is the classic vascular CT sign of PAH. Right heart catheterisation is required for definitive diagnosis and haemodynamic characterisation.

prompts.label: Right Heart Catheterisation and Autoimmune Panel
prompts.text: Right heart catheterisation: mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) 58 mmHg (severely elevated), pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) 12 Wood units, pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP) 8 mmHg (pre-capillary pattern). Anti-U1 RNP antibodies: strongly positive. Diagnosis: SLE-associated pulmonary arterial hypertension, WHO Group 1, functional class III.
prompts.reasoning: Right heart catheterisation is the gold standard for PAH diagnosis: mPAP ≥25 mmHg at rest with PAWP ≤15 mmHg (pre-capillary pattern) and elevated PVR (>3 Wood units) confirms pulmonary arterial hypertension. A PAWP of 8 mmHg excludes left heart (post-capillary) disease. PVR of 12 Wood units indicates severely elevated pulmonary vascular resistance. Strongly positive anti-U1 RNP in the context of SLE confirms connective tissue disease-associated PAH (WHO Group 1). The diagnosis is confirmed: CTD-PAH in a patient with SLE, functional class III, requiring initiation of targeted PAH therapy.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 69-year-old man with known COPD presents with a 3-week history of worsening breathlessness and bilateral ankle swelling.
prompts.reasoning: Worsening dyspnoea with new bilateral ankle swelling in a patient with known COPD raises the possibility of cor pulmonale (right heart failure secondary to chronic lung disease), acute COPD exacerbation, left heart failure, PE, or systemic causes of oedema. The combination of known pulmonary disease with new peripheral oedema is the discriminatory axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has been more breathless than usual for 3 weeks and his ankles have progressively swollen. He does not have orthopnoea and can still lie flat at night. He has no pleuritic pain and no haemoptysis. He has had a persistent productive cough for years but this has not changed.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of orthopnoea and ability to lie flat make left ventricular failure (which causes pulmonary congestion and orthopnoea) less likely. Ankle oedema without orthopnoea in the context of chronic lung disease suggests right heart failure secondary to pulmonary hypertension from COPD — cor pulmonale. COPD exacerbation can worsen dyspnoea but would not typically cause progressive bilateral ankle oedema without decompensated right heart failure. Stable baseline cough argues against a new acute infective precipitant.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has had COPD for 10 years (FEV1 last measured at 38% predicted 6 months ago). He smoked 40 cigarettes per day for 30 years and quit 8 years ago. He has no prior history of heart failure, valvular disease, or coronary artery disease.
prompts.reasoning: Severe COPD (FEV1 38%) with a 30-year smoking history provides the chronic lung disease substrate for pulmonary hypertension and secondary right ventricular hypertrophy — the pathological basis of cor pulmonale. Cor pulmonale develops progressively as chronic hypoxia causes pulmonary arterial vasoconstriction, vascular remodelling, and ultimately right ventricular pressure overload. Absence of coronary artery disease, valvular disease, or prior LV failure makes primary congestive cardiac failure less likely but echocardiographic evaluation is essential to differentiate.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 84% on room air, HR 104 bpm, BP 118/78 mmHg, RR 24 breaths/min. JVP elevated at 5 cm above the sternal angle. Loud P2 heart sound. Right parasternal heave. Bilateral pitting oedema to the mid-calves. Bilateral reduced breath sounds with a prolonged expiratory phase (consistent with COPD). No crackles suggesting pulmonary oedema. No ascites.
prompts.reasoning: The triad of elevated JVP, loud P2 (pulmonary hypertension), and right parasternal heave (RV hypertrophy) constitutes the clinical hallmarks of cor pulmonale in a COPD patient. Bilateral pitting oedema reflects right heart failure and fluid retention. Absence of crackles or orthopnoea distinguishes this from left heart failure. The COPD examination findings confirm the underlying lung disease. In a patient with severe COPD, SpO2 84% represents chronic hypoxia driving pulmonary vasoconstriction and eventual cor pulmonale.

prompts.label: Arterial Blood Gas and Chest X-Ray
prompts.text: ABG on room air: pH 7.35, PaCO2 58 mmHg, PaO2 49 mmHg, HCO3 32 mmol/L (compensated type 2 respiratory failure with chronic CO2 retention). Chest X-ray: bilateral hyperinflation and flattened diaphragms (COPD pattern). Enlarged central pulmonary arteries. Prominent right heart border. No pulmonary oedema or pleural effusions.
prompts.reasoning: Compensated type 2 respiratory failure (chronic CO2 retention, elevated HCO3 indicating metabolic compensation) with severe hypoxia (PaO2 49 mmHg) confirms the degree of COPD severity driving pulmonary vascular disease. CXR showing enlarged central pulmonary arteries and a prominent right heart border confirms pulmonary hypertension and right heart enlargement consistent with cor pulmonale. Absence of bat-wing oedema or Kerley B lines on CXR excludes left heart failure. The combination confirms cor pulmonale secondary to COPD.

prompts.label: Echocardiogram and BNP
prompts.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram: RV dilatation and hypertrophy, estimated RVSP 62 mmHg, interventricular septal flattening (D-sign), preserved LV systolic function (LVEF 62%), no valvular disease. BNP: 680 pg/mL (elevated, indicating right heart strain). Polycythaemia on FBC: haemoglobin 192 g/L.
prompts.reasoning: RVSP of 62 mmHg confirms significant pulmonary hypertension. RV dilatation and hypertrophy with septal flattening (D-sign) indicate chronic pressure overload — the echocardiographic signature of cor pulmonale. Preserved LV function definitively excludes left heart failure as the cause of oedema and pulmonary hypertension. Elevated BNP reflects RV wall stress. Polycythaemia (Hgb 192 g/L) is a compensatory response to chronic hypoxaemia. The diagnosis is confirmed: cor pulmonale secondary to severe COPD-related pulmonary hypertension, requiring long-term oxygen therapy.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 11-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric respiratory clinic with a lifelong history of recurrent respiratory infections and chronic nasal symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: Lifelong recurrent respiratory infections and chronic nasal symptoms from childhood in a boy requires consideration of congenital and hereditary conditions. PCD, cystic fibrosis, immunodeficiency, allergic rhinitis with recurrent infections, asthma, and GORD are all plausible at this stage. The lifelong pattern from infancy is the key discriminatory axis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has had daily productive cough producing green sputum since infancy, persistent year-round nasal discharge, and five episodes of acute sinusitis treated with antibiotics in the past 2 years. His parents also report that as a newborn he had unexplained breathing difficulties requiring brief oxygen supplementation.
prompts.reasoning: Daily productive cough from infancy, chronic year-round rhinorrhoea (not seasonal, which would suggest allergy), and recurrent sinusitis collectively describe pan-sinopulmonary mucociliary dysfunction. Neonatal respiratory distress in a term infant is a classic early manifestation of PCD — neonatal ciliary dysfunction causes failure to clear lung fluid and transition from liquid-breathing physiology after birth. CF causes the same pattern but also affects the GI tract. Immunodeficiency produces recurrent infections but not typically the constant mucociliary pattern. GI symptoms will differentiate CF.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: He has normal growth and weight. He has had no diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, or abdominal symptoms. He has a younger sister who was also noted to have neonatal respiratory distress and now has frequent ear infections. There is no consanguinity.
prompts.reasoning: Normal growth and complete absence of GI symptoms (no diarrhoea, steatorrhoea, or malabsorption) effectively argues against cystic fibrosis, which almost always causes some degree of exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and GI manifestations. Familial clustering in a sibling with neonatal respiratory distress and recurrent otitis media (another cilia-dependent mucosal site) is strongly consistent with autosomal recessive PCD. CF could be familial but the absence of GI involvement in both children makes PCD the leading diagnosis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: SpO2 95% on room air. Digital clubbing grade 1 (mild). Bilateral coarse crackles in the lower zones. Nasal mucosa chronically inflamed with turbinate hypertrophy. Ear: bilateral tympanic membrane retraction consistent with chronic otitis media with effusion. Heart auscultation: apex beat palpable on the right side.
prompts.reasoning: Right-sided cardiac apex (dextrocardia), combined with bronchiectatic crackles, recurrent sinusitis, and chronic otitis media, constitutes Kartagener syndrome — a subtype of PCD comprising the triad of bronchiectasis, sinusitis, and situs inversus (50% of PCD patients have situs inversus). Dextrocardia in this clinical context is essentially pathognomonic of Kartagener syndrome as a variant of PCD. Cystic fibrosis does not cause situs inversus. The diagnosis is now highly probable pending ciliary ultrastructural confirmation.

prompts.label: HRCT Chest and Sweat Chloride
prompts.text: HRCT thorax: bilateral lower-lobe bronchiectasis with mucus plugging and peribronchial thickening. Abdominal situs inversus confirmed. Sweat chloride test: 14 mmol/L (normal, excludes cystic fibrosis).
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral lower-zone bronchiectasis (in contrast to the upper-zone bronchiectasis of CF) combined with confirmed situs inversus constitutes Kartagener syndrome — the classic triad of bronchiectasis, sinusitis, and situs inversus. Normal sweat chloride definitively excludes cystic fibrosis. Lower-lobe predominant bronchiectasis distinguishes PCD from CF. Situs inversus results from failure of normal left-right asymmetry determination during embryogenesis, which is dependent on normal ciliary rotation of the embryonic node. Ciliary function testing will provide definitive molecular confirmation.

prompts.label: Nasal Nitric Oxide and Electron Microscopy
prompts.text: Nasal nitric oxide measurement: 18 nL/min (severely reduced; normal >77 nL/min). Transmission electron microscopy of nasal brush biopsy cilia: absent dynein arms on the majority of cilia (outer dynein arm defect). Genetic testing: biallelic mutations in DNAI1 gene confirmed. Diagnosis: Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia with Kartagener syndrome.
prompts.reasoning: Severely reduced nasal nitric oxide is a highly sensitive and specific screening test for PCD — ciliary dysfunction impairs nitric oxide production by nasal epithelium. Transmission electron microscopy demonstrating absent outer dynein arms confirms the ultrastructural defect — dynein arms are the motor proteins responsible for ciliary motility, and their absence results in complete ciliary immotility. Biallelic DNAI1 mutations confirm the autosomal recessive genetic basis. The diagnosis is confirmed: Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia with Kartagener syndrome (situs inversus, bronchiectasis, sinusitis) due to DNAI1 outer dynein arm defect.
